Headline: Could constructing public residential aged care in Tasmania help solve hospital bed block?
Full Text:
Last week a new funding agreement was struck between the Tasmanian and federal government for hospital funding.

    But Chris Grice from National Seniors Australia says aged care remains a big issue, with many elderly people unable to be discharged from hospital because they don't have a place in aged care to go to.

    

    "It doesn't translate for those folk who are stranded in hospital, those people who should be in residential aged care," he tells Lucy Breaden on Hobart Drive.

    

    Mr Grice says there's merit in the state government teaming up with the private sector to construct public aged care homes.

    

    Lucy Breaden, Presenter

    

    Grier Brindley, Producer

    

    Image Details

    

    National Seniors Australia say the shortage of places in residential aged care is concerning. Supplied: Rosie O'Beirne

Headline: Queensland disability client Bridie Gilligan choked to death on Hungry Jacks burger, coroner finds
Full Text:
Coronial findings revealed the woman lost consciousness and later died after shoving the fast food chain's "Yumbo" burger into her mouth at her Cairns home. 

    A Queensland disability client choked to death on a Hungry Jacks burger after snatching it from her support worker, a coroner has found.

    

    Cairns woman Bridie Gilligan had a history of choking and was on a restricted diet when she choked while eating a Hungry Jacks "Yumbo" burger at her Edmonton home on April 29, 2021.

    

    Coronial findings published on Tuesday by Deputy State Coroner Stephanie Gallagher revealed how Ms Gilligan, 42, lost consciousness and later died after shoving the burger into her mouth.

    

    The inquest heard Ms Gilligan's support worker Margaret Lowry had purchased a burger for each of them and had intended to cut Ms Gilligan's into small pieces - but Ms Gilligan quickly snatched it and ran outside first.

    

    "The support worker called an ambulance and Ms Gilligan was taken to hospital but later died," Ms Gallagher said.

    

    The inquest heard Ms Gilligan had suffered a catastrophic brain injury and was pronounced brain dead on May 3, 2021.

    

    Her family have since expressed concerns about whether it had been appropriate to give her the burger given her disability and dietary needs.

    

    Ms Gilligan had been living in accommodation provided by Endeavour Foundation at the time of her death, as she required support for her intellectual disability associated with Cornelia De Lange Syndrome.

    

    She had been largely non-verbal and "required assistance with all her activities of daily living", Ms Gallagher noted.

    

    Ms Gilligan's mother told police her daughter was supposed to be given soft-textured foods and also be "completely supervised and assisted with eating, as if she was left alone with food then it is likely a choking incident would occur".

    

    Ms Gilligan's death was investigated by the Office of the Work Health and Safety Prosecutor, who concluded they were "not satisfied there was sufficient evidence to commence a prosecution against either the Endeavour Foundation or its worker, Ms Lowry".

    

    Ms Gallagher said in her findings on Tuesday that she accepted Ms Gilligan's death was preventable; however, she found Ms Lowry's supervision and Endeavour's systems regarding diet were both appropriate.

    

    "The concerns expressed by Ms Gilligan's family must be balanced against the fact that Ms Gilligan was a challenging client who did not cope well with new support workers," Ms Gallagher said.

    

    Support workers had given varying views to the inquest about whether giving fast food to a client was appropriate - with one suggesting it may actually support their independence.

    

    However, the worker noted that the decision should still be communicated with and resolved with the appropriate decision-maker - which would have been Ms Gilligan's mother in this case.

    

    A dietitian had given evidence saying she would not consider a burger or bun appropriate for Ms Gilligan's specific meal plan.

    

    Ms Gallagher ultimately agreed that the burger, even if cut up into small pieces, would not have complied with the standards set out for Ms Gilligan's diet - but she pointed out that Ms Gilligan's meal plan had contained other foods that also didn't comply with the standards.

    

    She said this had been due to competing considerations surrounding Ms Gilligan's quality of life, and the foods included had been ones Ms Gilligan liked or ones that gave her some level of autonomy or independence.

    

    Ms Gallagher considered it was not unreasonable for Ms Lowry to have thought a burger was suitable under the circumstances, given she had not been given specific training on the requirements of Ms Gilligan's "soft and bite-sized" diet, and the meal plan had included sandwiches.

    

    "Ms Lowry's evidence, which was not challenged, was that she had given Ms Gilligan cheeseburgers before, and Ms Gilligan had been able to eat them when they had been cut up into small pieces," Ms Gallagher noted.

    

    The inquest heard Endeavour had multiple policies and procedures in place to ensure workers had adequate information and training to care for a person with eating and swallowing difficulties.

    

    Endeavour staff had emphasised during the inquest that the support worker's goal is to support their clients to live as independently as possible.

    

    They acknowledged that the introduction of the NDIS had created a separation between Supported Independent Living providers like Endeavour and their client's other health services, however.

    

    In Ms Gilligan's case, the court heard this resulted in delayed follow ups with her other health services, a lack of collaboration on meal plans, and no guidance or training being provided to Ms Gilligan's support workers about her specific diet requirements.

    

    Ms Gallagher found Endeavour had not received sufficient information from Ms Gilligan's health care providers to identify the "real risk" bread products posed to her, and acknowledged Ms Gilligan's risk of choking was not appropriately managed by her overall system of care.

    

    "Endeavour has made sensible and appropriate changes to its systems and processes since Ms Gilligan's death to ensure that a similar tragic outcome does not occur in the future," Ms Gallagher said.

    

    "... I do not accept, on the evidence before me, that the responsibility for the circumstances which led to Ms Gilligan's death lies with Endeavour Foundation or their employees and agents."

    

    She found four recommendations suggested by Endeavour to be practical - including that the NDIS or NDIA review the processes for Supported Independent Living providers to access information about allied health supports, and consider appropriate funding in NDIA plans to ensure training of Supported Independent Living staff.

Headline: Calls for mandatory fire sprinklers in Specialist Disability Accommodation
Full Text:
Brisbane resident Todd Winther, who has cerebral palsy, is terrified of what could happen if a fire broke out in his home.

    "I can't get out of bed independently on my own," the 42-year-old, who uses a wheelchair, said.

    

    He said that even if he was in his wheelchair, it would still take time to get out of his apartment.

    

    People with disability are at higher risk from home fires and are over-represented in fire fatalities, a Bushfire Cooperative Research Centre report from 2019 said.

    

    It found that, nationally, 62 per cent of people who died in preventable residential fires in between 2003 and 2017 had a disability.

    

    Mr Winther lives in Specialist Disability Accommodation (SDA) through the National Disability Insurance Scheme (NDIS).

    

    His house has been fitted with water sprinklers.

    

    "Sprinklers provide another barrier [for] safety," Mr Winther said.

    

    "[I] can't handle things like fire extinguishers independently."

    

    But he said home fire sprinklers were not mandatory in this type of accommodation.

    

    "The thought of not having every safety precaution in place is terrifying," Mr Winther said.

    

   'Participants are fearful'

     Specialist Disability Accommodation Alliance's CEO, Jeramy Hope, said it was a sentiment expressed by other SDA residents.

    

    "I know participants are fearful at night," he said.

    

    More than 15,500 NDIS participants live in SDAs that are designed to help them live more independently, according to the NDIS's Quarterly Report released in September.

    

    In SDAs, sprinklers are only recommended, with smoke alarms required in bedroom and living spaces.

    

    "The safety of people with disabilities in their living environments isn't negotiable," Mr Hope said.

    

    The alliance has called for home fire sprinklers and interconnected smoke alarms to be mandated for all SDA properties.

    

    "It's not even new technology," he said.

    

    He said the prices for the system ranged between $5,000 to $20,000 to fit into the homes.

    

    Mr Hope said it was a "fairly inexpensive implementation" that could prevent people with a disability dying in their homes.

    

    "That's life changing," he said.

    

    A spokesperson for the National Disability Insurance Agency (NDIA), which implements the NDIS, said the SDA design standards were currently under review.

    

    The spokesperson said the current technical and design requirements were being reviewed to strengthen safety, including the recommendation of fire sprinklers in SDA dwellings.

    

    The outcome of the review is set to be available later this year.

    

    "SDA rules require SDA providers to meet conditions, including that the dwelling is in a good state of repair and appropriately maintained," the spokesperson said.

    

    They said the NDIA also funds an "additional allowance for fire sprinklers installed in SDA".

    

   'Ninety per cent improvement in survival'

     Home Fire Sprinkler Coalition Australia's Chair Mark Whybro said the technology had been installed in aged care homes with great success across the country.

    

    "People are safer," the former assistant commissioner of Fire and Rescue New South Wales said.

    

    Mr Whybro became involved in advocating for sprinklers in aged care homes after the 2011 Quakers Hill nursing home fire in Sydney's west that killed 11 elderly residents.

    

    "We were successful in getting the retrofitting of sprinklers in residential aged care in New South Wales," he said.

    

    It then became mandatory across Australia under the National Construction Code.

    

    Mr Whybro wants the same changes to be made to the SDA sector.

    

    "We've got proven, tried, tested technology that can reduce the risk for those people who we know are most vulnerable in our community," he said.

    

    Mr Whybro said smoke alarms alone were not enough as they only provided a 50 per cent improvement in surviving a typical house fire and did nothing to suppress the blaze.

    

    "Home fire sprinklers will give you around a 90 per cent improvement in surviving a house fire," Mr Whybro said.

    

    "The quicker you get water on fire, the better it is for everyone."

    

    He said there had been a rise in fires involving plastics, synthetics, and polystyrene foam, which could set an entire room alight in two to three minutes.

    

    "Fire sprinklers manage the fire to allow you to get out," Mr Whybro said.

    

   An important part of the NDIS

     Disability accommodation has changed Mr Winther's life.

    

    He said if he had not moved in, he would not have been able to work full-time and would not have met his now-wife.

    

    "I'd still be living with my parents," the NDIS consultant said.

    

    But Mr Winther said he would like to see the accommodation made safer for all people with a disability.

    

    "SDAs [are] one of the most important parts of the NDIS," he said.

Headline: Like a fancy pack of Tim Tams, aged care now gives less for more
Full Text:
February 4, 2026 — 5:01am  Did you know that when you buy a packet of Tim Tams, depending on the flavour, you get 11 biscuits or only nine?

   The packets look the same but inside there's less.

    

    As the government's new Support at Home aged care program rolls out, many older Australians are discovering their care is starting to feel uncomfortably similar.

    

    On September 12, 2024, the government pledged a "no worse off" principle for people approved for or receiving a Home Care Package. It guarantees they won't contribute more under Support at Home than they previously did. And technically, that commitment is being honoured: their fees remain the same.

    

    While their contribution hasn't changed, the cost of care inside their package has. People are receiving less care for the same price; the hours have shrunk. The package looks the same on the outside, but inside there's less. It's shrinkflation — the aged care edition.

    

    A major part of the shift comes from the new funding and pricing structure. The government has capped management fees at 10 per cent, and only on the services you actually use, not across your whole package. Under the old Home Care Package system, management and admin fees could swallow up to 35 per cent of your funding.

    

    On paper, that's great news. It makes comparing providers clearer, with travel, wages, admin and profit all built into the hourly price. But here's the catch: the new bundled price of services is more expensive than the old one. Even people who are self-managing their home care services are reporting a 10 per cent jump in price.

    

    Money contributor

    

    Some of the bundled prices we're seeing include $155 per hour for personal assistance, nursing care for $180 per hour and allied health services for $230 per hour. Add 10 per cent for management fees, and it's easy to see why people are reporting that a package that was providing 17 hours a week is now only delivering 13.

    

    Replacing those missing four hours a week can cost anywhere between $240 and $620 — and that's assuming care is provided during standard business hours. Evening, weekend or public holiday services attract even higher rates.

    

    For those who don't have the protection of the no-worse-off principle, the reality is even tougher. They can pay up to 50 per cent of the cost of their independence services, such as help with showering and dressing, and 80 per cent of their everyday living services, such as cleaning.

    

    With hourly prices this high, many part-pensioners and self-funded retirees need to look closely at alternatives. If cleaning is $100 an hour and your contribution is $80, the arithmetic becomes obvious.

    

    Support at Home promised simplicity, transparency and fairness. But as the system settles in, many older Australians are finding that while the packaging is new, what's inside feels smaller. And when it comes to care, unlike Tim Tams, having less is not good for your health.

    

    Rachel Lane is the author of Downsizing Made Simple , a book and website aimed at demystifying downsizing.

Headline: Like a fancy pack of Tim Tams, aged care now gives less for more
Full Text:
February 4, 2026 — 2:01am  Did you know that when you buy a packet of Tim Tams, depending on the flavour, you get 11 biscuits or only nine?

   The packets look the same but inside there's less.

    

    As the government's new Support at Home aged care program rolls out, many older Australians are discovering their care is starting to feel uncomfortably similar.

    

    On September 12, 2024, the a "no worse off" principle for people approved for or receiving a Home Care Package. It guarantees they won't contribute more under Support at Home than they previously did. And technically, that commitment is being honoured: their fees remain the same.

    

    While their contribution hasn't changed, the cost of care inside their package has. People are receiving less care for the same price; the hours have shrunk. The package looks the same on the outside, but inside there's less. It's shrinkflation — the aged care edition.

    

    A major part of the shift comes from the new funding and pricing structure. The government has capped management fees at 10 per cent, and only on the services you actually use, not across your whole package. Under the old Home Care Package system, management and admin fees could swallow up to 35 per cent of your funding.

    

    On paper, that's great news. It makes comparing providers clearer, with travel, wages, admin and profit all built into the hourly price. But here's the catch: the new bundled price of services is more expensive than the old one. Even people who are self-managing their home care services are reporting a 10 per cent jump in price.

    

    Some of the bundled prices we're seeing include $155 per hour for personal assistance, nursing care for $180 per hour and allied health services for $230 per hour. Add 10 per cent for management fees, and it's easy to see why people are reporting that a package that was providing 17 hours a week is now only delivering 13.

    

    Replacing those missing four hours a week can cost anywhere between $240 and $620 — and that's assuming care is provided during standard business hours. Evening, weekend or public holiday services attract even higher rates.

Headline: Bain Capital to go back to the future with Estia Health?
Full Text:
The US equity investor has reportedly recruited three investment banks to relist residential aged care operator Estia Health on the Australian Securities Exchange (ASX).

    Bain Capital has hired UBS, Barrenjoey Capital Partners and Morgan Stanley to lead an initial public offering that would give the company a market capitalisation of more than $2 billion, according to The Australian Financial Review.

    

    Bain Capital and Estia Health Chief Executive Officer Sean Bilton and Chief Financial Officer Anthony Rice met fund managers in late 2025 as part of a Sydney and Melbourne roadshow. At the time, Bain was weighing up an IPO or trade sale, advised by Barrenjoey and Gresham.

    

    Estia was listed on the ASX until 2023, when Bain acquired it for $838 million, valuing the company at $280,000 per bed . At the time, we said the sale represented 'rolled gold' for Bain. The cost to build a new aged care bed today currently sits at around $500,000.

    

    Since 2023, Estia has grown significantly – from 75 homes in 2023 to 94 homes today. After securing $300 million in mezzanine finance from Nomura Securities, the company has acquired six Queensland homes from Vacenti Aged Care , two Sydney homes from Mark and Evette Moran , seven NSW and Victorian homes from Aurrum Aged Care , and three Queensland homes from Calvary Health Care . The company has also opened new greenfield developments and has a pipeline underway.

    

    The aged care environment is also markedly different from when Bain acquired the operator in the shadow of COVID-19 and the Royal Commission into Aged Care Quality and Safety.

    

    Today, there is improved regulatory stability for residential aged care under the new Aged Care Act, increased funding options available to operators, and the long-term demographic tailwinds of an ageing population.

    

    In December, The Australian reported the ASX-listed Regis Healthcare – which operates 72 aged care homes – had been approached about a merger with Estia.

Headline: Revenue Partner: Transforming aged care funding from reactive to real-time
Full Text:
Revenue Partner is a service transformation that replaces fragmented funding processes with an integrated model combining automation, resident-level insights and embedded expertise.

   It was awarded Highly Commended in the Service Transformation category at the 2025 Future of Ageing Awards. Results were unveiled at a presentation dinner in Sydney on November 27, 2025… To view all of the results…

    

    Aged care finance and billing teams operate in one of the most complex administrative environments in the sector, navigating fragmented systems, manual reconciliations and opaque government processes, often without a single source of truth for resident funding and fees.

    

    Launched in July 2024, Revenue Partner was developed to fundamentally transform how providers manage aged care funding. The service combines automated data workflows, resident-level insights, reconciliation, education and embedded subject matter expertise into a single, scalable model. Unlike traditional tools or advisory services, Revenue Partner embeds funding specialists directly alongside providers to resolve issues in real time, from interpreting resident fee letters to resolving payment anomalies with Services Australia.

    

    The impact is immediate and sustained: reduced administrative burden, recovered revenue, faster resolution of complex issues and greater transparency.

    

    Why it was developed

    

    Over the past decade, Provider Assist's Supplement Recovery team reviewed more than 4,500 facilities, representing over one million residents, and identified more than $400 million in previously missed accommodation-related funding and fees.

    

    Across these reviews, the same systemic issues repeatedly emerged: inaccurately charged resident fees, misalignment between Services Australia assessments and provider billing, and revenue gaps that could not be recovered retrospectively. While Supplement Recovery addressed historical losses, it did not prevent future leakage.

    

    At the same time, providers were facing major funding reform, rising costs and increasing pressure to demonstrate financial sustainability, often without the internal expertise or systems required to manage this complexity. The sector needed a proactive, embedded service that delivered ongoing assurance, capability building and real-time intervention.

    

    Revenue Partner was created to meet that need, and several testimonials were provided in support of the Future of Ageing Award submission.

    

    "Since AN-ACC came in, that's the first time anybody has ever shown us that breakdown. [Though this session was focused on funding and finance] I want to take those slides and give them to our RNs — nobody has ever explained that to us in this detail."

    

    Testimonial from an aged care provider using Revenue Partner.

    

    The problem facing provider s

    

    Finance and billing teams must navigate three funding systems and four government departments, often receiving conflicting advice and limited transparency. Data is fragmented, fee letters are difficult to track and reconcile, and even advanced teams struggle to interrogate Services Australia payment statements at scale.

    

    Manual reconciliation remains common, relying on spreadsheets and overstretched staff. Without timely, resident-level insights or access to funding expertise, providers are exposed to revenue leakage, compliance risk and reputational harm at a time when financial sustainability is critical.

    

    The solution: Revenue Partner

    

    Revenue Partner replaces fragmented tools and manual processes with an integrated service model that brings together data infrastructure, automation and embedded expertise.

    

    Key components include:

    

    Revenue Bootcamp: Structured education for admissions, finance, billing and sales teams, building shared understanding of payment statements, AN-ACC, means-testing and regulatory change.

    

    RevNav Platform: A central system reconciling millions of Services Australia transactions to individual resident records, eliminating manual tracking and creating a single source of truth.

    

    Letters Module: Automated retrieval, storage and data extraction of Residential Care Fee Letters, ensuring no correspondence is missed.

    

    Reconciliation Module: Detailed comparisons between billed and paid amounts across Means-Tested Care Fees, Refundable and Daily Accommodation Contributions, and upcoming non-clinical contributions, with month-on-month variance analysis.

    

    Embedded mentoring and support: Monthly sessions and on-demand access to subject-matter experts to resolve issues, monitor trends and respond to Services Australia in real time.

    

    Admissions audit: Ongoing audits to ensure accommodation funding and fees are never missed again.

    

    Implementation and sustainability

    

    Revenue Partner is implemented through a structured onboarding phase anchored by Revenue Bootcamp, followed by a predictable monthly service cadence. Monitoring and evaluation are embedded, focusing on billing gaps, funding variances, emerging risks and client confidence.

    

    The service is priced on a tiered, per-bed-per-day model, with modular flexibility that allows providers to scale up or down as needs change.

    

    "Great commercial product to support providers."

    

    2025 Future of Ageing Awards judge

    

    Evidence of success

    

    In its first 12 months, Revenue Partner has returned more than $1 million in withheld funding and incorrectly charged fees to providers and residents. Reconciliation time for Means-Tested Care Fees and accommodation contributions has been reduced from over an hour per resident to seconds, with full visibility back to each resident's date of entry.

    

    Beyond financial outcomes, Revenue Partner has significantly improved provider capability, confidence and transparency, delivering a step change in how aged care funding is managed.

Headline: Most for-profit homes fail on care targets
Full Text:
Less than half of for-profit-run aged care homes met both of their care minute targets (44 per cent) and only 51 per cent met total care minute

   targets during Q1 2025-26 (July to September), the latest Care Minutes in Residential Aged Care Dashboard shows.

    

    These findings come amidst total care minutes delivered sector-wide increasing to 220, which is five minutes more than the 215-minute target.

    

    And they compare to the sector average of just over two-thirds of homes meeting total care minute targets (67 per cent), four in five achieving the required registered nursing minutes (81 per cent) and three in five meeting both targets (60 per cent).

    

    See below for comments from Mirus Australia's Tyler Fisher and StewartBrown's David Sinclair on financial costs and benefits of meeting care minute targets.

    

    Regarding the for-profits, compliance was lowest in large regional towns – Modified Monash Model 3 – sitting at 27 per cent. But even in metropolitan areas – MM1 – just 46 per cent of homes reached both care minute targets.

    

    Not-for-profit-run homes are performing better, with two-thirds reaching both of their care minute targets. In MM1, 71 per cent of homes met both targets and even in very remote areas 60 per cent of homes met both targets.

    

    Registered nurse care minutes were higher for both for-profit and not-for-profit homes, with the rate of not-for-profits meeting their RN requirements ranging from 70 per cent in medium rural towns to 100 per cent in very remote communities.

    

    There are no for-profit providers operating homes in MM6 or MM7, but compliance with RN requirements ranged from 58 per cent in large regional towns to 76 per cent in both metropolitan and small rural towns.

    

    Cost of compliance versus profit

    

    Care minute targets have additional implications for homes operating in MM1 areas, with non-compliant facilities facing reduced AN-ACC funding from April 2026 based on the October-December 2025 period. Aged care homes in MM1 areas delivering services to specialised communities are excluded from the penalty.

    

    Those not complying stand to lose up to in excess of $30 per resident per day in funding, according to aged care number crunchers including FicusBridge chief executive officer Dr Tanvi Dalal and the team behind the UTS Ageing Research Collaborative Australia's Aged Care Sector: Full-Year Report 2024-25.

    

    The UARC report also showed how direct care margins decline with higher compliance ratios. On average, homes currently reaching less than 90 per cent of their care minute target reported a $41.87 margin while those exceeding targets reported a $0.58 margin, the report noted.

    

    The homes that reached their care minute targets were also found to have a greater reliance on agency staff for registered and enrolled nurses, and reported higher staff overtime.

    

    Cost of compliance versus the penalty

    

    Speaking to Australian Ageing Agenda, Mirus Australia data scientist Tyler Fisher said the additional tax burden on for-profit providers means they need to be more diligent with their profit and loss to achieve the same financial performance as not-for-profits and that "it is frequently the case" that the cost to meet a care minute target is more expensive than the penalty for not meeting it.

    

    "The higher the cost of employment is, the more frequently this scenario occurs, while lowering employment costs through fringe benefits such as salary packaging makes it easier to add minutes at a price that is lower than the penalty," he said.

    

    But while it may be more economical for some homes to not meet their targets right now, the entire sector is trending towards compliance and it will become harder for them to maintain non-compliance as they become more of an outlier, he added.

    

    Also speaking to AAA, StewartBrown partner David Sinclair said both cohorts may look at the cost-benefit of taking a hit on the AN-ACC supplement compared to hiring staff, but most of the for-profit providers he has spoken to are aiming to meet the care minute targets.

    

    "When you have a look at the total care when it's delivered, they're only half a minute away from overall meeting the target on average," he said.

    

    "There's a hard statistic that 46 per cent of them [in MM1] aren't meeting both targets. It is what it is. It's the statistic and there are less for-profits that are going to be meeting it than not-for-profits. That's just a hard fact. But it looks as if they're probably not that far away.

    

    "When you look at the distribution graph… the majority is sitting in the 5-10 minute mark. So, it won't take much for them to meet it."

    

    He also noted that the care minute results are based on a single home level, making it difficult to really see whether care minute non-compliance is systemic at a provider level.

    

    Mr Fisher also noted that Mirus Australia benchmarks all its data across both the for-profit and not-for-profit cohorts but historically they have behaved quite differently – with different markers used when analysing the occupancy, claiming activity, care minute targets and average daily subsidy – the mean entitlement of subsidy for all residents, including respite but excluding defaults.

    

    However, in the last 18 months, Mr Fisher said the two cohorts have started aligning very closely with ADS, occupancy and claiming.

    

    Providers incorrectly reporting minutes

    

    This week, the Department of Health, Disability and Ageing has flagged providers incorrectly reporting care minutes. It has updated Appendix 3 in the care minutes guide for residential aged care.

    

    Some of the common reporting errors seen in provider's quarterly financial report include:

    

    the misallocation of staffing categories

    

    the inclusion of non-direct care activities like cleaning, laundry and food preparation

    

    the incorrect apportionment of direct and non-direct care work time by staff working in hybrid roles.

    

    The updated care minutes guide is available here

Headline: The Shellharbour business navigating new end-of-life care funding roadblocks
Full Text:
A Shellharbour palliative care business is spreading the word about money designed for older people with less than three months to live and who want to remain at home.

    The End-of-Life Pathway supplies participants with $25,000 over 12 weeks after being assessed as eligible by a doctor or nurse practitioner and is part of the Support at Home program.

    

    The Support at Home program replaced the Home Care Packages (HCP) program and the Short-Term Restorative Care (STRC) programme from November 1, 2025.

    

    The Commonwealth Home Support Program will also transition to Support at Home "no earlier than July 1, 2027.

    

    The new program is designed to help older people keep living at home longer, be connected to their community and receive health and wellbeing support.

    

    The changes are a response to the Royal Commission into Aged Care Quality and Safety's recommendation to bring together the in-home aged care programs.

    

    Sisternightingale Home Nursing 's Jade Rowland said the funding in the End-of-Life Pathway is a "great change", but there have been difficulties accessing it.

    

    "The thing that we have found is that people who already have a provider, and have already been receiving services for years, some of these providers aren't allowing them to do the end-of-life pathway," she said.

    

    "When they contact the provider, the private provider is saying, 'Oh, sorry, we're not able to do that yet'.

    

    "Some clients could be turned off by this instead of exploring other options, leading to a lack of support that actually is available."

    

    Once a person is assessed to be eligible for the funding by a doctor, Ms Rowland says, "it's left in their hands to find a provider".

    

    "It's amazing this money is there for people to use, there are a few roadblocks with some people being able to access it the way that they should be able to," she said.

    

    'We would like our community to remember our name, Sisternightingale, and to call us. Even if people are calling us just to ask what the funding is and how to use it."

    

    The $25,000 in funding can provide "a lot of support" for patients who need it.

    

    "We are able to get clients a lot more care, if someone's got say three or four weeks left to live, that is a significant amount of nursing. That would be hours of overnight and daily care," Jade's wife, and palliative care nurse Sarah Rowland, said.

    

    "At the moment, we're providing care for a patient with two to three and a half hour respite shifts, plus daily two-hour visits.

    

    "We won't even touch that funding in the period of weeks we'll be looking after her."

    

    Sisternightingale only sends palliative register or endorsed enrolled nurses to clients, and offers a liaison service with providers, community palliative care services and the patient's extended health care team.

    

    The service based in Shellharbour also has 24-hour phone and clinical support, and works with families on roster systems to help personalise care to needs.

    

    Jade Rowland urged anyone with questions about the funding to contact Sisternightingale

    

    "Even if they don't use our service, that's fine. I can still direct them to what they should be looking for and the supports that should be available for them."

Headline: 'Lot of complaints': ex-minister's aged care warning
Full Text:
Older Australians are dealing with big price increases and longer waiting lists for care under major reforms to the aged care sector, a former Liberal minister warns.

    Christopher Pyne, who spent 26 years in federal parliament, has also warned against stereotyping older people as either wealthy baby boomers or frail retirees, describing both framings as "crude caricatures".

    

    Mr Pyne, now the chairman of advocacy group Council on the Ageing, said it would take time to iron out the kinks in the government's new aged care act.

    

    Advertisement

    

    The overhaul of the aged care sector took effect in November 2025, requiring wealthier retirees to pay more for their care and restructuring the way services are funded.

    

    Addressing the National Press Club to launch the council's State of the Older Nation report, the former cabinet minister said while it had only been a few months since the reforms kicked in, people in care had raised a number of complaints.

    

    They included longer waits for home care packages, pressure to sign care agreements quickly, and sharp price rises.

    

    "We expect the government will react to those over time," Mr Pyne said.

    

    He said older people were also being forced to pay for assistance showering.

    

    "Because that's such a big thing, of course, it gets a lot of coverage and a lot of complaints," Mr Pyne said.

    

    The State of the Older Nation report, released by Mr Pyne on Wednesday, includes alarming figures about the number of people experiencing age-based discrimination.

    

    Advertisement

    

    It found 38 per cent of people had experienced ageism since turning 50 and 36 per cent said they didn't want to return to the workforce because of ageism.

    

    "Too often, the story of older Australians is reduced to one of two basic plot lines," Mr Pyne said.

    

    "The first goes like this: older people - often framed as baby boomers - are wealthy, secure, asset rich, sitting on housing, insulated from economic pressure and blocking opportunities for younger generations.

    

    "The second sits at the opposite extreme. Older people, often those in their late 70s and 80s are portrayed as frail dependent in nursing homes, clogging hospital beds and placing an unsustainable burden on public resources."

    

    Mr Pyne said the stereotypes persisted due to convenience, not accuracy.

    

    "They ignore the community and economic contribution of older people, simplify complex policy changes and allow us to avoid uncomfortable conversations about inequality and disadvantage."

    

    Australian Associated Press

Headline: The Shellharbour business navigating new end-of-life care funding roadblocks
Full Text:
Jade, left, and Sarah Rowland from Sisternightingale. Picture by Robert Peet  Shellharbour elderly can benefit from $25k care funding

    Jade, left, and Sarah Rowland from Sisternightingale. Picture by Robert Peet

    

    A Shellharbour palliative care business is spreading the word about money designed for older people with less than three months to live and who want to remain at home. The End-of-Life Pathway supplies participants with $25,000 over 12 weeks after being assessed as eligible by a doctor or nurse practitioner and is part of the Support at Home program. The Support at Home program replaced the Home Care Packages (HCP) program and the Short-Term Restorative Care (STRC) programme from November 1, 2025. The Commonwealth Home Support Program will also transition to Support at Home "no earlier than July 1, 2027. The new program is designed to help older people keep living at home longer, be connected to their community and receive health and wellbeing support. The changes are a response to the Royal Commission into Aged Care Quality and Safety's recommendation to bring together the in-home aged care programs. Sisternightingale Home Nursing's Jade Rowland said the funding in the End-of-Life Pathway is a "great change", but there have been difficulties accessing it. "The thing that we have found is that people who already have a provider, and have already been receiving services for years, some of these providers aren't allowing them to do the end-of-life pathway," she said. "When they contact the provider, the private provider is saying, 'Oh, sorry, we're not able to do that yet'. "Some clients could be turned off by this instead of exploring other options, leading to a lack of support that actually is available." Once a person is assessed to be eligible for the funding by a doctor, Ms Rowland says, "it's left in their hands to find a provider". "It's amazing this money is there for people to use, there are a few roadblocks with some people being able to access it the way that they should be able to," she said. 'We would like our community to remember our name, Sisternightingale, and to call us. Even if people are calling us just to ask what the funding is and how to use it." The $25,000 in funding can provide "a lot of support" for patients who need it. "We are able to get clients a lot more care, if someone's got say three or four weeks left to live, that is a significant amount of nursing. That would be hours of overnight and daily care," Jade's wife, and palliative care nurse Sarah Rowland, said. "At the moment, we're providing care for a patient with two to three and a half hour respite shifts, plus daily two-hour visits. "We won't even touch that funding in the period of weeks we'll be looking after her." Sisternightingale only sends palliative register or endorsed enrolled nurses to clients, and offers a liaison service with providers, community palliative care services and the patient's extended health care team. The service based in Shellharbour also has 24-hour phone and clinical support, and works with families on roster systems to help personalise care to needs. Jade Rowland urged anyone with questions about the funding to contact Sisternightingale. "Even if they don't use our service, that's fine. I can still direct them to what they should be looking for and the supports that should be available for them."

Headline: ‘We're all in the trenches': Friend reveals private struggle of Mosman Park family
Full Text:
A friend has shared the private struggles of the Perth family found dead in a suspected murder-suicide, amid criticisms they were "failed" by the NDIS.

    Listen to this article

    

    Four dead in suspected double murder-suicide

    

    Four people were found dead in a residence in Mosman Park Perth. The four individuals included a...

    

    A friend has shared the hidden struggles of the Perth family found dead in a suspected murder-suicide, describing them as "in the trenches trying to survive".

    

    Maiwenna 'Mai' Goasdoue, 49, and Jarrod Clune, 50, and their two teenage sons, Leon, 16, and Otis, 14, were found dead inside their Mosman Park home on Friday.

    

    Both boys had severe autism – Otis was non-verbal – and were previously enrolled at the Peter Moyes Centre, the highly sought-after special needs centre at the prestigious Christ Church Grammar School. The brothers stopped attending the school in recent years, reportedly after one of them was asked to leave.

    

    A carer for Leon and Otis alerted police to the tragedy early on Friday morning, after finding a note on the home's front door urging them to call authorities.

    

    It's understood that a second note found inside – which reportedly indicated the parents decided together to end their lives – led police to determine it a murder-suicide.

    

    In the days since, support workers and friends have said they were "failed" by the National Disability Insurance Scheme (NDIS), and that Mai and Jarrod likely felt "they had no other choice".

    

    One family friend met Ms Goasdoue through Perth's autism community, with the two supporting each other and "talked about our respective challenges" as full-time carers of children with "extreme behaviours".

    

    "You reach out to other people who are in similar situations … But the people who understand you best are in the least position to give you support," the friend, who asked not to be named, told The Age

    

    "We're all in the trenches trying to survive. And that tends to lead to increasing isolation."

    

    Detectives have said the family engaged with care workers, but declined to comment further.

    

    The friend said there was "no doubt" the boys' behaviour would have taken a toll.

    

    "You're talking about violence, meltdowns, vicious behaviour that could leave you with injuries," they said.

    

    "And these kids with these extreme behaviours are let down the most. There really isn't support for families like ours. There's nothing meaningful."

    

    Asked whether the case would influence future reforms to the NDIS, Federal Health Minister Mark Butler on Tuesday declined to comment on the nature of care given to the family.

    

    "It's an awful tragedy at so many different levels but particularly the loss of two children's lives who obviously did not choose to die is an unspeakable tragedy," Mr Butler told reporters.

    

    "I know there's been a bit of commentary over in Western Australia about the NDIS packages that these children were on. I want to be really clear. First of all, I don't want to say anything that would impact the police investigation.

    

    "I want to give people the confidence that the NDIA, the agency, is cooperating fully with the police to the extent that they have any useful inputs to the police investigation.

    

    "I also want to be clear that there are very clear legal restrictions on our ability, my ability, the ability of the NDIA, to say anything about the packages that the children were on, so I'm not in a position to respond to some of the speculation that I've seen in Western Australian media.

    

    "All I want to say is that this is just an awful tragedy, just an unspeakable tragedy for this family, but for the broader community, and I know the NDIA will do everything it can to assist the police in its inquiries."

    

    An NDIA spokesperson said the organisation would work closely with authorities to support their investigations.

    

    "We extend our deepest sympathies to the Clune Goasdoue's family, their loved ones and all those affected by this tragic loss," the spokesperson said.

    

    "This matter is currently under investigation by Western Australian authorities.

    

    "The NDIA will work closely with the Western Australian authorities to support their inquiries or investigations."

    

    A former support worker for Leon and Otis, Maddie Page, said that despite the "love and resilience" Mai and Jarrod showed to their sons "during times of extreme difficulty and countless obstacles", the parents may have felt "they had no other choice".

    

    "The consistent dedication and deep love Mai and Jarrod showed for their boys, and the ways they never stopped fighting for them to feel seen and heard in this world will never be forgotten," Ms Page said.

    

    "May they rest in peace together as a family, NDIS – do better."

    

    Developmental Disability WA chief executive Mary Butterworth said the tragedy showed it was "fairly obvious" the family were not getting enough support.

    

    More investment in extra training of staff and support would alleviate stress so families do not feel it is "all up to them to try and survive", she said.

    

    "The reality is, in Western Australia we do not have enough trained support workers who can provide support to families," Ms Butterworth told the ABC.

    

    "I feel sorry for the support providers; they do not have enough funding in their plans that allows for them to provide deep and thorough training and mentoring to their staff."

Headline:
Interview
ladies ready for them yet we love the cable cars in san francisco broke down once and along came a vehicle with a bar on the front which pushed us to get us going again fun if not in a hurry
Full Text:
Interview
ladies ready for them yet we love the cable cars in san francisco broke down once and along came a vehicle with a bar on the front which pushed us to get us going again fun if not in a hurry just loved our stay in san fran just like you one of my favourite stops yeah it is an amazing city, San Francisco. Just, yeah, it's probably the one place America I could live. It is a magnificent city. Well, new retirement village laws take effect in South Australia from today. What will they mean for residents and operators? Joining us now for the Minister for Ageing Well is Nat Cook. Nat, good morning to you. Hi, Graeme. How are you? Good, thanks. What is this going to mean? Yeah, so we've been been talking about this on and off, and thanks for the time that we've had talking about this before. All those, the review that's happened on the Retirement Villagers Act and all the work that's gone into it from so many residents and people in the public who are very interested in this, as well as the cooperation of the operators, we've now got yesterday, these new rules come into play. So the important thing that we are focusing on is the way that looks for people who, A, want to live in a retirement village and need information and need clarity about what their life will look like from a, not just a connected community point of view, but from a financial point of view. And then what happens once you're in the retirement village and how you work together with the operator and other residents for harmony in your life and making sure everyone's on the same page in of what happens in the village and that you're given appropriate information. And then also really understanding what could happen from a financial and a personal point of view when and if you want to leave or need to leave the retirement village and go on to another phase of life. So I think it's about the transparency of the information up front, the clarity of that information and how to seek help to understand it and then also how that might look when you live in the retirement village, receiving that information and working with the operators and then also peace of mind so that you know what you're up for when you're leaving.
Yeah. So what's biggest change that residents will actually notice?
Oh, I think the transparency in contracts and the disclosure statement for people who are going to live in there should provide people with better information to continue to refer back to once they live there. And then also for people who are living in retirement villages also, there are enhanced codes of conduct to come in for operators, staff and residents. And what we're doing there is providing support for operators and staff to receive education over the next year to make sure people do have a better standard and quality of and staff member working in and around villages. So there's also some strengthening around emergency planning and safety standards and that's really important and should offer people peace of mind around their village. Headline: Mater staff finally feel action will occur
Full Text:
CALVARY Mater Newcastle staff feel for the first time that action will be taken to fix the mould fiasco at the hospital, after almost a decade of frustration over maintenance trouble.

    In NSW Parliament yesterday, the Opposition and Greens put pressure on the Minns government to ensure it follows through on promised action.

    Greens MP Amanda Cohn said "there were critical safety and maintenance issues at the Calvary Mater hospital that were reported by staff through the proper channels as early as 2017".

    "It has taken years for there to be the attention on this issue that it deserves," Dr Cohn said.

    Staff estimate the systemic mould issue has been occurring for at least a year, but water leaks and other maintenance trouble had been happening for years.

    Shadow Health Minister Sarah Mitchell said "the problem we have is there are repeated incidents every week".

    "The staff up there literally told the Newcastle Herald it feels like an episode of Stranger Things. This is not what a health service should look like in 2026."

    After meeting NSW Health Minister Ryan Park at the hospital on Monday, staff "felt heard for the first time and that action was going to be taken", one staffer said.

    "His remarks and obvious frustration are exactly our experience when dealing with the PPP [public-private partnership]."

    As for fixing the mould problem, Mr Park said "it has to happen". "I'm determined to do it as fast as I possibly can," he added.

    The Newcastle Herald reported on Monday that most of the intensive care unit and rooms in three wards were shut at the weekend for "deep cleaning".

    These areas had "slightly elevated levels" of the mould Aspergillus fumigatus.

    The hospital also closed two wards in January due to mould in the air-conditioning ducts and maggots falling from the ceiling onto patients.

    This included the closure of a haematology day ward - also known as ward 5Dwith cancer patients moved to another ward.

    Mr Park said, "I am deeply angry and sorry that staff are in this position".

    "They shouldn't have to be worrying about trying to manage this situation with private contractors who should be doing their job."

    The Mater is a public hospital run by a private company called Novacare, which is responsible for the building maintenance and cleaning.

    Honeywell is also responsible for the hospital's maintenance, as one of four companies that make up Novacare, along with Westpac Bank, Medirest and Abigroup.

    Mr Park said the government was looking at legal options and would "hold the contractors to account".

    Late last year, the minister "brought in the contractors on multiple occasions at my office in Sydney and at Parliament House".

    "I'd been given the clear impression and undertaking that there was a pathway forward to deal particularly with the cladding, which will go a long way to deal with the water and mould-related issues. Over the summer break, it was clear - although there'd been scaffolding put up - that work was not happening."

    Mr Park said he and NSW Health secretary Susan Pearce decided to "step into" the PPP contract and "begin the cladding rectification works".

    His [Ryan Park's] remarks and obvious frustration are exactly our experience when dealing with the PPP [public-private partnership] Mater staffer

In NSW Parliament yesterday, the Opposition and Greens put pressure on the Minns government to ensure it follows through on promised action.

Headline: NSW parliamentary inquiry to investigate mould and maggot issues at Newcastle's Mater Hospital
Full Text:
A state parliamentary inquiry will be established to probe a NSW hospital that was impacted by mould and maggot contamination in the last month.

    The Calvary Mater Hospital in Newcastle was forced to close a cancer ward in January when mould was discovered in an air-conditioning system.

    

    Then later that month patients were moved from the haematology ward when maggots were discovered in their room

    

    And last week mould was again detected in several of the hospital's wards, including the intensive care unit.

    

    NSW Opposition spokesperson for Health Sarah Mitchell moved the motion in the NSW Legislative Assembly this afternoon to establish an inquiry to investigate the issues in depth.

    

    The motion was backed by the Coalition and the Greens but Labor voted against it.

    

    "I know that [Health Minister Ryan Park] has been to Newcastle.. I don't think it's good enough to sort of say, 'well, we're working through it and we're going to try and fix the immediate problems'," Ms Mitchell said.

    

    She said the inquiry would also look into the public private partnership [PPP] that runs the hospital, with company Novacare in charge of maintaining the facilities and Calvary providing healthcare services alongside the NSW Government.

    

    This is an opportunity for this parliament and for this committee to do its job, have a transparent look at what has happened there, make sensible recommendations to the minister and to the government about ways forward which can be done.

    

   Patients 'anxious'

     Greens spokesperson for health Dr Amanda Cohn said it had taken far too long to fix the issues.

    

    "Staff reported issues through the proper channels with maintenance and cleanliness of this hospital as far back as 2017," she said.

    

    "It has taken far, far too long for these issues to be resolved at the expense of patient safety and patient care.

    

    "It's disrupted their continuity of care and it's made a lot of people rightly anxious about the safety of the hospital."

    

    Despite voting against the inquiry, Labor spokesperson in the legislative assembly, Courtney Houssos told parliament that solving the issue had to be the first priority.

    

    "We're committed to resolving that as quickly as possible," she said.

    

    "That's got to be the first priority ... it has to be about fixing the problems and then we can get to the bottom of what's happened."

    

    She said it was important that members of parliament and the community had the opportunity to ask questions.

    

    "I would just absolutely emphasise the fact that we're stepping in,"

    

    she said.

    

    "There's a rectification of the cladding, there's rectification of the heating, the ventilation and the air conditioning."

    

    Health Minister Ryan Park visited the Mater Hospital on Monday and said the government was stepping in to get the cladding fixed and hold contractors to account, after remediation works were not completed on time.

    

    "We absolutely understand that staff are frustrated that this has taken too long," Ms Houssos said.

    

    A spokesperson from the Calvary Mater welcomed the inquiry.

    

    "Calvary Health Care welcomes the call for a transparent inquiry and we await the terms of reference," the statement read.

    

    The ABC has been unable to contact any of the private partners for comment while Mr Park declined to comment.

Headline: Tasmanian Health Minister Bridget Archer responds to calls for new Royal Hobart Hospital
Full Text:
The Australian Medical Association has called for a new Royal Hobart Hospital to be built by 2035, saying the current building won't be able to meet demand.

    It comes as a funding agreement is finalised between the state and federal government for Tasmania's hospital system.

    

    Health Minister Bridget Archer responds, speaking to Leon Compton on Tasmania Mornings.

    

    "What we have is a 30 year master plan for the Royal Hobart Hospital and that is what we have been investing in to date, some $520 million on a range of upgrades," she says.

    

    Ms Archer is also asked about access to IVF, gender affirming care and terminations at the Hobart Private Hospital when Calvary takes it over, as well as her views on the leadership of the federal Liberal Party.

    

    Leon Compton, Presenter

    

    Penny McLeod, Producer

    

    Eliza Kloser, Producer

    

    Image Details

    

    Heath Minister Bridget Archer pictured in state parliament. ABC News: Ebony ten Broeke

Headline:
News
Meetings will take place on Wednesday and Locally in Tasmania's health minister, Bridget Archer, says she's committed to ensuring gender affirming and reproductive health care remains accessible
Full Text:
News
Meetings will take place on Wednesday and Locally in Tasmania's health minister, Bridget Archer, says she's committed to ensuring gender affirming and reproductive health care remains accessible in the state. Catholic provider Calvary is set to buy the Hobart private hospital from Healthscope, ending fears about the hospital's future. However, at its its other sites, Calvary does not allow vasectomies, gender-affirming care, surgical terminations or fertility treatments. Bridget Archer told ABC Mornings those services will continue to be available in the state, but wouldn't be drawn on whether should we push in Calvary to deliver them.
Whether that is through Calvary and those are issues continue to be worked through the sale process, but certainly from my point of view, we will continue to ensure that those essential services are delivered to Tasmanians.
Northern Territory Police say their efforts to quell violence in the remote community of Wadair have been hampered because children as young as eight are involved. Jane Barden reports.
Police fired tear gas at the weekend to try to stop the latest round of street fighting involving up to 100 people armed with axes and window louvres at a time. NT Police Acting Commander Terry Zhang says officers then had to pull back.
It wasn't that we couldn't respond effectively, it's just it would require a higher level of force to respond. And because of the young people involved, we didn't want to do that. We want to work with community leaders to safely resolve this incident.
The rioting has been caused by a decades-old feud between Indigenous families.

Headline: ‘Treated like slaves': Junior doctors' class action against St Vincent's Hospital
Full Text:
Junior doctors are suing St Vincent's Hospital for millions in unpaid overtime, alleging the hospital enforces a shadow policy that demands they work excessive hours off the books, or risk career sabotage.

    

    As many as 2500 doctors are expected to join the class action launched this week, which has the hallmarks of another junior doctor class action against NSW Health that led to the biggest underpayment settlement in Australian history.

    

    "Relying on unpaid overtime carried out by doctors is now sadly part of the business model of how hospitals operate," said Hayden Stephens, director of the law firm representing the junior doctors in both class actions.

    

    Junior clinicians, senior specialists and doctors' unions have repeatedly warned that unpaid overtime is baked into Australia's hospital system as a shoddy fix for understaffing

    

    Ultimately, the fatigue caused by doctors working excessive hours was an "unacceptable risk to patient safety", Stephens said.

    

    More than 350 junior doctors have registered for the class action, which covers those who worked at St Vincent's from January 2020.

    

    They described being required to start early and stay back after their rostered shifts for ward rounds and handovers, to chase blood results, update patients and families, speak to other hospitals, complete discharge summaries and respond to emergencies.

    

    Several reported working to the point of exhaustion, their management actively discouraging them from claiming overtime, and fearing that doing so would jeopardise their future in highly competitive specialties.

    

    During her interview with Stephens' firm, one female doctor described being called in almost every night and working 20 hours of unpaid overtime a week.

    

    Another junior doctor alleged that human resources had told them there was an "unsaid expectation and unwritten rule to perform unrostered overtime without remuneration".

    

    A third said she was instructed: "Don't try and claim [sic]. If you try to claim for overtime, you are not working hard enough, and you will not be paid."

    

    "[I]nterns and residents specifically do not claim for their overtime [fearing] the department view them as a burden and an inefficient doctor," a registrar said.

    

    Stephens said the doctors' case was simple. "This is not a call for increased pay, just that doctors be paid for the work they do," he said.

    

    A spokesperson said St Vincent's Hospital Sydney "treats the safety and wellbeing of our people, including our junior medical officers, with the utmost seriousness".

    

    Professor Deborah Yates, a senior respiratory specialist who resigned from St Vincent's in 2025, said junior doctors' fears that claiming overtime could jeopardise their careers were justified.

    

    "Their overtime claim is fed back to the head of department, and the head of department … and the supervisors are the ones who allow the person to progress in their training," Yates said.

    

    "If you upset your supervisor, you basically can be out of it all, [so] there's an advantage to not being seen to be any trouble.

    

    "They are doing very long, onerous work, and people become so stressed that they develop a range of other issues. Families break up … and the suicide rate is really high."

    

    In 2024, NSW Health agreed to pay $299.8 million in unpaid overtime to more than 20,000 junior doctors in NSW public hospitals to resolve a class action lodged by Hayden Stephens and Associates and Maurice Blackburn.

    

    Stephens said the state award covering these doctors has terms and conditions nearly identical to those of the federal enterprise agreement covering St Vincent's doctors.

    

    Dr Meg Landgraf, the class action's lead applicant, said that for years St Vincent's management has not recognised doctors' excessive hours.

    

    "The NSW doctors' settlement ensures that every hour a doctor works counts," Landgraf said.

    

    "All we ask is that St Vincent's now do the same."

    

    Meanwhile, in Victoria, a class action on behalf of 12,000 Victorian junior doctors settled for $175 million in October, and in the ACT, 2200 junior doctors received a $31.5 million settlement in 2024.

    

    "We need to stop people being treated like slaves, basically, and move to a situation where they recognise their contribution," Yates said. "If we lose them, we're really in trouble."

    

    Stephens said his firm wrote to St Vincent's Sydney in July. Informal discussions took place in August and September, but stopped, prompting Stephens to write to St Vincent's lawyers, urging them to engage. Stephens said he received no response and has had zero communication since September.

    

    From our partners

Headline: Queensland disability client Bridie Gilligan choked to death on Hungry Jacks burger, coroner finds
Full Text:
Coronial findings revealed the woman lost consciousness and later died after shoving the fast food chain's "Yumbo" burger into her mouth at her Cairns home.

    A Queensland disability client choked to death on a Hungry Jacks burger after snatching it from her support worker, a coroner has found.

    

    Cairns woman Bridie Gilligan had a history of choking and was on a restricted diet when she choked while eating a Hungry Jacks "Yumbo" burger at her Edmonton home on April 29, 2021.

    

    Coronial findings published on Tuesday by Deputy State Coroner Stephanie Gallagher revealed how Ms Gilligan, 42, lost consciousness and later died after shoving the burger into her mouth.

    

    The inquest heard Ms Gilligan's support worker Margaret Lowry had purchased a burger for each of them and had intended to cut Ms Gilligan's into small pieces - but Ms Gilligan quickly snatched it and ran outside first.

    

    "The support worker called an ambulance and Ms Gilligan was taken to hospital but later died," Ms Gallagher said.

    

    The inquest heard Ms Gilligan had suffered a catastrophic brain injury and was pronounced brain dead on May 3, 2021.

    

    Her family have since expressed concerns about whether it had been appropriate to give her the burger given her disability and dietary needs.

    

    Ms Gilligan had been living in accommodation provided by Endeavour Foundation at the time of her death, as she required support for her intellectual disability associated with Cornelia De Lange Syndrome.

    

    She had been largely non-verbal and "required assistance with all her activities of daily living", Ms Gallagher noted.

    

    Ms Gilligan's mother told police her daughter was supposed to be given soft-textured foods and also be "completely supervised and assisted with eating, as if she was left alone with food then it is likely a choking incident would occur".

    

    Ms Gilligan's death was investigated by the Office of the Work Health and Safety Prosecutor, who concluded they were "not satisfied there was sufficient evidence to commence a prosecution against either the Endeavour Foundation or its worker, Ms Lowry".

    

    Ms Gallagher said in her findings on Tuesday that she accepted Ms Gilligan's death was preventable; however, she found Ms Lowry's supervision and Endeavour's systems regarding diet were both appropriate.

    

    "The concerns expressed by Ms Gilligan's family must be balanced against the fact that Ms Gilligan was a challenging client who did not cope well with new support workers," Ms Gallagher said.

    

    Support workers had given varying views to the inquest about whether giving fast food to a client was appropriate - with one suggesting it may actually support their independence.

    

    However, the worker noted that the decision should still be communicated with and resolved with the appropriate decision-maker - which would have been Ms Gilligan's mother in this case.

    

    A dietitian had given evidence saying she would not consider a burger or bun appropriate for Ms Gilligan's specific meal plan.

    

    Ms Gallagher ultimately agreed that the burger, even if cut up into small pieces, would not have complied with the standards set out for Ms Gilligan's diet - but she pointed out that Ms Gilligan's meal plan had contained other foods that also didn't comply with the standards.

    

    She said this had been due to competing considerations surrounding Ms Gilligan's quality of life, and the foods included had been ones Ms Gilligan liked or ones that gave her some level of autonomy or independence.

    

    Ms Gallagher considered it was not unreasonable for Ms Lowry to have thought a burger was suitable under the circumstances, given she had not been given specific training on the requirements of Ms Gilligan's "soft and bite-sized" diet, and the meal plan had included sandwiches.

    

    "Ms Lowry's evidence, which was not challenged, was that she had given Ms Gilligan cheeseburgers before, and Ms Gilligan had been able to eat them when they had been cut up into small pieces," Ms Gallagher noted.

    

    The inquest heard Endeavour had multiple policies and procedures in place to ensure workers had adequate information and training to care for a person with eating and swallowing difficulties.

    

    Endeavour staff had emphasised during the inquest that the support worker's goal is to support their clients to live as independently as possible.

    

    They acknowledged that the introduction of the NDIS had created a separation between Supported Independent Living providers like Endeavour and their client's other health services, however.

    

    In Ms Gilligan's case, the court heard this resulted in delayed follow ups with her other health services, a lack of collaboration on meal plans, and no guidance or training being provided to Ms Gilligan's support workers about her specific diet requirements.

    

    Ms Gallagher found Endeavour had not received sufficient information from Ms Gilligan's health care providers to identify the "real risk" bread products posed to her, and acknowledged Ms Gilligan's risk of choking was not appropriately managed by her overall system of care.

    

    "Endeavour has made sensible and appropriate changes to its systems and processes since Ms Gilligan's death to ensure that a similar tragic outcome does not occur in the future," Ms Gallagher said.

    

    "... I do not accept, on the evidence before me, that the responsibility for the circumstances which led to Ms Gilligan's death lies with Endeavour Foundation or their employees and agents."

Headline: Inside the new $411 million ‘purpose-built' St George Hospital redevelopment
Full Text:
A southern Sydney hospital's $411 million redevelopment is complete, with a new nine-storey building and upgraded campus set to open within months and provide enhanced services.

    A major hospital upgrade in Sydney's south has been completed to the tune of $411 million after a year of major construction.

    

    The third stage in the redevelopment of St George Hospital is complete, with its doors expected to open to the public within months.

    

    The new facilities in the hospital's redevelopment include pathology, aged care, behavioural support, palliative care, MRI and nuclear medicine.

    

    The nine-storey Kensington Street Building capped out the project, housing community and outpatient services.

    

    Previous stages of the development delivered a new Emergency Department, an acute services building, upgraded birthing suite and operating theatre. The initial stages of development began more than a decade ago.

    

    Health Minister Ryan Park said he's looking forward to opening the Kensington Street Building which will "bring together a range of ambulatory, outpatient and community services to provide better health outcomes".

    

    "It is so important to provide our dedicated health staff with a modern, purpose-built health facility, which caters to the specific needs of patients, families, and clinicians," he said.

    

    "We are getting on with the job by delivering more hospitals, more beds and more services to ensure patients and their families receive the care they need, when they need it."

    

    In the new facilities, patients recovering from brain injuries will be able to access structured rehabilitation for the first time at the hospital.

    

    The St George Hospital redevelopment is one of 32 new and upgraded hospital projects underway by the government, including Rouse Hill Hospital, Bankstown Hospital and Blacktown and Mount Druitt Hospital.

Headline: Mental health failures behind Bondi Junction tragedy
Full Text:
As the coroner prepares to hand down findings on the death of six stabbing victims and the perpetrator at Bondi Junction's Westfield shopping centre in 2024, the spotlight will fall on our mental health system.

    The image of a young man in a footy shirt hurtling through a shopping centre on a perfectly ordinary Saturday will be forever seared into our collective national consciousness. A devastating loss of life. Nearly two years on, the Bondi Junction Inquest findings are being handed down on Thursday.

    

    This inquiry echoes the countless reviews that have come before, each documenting the decades of decline and fragmentation of our mental health care system.

    

    At the heart of what unfolded on our screens was a young man who was seriously unwell, but left in freefall without mental health supports for four years. That is four years of his parents in panic, four years of missed opportunities for intervention, four years where our mental health system went from bad to worse. More gut-wrenching is that, even after this tragedy, little has changed to turn the situation around

    

    The warning bells have been ringing for years. The number of mental health beds in Australia has essentially fallen off a cliff over the past 10 years. Today, we are at a historic low, with just 27 beds per 100,000 people. Comparable developed countries like Germany and Belgium have over four times that amount, with bed numbers in excess of 130 per 100,000 people. International consensus places the optimum number at 60 per 100,000 people, with 30 as the bare minimum. So, we are operating well below even that floor.

    

    At the same time, mental health presentations to emergency departments have reached record highs. Not only are more people presenting, but they are sicker when they arrive – only to encounter gridlock. One in 10 mental health patients now waits over 23 hours in emergency departments to access care. Consider the cruelty and harm of leaving someone in acute psychosis – terrified, disoriented, hearing voices – sitting on a hard plastic chair under fluorescent lights, amid noise and chaos, for an entire day.

    

    The stress on our hospital system continues to build because community mental health care has been hollowed out. These are the services and touchpoints which should keep people well and supported out of hospital before crisis strikes. These are the outreach teams that should have been there to help Joel Cauchi access help.

    

    Of course, the case of Cauchi represents an extreme outcome . People living with severe mental illness, including schizophrenia, are far more likely to harm themselves than others. They are far more likely to be victims of crime than to ever hurt another person. Yet, government data shows that there are 58,000 people in NSW alone living with severe and complex mental illness who cannot access care and are left to fend for themselves. So the freefall Cauchi experienced is far from rare.

    

    The situation is shameful. We live in one of the wealthiest countries on the planet, and yet we have deserted people with severe mental illness. Families are left to carry unimaginable pain while loved ones tumble down a preventable cascade into social isolation, homelessness, the criminal justice system and death. There is welcome recognition at the highest levels of government, with our federal Health Minister acknowledging this as "a stain on our nation" – but recognition must be matched with decisive action.

    

    A fundamental shift in approach is needed to reverse the downward spiral. For decades, mental health funding has been channelled into one-off, stand-alone initiatives promising simple solutions. While well intentioned, this piecemeal approach has rarely delivered lasting impact and has fuelled the false belief that the problem is unsolvable.

    

    The reality is that the whole house is in disrepair, and it needs a rebuild from the ground up. Decades of neglect have left us without proper structures across the whole spectrum of care – early interventions, community outreach, crisis care, inpatient facilities and recovery options. There is no single silver bullet, but there are so many opportunities to do better. Change won't happen overnight, but it can happen if we stop patching cracks and start rebuilding strong foundations.

    

    We need to start by surveying the landscape – mapping what services exist and where the gaps lie. From there, investment must be guided by evidence, scaling what works, redesigning what doesn't and stopping the cycle of funding by intuition. This is not a new idea. This data-driven approach has transformed cancer care, intensive care and surgical services, where clinical registries are used to guide improvement and keep investment accountable.

    

    Out of respect to the victims of this tragedy, if anything comes out of this inquiry it

    

    must be to compel us to act differently. Mental health care cannot be built on goodwill

    

    and guesswork. It is the safety net that Australians rely on when they are most

    

    vulnerable, and we cannot leave it in tatters.

    

    Dr Pramudie Gunaratne is a neuropsychiatrist and CEO of the Australian Society of Psychiatrists.
