Headline: The hidden struggle behind closed doors at a quiet Mosman Park home
Full Text:
Pictures of two smiling boys on their parents' social media accounts are hard to reconcile with the events that unfolded on Friday in their Mosman Park home.

    The online presences of Maiwenna Goasdoue, 49, and Jarrod Clune, 50, does not paint a picture of a family struggling or on the brink.

    

    Both parents' pages are littered with photos of their sons, Leon and Otis, going back years to when they were babies; smiling, enjoying the beach, cuddling their parents.

    

    It was a stark contrast to the scene first responders arrived to when they screamed down an otherwise quiet street early on Friday morning.

    

    A carer had found a note on the door urging them to phone police, while another note inside the home is believed to have led police to determine the tragedy was likely a double murder-suicide.

    

    An investigation into the tragedy remains ongoing, and a report is being prepared for the coroner.

    

    According to their friends, the Clunes were a close and loving family.

    

    "Mai was such a beautiful person," a friend of the family, who asked not to be named, told this masthead.

    

    "She loved those kids so much, her whole life revolved around them."

    

    The friend had connected with Goasdoue through Perth's autism community. They shared stories about what they faced as full-time carers of children with extreme behaviours, and offered each other support.

    

    "Like many, many families in their situation, you tend to run inwards after a while," the friend said.

    

    "You reach out to other people who are in similar situations. We talked about our respective challenges.

    

    "But the people who understand you the best are in the least position to give you support.

    

    "We're all in the trenches trying to survive. And that tends to lead to increasing isolation."

    

    Leon, 16, and Otis, 14, had severe autism. Otis was non-verbal. They were previously enrolled at the prestigious private Christ Church Grammar School, where fees per student are in the tens of thousands of dollars. It was the school their father also attended, graduating in 1992.

    

    Christ Church principal Alan Jones released a statement to parents saying the boys were both enrolled in the school's dedicated special needs department, the Peter Moyes Centre. Enrolment in the centre is highly sought-after and places are limited.

    

    But the boys left Christ Church in recent years, reportedly after one brother was asked to leave, although the school would not confirm that.

    

    "There's no doubt that their behaviour would have been extremely challenging," the family friend said.

    

    "You're talking about violence, meltdowns, vicious behaviour that could leave you with injuries.

    

    "And these kids with these extreme behaviours are let down the most.

    

    "There really isn't support for families like ours. There is nothing meaningful.

    

    "There's carers, but the hurdles you have to jump through to get it is impossible to navigate. What you need most is respite, but they won't take complex children.

    

    "Where does that leave you?"

    

    The boys were understood to later be enrolled in a specialist special needs school, but the family friend said many people in their situation turned to homeschooling as a last resort, as even designated schools were unable to cope with extreme behaviour including violence and aggression.

    

    Goasdoue, who is originally from a small western French town called Morlaix, appeared to have no family in Australia.

    

    While it has been reported the family's NDIS funding had recently been cut, Goasdoue's friend did not believe that was the case.

    

    However, she said, "it's not about the funding".

    

    "I need a break – I can't get a break – it's not about the lack of funding, it's the support that is not there," she said.

    

    "Not just anyone can cope with a child with complex, extreme and serious behaviour problems. The carers out there that are good at what they do, they are in high demand, they're not available.

    

    "Getting the NDIS is just the start, and then managing it all is a part-time job, and you have to be accountable to them for everything you do.

    

    "I'm grateful for it, but it's exhausting to navigate. The system is groaning and under a huge demand and lack of resources."

    

    Goasdoue was a stay-at-home mum to the boys, which her friend said was "absolutely necessary" for families like theirs, but would have taken a huge toll.

    

    The friend believed trouble finding – and trusting – somewhere to take their children could have contributed Friday's tragedy.

    

    "If your children have severe problems it's terrifying to put them in with strangers," she said.

    

    "And you know you can handle them better than anyone else, you have learnt to over time, so just keep them with you all of the time.

    

    "Then it comes full circle back around to when you care about your children so much you work yourself into the ground doing everything for them yourself, and you just end up completely burnt out."

    

    Goasdoue's friend said she had reached out with some suggestions for hiking adventures and support services, she knew their problems ran far deeper.

    

    On Tuesday, federal Disability Minister Mark Butler described the suspected double murder-suicide as an "unspeakable tragedy".

    

    However, legal restrictions prevented the minister from commenting on the NDIS packages the children were on.

    

    "All I want to say is that this is just an awful tragedy, just an unspeakable tragedy for this family, but for the broader community, and I know the [National Disability Insurance Agency] will do everything it can to assist the police in its inquiries," Butler said.

story_title: Perth Family Tragedy Sparks NDIS Support Criticism
story_summary: The suspected murder-suicide of the Goasdoue-Clune family in Mosman Park, Perth, has triggered intense criticism of the National Disability Insurance Scheme (NDIS). Parents Maiwenna Goasdoue and Jarrod Clune, and their two teenage sons, Leon and Otis, who had severe autism, were found deceased in their home. Friends, support workers, and disability advocacy groups like Developmental Disability WA claim the family was 'failed' by the system, facing immense challenges, isolation, and a lack of meaningful support for managing the sons' extreme behaviours. Headline: Financial toll hangs heavy over cancer patients
Full Text:
Jacinta Andrews knows all too well that the true cost of cancer extends far beyond medical bills or day treatment.

    Her husband has been battling multiple forms of cancer for decades, and although he is in remission the family continue to navigate the ongoing effects of his treatments, with some procedures costing thousands.

    

    While the public healthcare system covers a range of care, many patients still face the financial burden of cancer, research has found.

    

    Cancer Council Australia's national survey published on Wednesday found that nearly 99 per cent of Australians diagnosed with cancer incur out-of-pocket expenses, even when accessing public care.

    

    Tony Andrews was first diagnosed with head and neck cancer in 2009, forcing Ms Andrews to act as a full-time carer, assisting with daily transport, medical treatments and emotional wellbeing.

    

    While Ms Andrews was able to take paid time off, her husband was self-employed, and wasn't eligible for assistance.

    

    She considers herself lucky though that she could take leave, adding that they wouldn't have been able to pay their mortgage if it wasn't for her supportive workplace.

    

    The family were rocked by cancer again in 2023, after Mr Andrews was diagnosed with non-Hodgkin's lymphoma, which they are still navigating.

    

    Although Mr Andrews remains in remission, the side effects of his treatment have proved costly.

    

    "There are issues that continue and the latest ongoing thing since 2010, is the severity of the radiation treatment had on his gums and teeth," she told AAP.

    

    "We have had a lot of dental costs, and we can't afford private health insurance."

    

    Ms Andrews said the side effects on his teeth was unexpected, with her husband now struggling to eat.

    

    "We are saving up for him to get dentures or plates," she said.

    

    "We didn't know this was going to happen and you don't think about that stuff at the time of treatment, because you are just trying to survive."

    

    Cancer Council analysis found that loss of income or disruption to employment is the most common financial cost, affecting more than a third of Australians diagnosed with cancer.

    

    Surgical expenses (23.7 per cent ) and insurance gap payments (23.6 per cent) represented the largest out-of-pocket cost for nearly one quarter of respondents.

    

    "It is a huge burden for people," Cancer Council's Peter Diamond told AAP.

    

    "These costs can include both direct medical costs and indirect expenses, which people experience regardless of what stage (of) cancer diagnosis."

    

    Australians should be able to access high-quality cancer care, based on what best meets their needs, not what they can afford, Dr Diamond added.

    

    The research coincides with World Cancer Day and calls for increased access to financial counselling services for Australians affected by cancer through a mandatory industry-funded model, alongside greater investment from federal, state and territory governments.

    

    Australian Associated Press

Headline: Children won't need autism diagnosis to qualify for new support program
Full Text:
Parents will no longer have to spend thousands of dollars chasing autism diagnoses for their struggling children to qualify for government disability support under the new Thriving Kids program, which will help children with lower support needs outside the NDIS. Source:

    The Age

    

    After federal and state governments last week signed a long-awaited health and disability funding deal, Health and Disability Minister Mark Butler yesterday unveiled the model for Thriving Kids and revealed it would mainly be run through states and territories.

    

    The Albanese Government rebuffed a suggestion from its advisory group that children access therapies through new Medicare plans, and will instead give states money to run the scheme their own way. "We won't be funding services directly. We'll be providing that money to states," Mr Butler said.

    

    "[They] will largely leverage off a lot of programs they already run … It won't be a traditional Medicare-funded allied health professional visit, that might be bulk billed or might attract a gap fee. This will be quite a different model that state governments, particularly, asked us for."

    

    Each jurisdiction will devise its own system for supporting tens of thousands of children, aged eight and under, who have autism or developmental delays – many of whom currently have individual packages through the $48 billion NDIS.

    

    State systems will be expected to adhere to the same model, which has four key functions: identifying when children have developmental delays or differences; providing easy access to information about where to get help; supplying programs and tools for parents to build their own skills; and providing targeted support, including therapies and equipment, for children who need it.

    

    The federal government will support Thriving Kids with national components – such as information campaigns, online resources and a call line – but it will otherwise be delivered by states and territories, which will scale up their existing services as well as establish new ones.

    

    Yesterday's release of the Thriving Kids model comes after Mr Butler's taskforce took over the design last August because the states and territories were too slow, after first agreeing to help run new services for kids in late 2023.

    

    Autism diagnoses have soared in Australia in the last decade – a trend some experts attribute to the NDIS requirements.

    

    Thriving Kids will start rolling out from October this year and launch fully by January 2028.

    

    FULL STORY

story_title: Government Unveils 'Thriving Kids' NDIS Alternative Program
story_summary: The Albanese government has released the model for its $4 billion 'Thriving Kids' program, a major reform designed to shift children aged eight and under with developmental delays or mild autism from the National Disability Insurance Scheme (NDIS) to state-led support systems. Championed by Health Minister Mark Butler, the program aims to provide community-based support and easier access to early intervention without requiring a formal medical diagnosis, in an effort to make the NDIS more sustainable. While the move has been welcomed by some stakeholders, disability groups, parent advocates, and the Australian Education Union have called for more operational details and raised concerns about implementation, parental control, and potential increased workloads for teachers. The program is set for a phased rollout starting in late 2024, with full implementation by 2028.

Full Text:
Interview
For Bulletin at nine.
Thanks for that, Ellie. Ward in charge of the news this morning. you questions, tas Water, please. They wanted a 40% increase. Over the next four years, they wanted to invest in record spending and infrastructure. The regulator said that's too much. How do you see it? Would you be prepared to pay more? What would a 40% increase to your water bill have have meant for you? Get in touch. Let me know. We'll talk about that this morning and we'll talk to you, the regulator, to join us in just a minute. First, a quick squeeze at your traffic. Look, there are some delays around the network statewide that we can see this morning. Tasman, quite heavy inbound. Outbound... Inbound, sorry, from Mornington, congested. Things do improve once you cross to the western shore. Southern inlet, bound is a little slow. Let's see, what else have we got? Broken down truck in the left-hand lane leading to some traffic. That's Davie Street this morning. Launceston, Devonport, Burnie, things looking pretty good this this morning. That's what we can see. If you can see something out there, let me know. 936, ABC Radio Hobart and ABC Northern Tasmania. Hope your day's going well. School back for you. State system back on Friday. Slowly but surely, the private sector dribbling back over the course of the week. What are you seeing out there? How are you feeling? Get in touch. Tell me about it. 1300 222 936. My question for you this morning, are the excuses now over? What's the significance of this deal signed on Friday between the state and the federal governments? rather the federal government and the states, plural. The feds will now contribute hundreds of millions of dollars in additional fund into the Tasmanian healthcare system to pay for the hospital system in the years ahead. So what does it mean? Are the waiting lists to see a specialist going to fall? To get surgery? To get admitted through emergency? To get an ambulance? Like, do all those numbers now now. Are the problems that have seemed near unfixable, are they over? We talked to the president of the AMA about it yesterday, Michael Lumsden-Steele, talking with us yesterday morning about the scale of the challenges.
Look, I think this will help, Leon, but it's not going to help immediately. And look, I think the critical thing here is we've arisen to a point point where just about every metric we meet in our health system and the KPIs we have, we are not meeting. We cannot suddenly meet these KPIs tomorrow or in the next two months, week, three months, six does it happen over the term of the deal? Do the excuses end? Is it enough money for that? Is there enough will, skill to make that happen? Bridget Archer is Tasmania's Health Minister. good morning to you. Morning, Leon. Is this enough money for the excuses to end? You've looked after the health system for 11 years as a government. The things that seem to be getting worse and not better now improve under this deal?
Yeah, well, it is certainly a really good outcome and it's a significant improvement on the original offer that was made some months ago and an improvement that we fought for. So we are very pleased that has delivered more money for the Tasmanian health system. And we know that it already, the investments that we have been making in the health system in some of those areas that you talked about, in elective surgery outpatient transformation have made a difference we do continue to see rising demand and that is the challenge that we continue to try to meet and some of those things actually have to happen outside the hospital as well so this funding is really welcomed in terms of continuing to do all of those things that you talked about that happen inside our hospital. And we'll also continue to invest in the things that we know will make a difference outside the hospital in terms of preventing people having to present to the hospital, whether that's in mental health emergency responses or hospital in the home or a whole range of those things, and including continuing to invest in primary care in GP clinics, which we've got our expression of interest have just closed for the Tassie Docs clinics, for example, and even pharmacy scope of practise All of those things are making a contribution to the health of Tasmanians outside the hospital system.
Sure, Minister. As Dr Michael Lumsden-Steele says, at almost every metric you look at, things are worse and not better. Is this enough money to actually fix those issues in ramping with bed block, elective surgery and so on?
Well, it will certainly continue to go towards addressing those issues, but we do have to deal with the issue of demand as well. So that is the continual issue that we face is demand on our hospital system. And that is why we have seen, although we've invested and we're into our second four-year elective surgery plan, we have performed more elective surgeries. We've performed record numbers of elective surgeries, but we're still seeing high demand for those services as well. So this will very much help in relation that. It is a terrific deal and we really welcome it. And we will continue to invest in those things and also in things outside of the hospital.
But with respect, increased demand is an understood issue. We have an ageing population. Demographers can clearly identify that. We know that we are a sicker population compared to the national average. When is this enough money to actually get on top of those known issues and make the numbers on elective surgery waiting go down significantly over the course of this deal?
Well, that is certainly the intention of this funding is to go into those hospital services and continue deliver those hospital services. But if we want to see demand decrease over time, and that's the point that Michael Lumsden-Steele was making about over time and demand over time, you have to start filling the hole in the bottom of the bucket as well. you know that's why we're investing in a 20-year preventative health strategy as well a whole of government response a transformation in the way that we look at prevention in Tasmania as well because we want people over time we want people into the future to not have to be presenting at our hospitals with some of those chronic conditions that we are seeing the demand for now. So it has to be both those things. We have to continue to meet the acute demand that presents to our hospitals and that's why this funding is really, welcomed and this additional funding is really welcomed because we can invest more money to do that and to continue with those plans that we have already put in place. But we have to also do those things that happen outside the hospital. And we can't understate how important those are in terms of addressing that future demand and reducing that future demand for acute services.
Do we have a plan, and for people just tuning in, Bridget Archer's my guest this morning, Minister for Health, Mental Health, Wellbeing in Tasmania. Do we have a plan for replacing the Royal Hobart Hospital? Some eyebrows raised, AMA saying we need a plan, a Greenfield site, not now, but for 10 years time to build a new Royal. Does that plan exist?
Well, what we have is a 30 year master plan for the Royal Hobart Hospital, and that is what we have been investing in to date, some $520 million to date on a range of upgrades to the Royal Hobart Hospital and that work is ongoing. That's part of a 30-year master plan that also deals with future planning, that deals with future demand, that accounts for future demand. So we continue to invest in that and there's been a lot of developments already undertaken there. A new emergency, our first stage of the new emergency department and work will begin soon on the second stage of the emergency department. We've got new short stay beds.
We had an ER nurse text us while we were talking yesterday. The new emergency department, is it permanently planned to operate over three levels? Have you been convinced that is a good system to operate an emergency department at the state's major treating hospital?
Well, I have been visiting all of our hospitals in the time that I have been the Health Minister and talking to clinicians, talking to staff and talking to a whole range of people across all of our facilities. Headline: Financial toll hangs heavy over cancer patients
Full Text:
Jacinta Andrews knows all too well that the true cost of cancer extends far beyond medical bills or day treatment.

    Her husband has been battling multiple forms of cancer for decades, and although he is in remission the family continue to navigate the ongoing effects of his treatments, with some procedures costing thousands.

    

    While the public healthcare system covers a range of care, many patients still face the financial burden of cancer, research has found.

    

    Cancer Council Australia's national survey published on Wednesday found that nearly 99 per cent of Australians diagnosed with cancer incur out-of-pocket expenses, even when accessing public care.

    

    Tony Andrews was first diagnosed with head and neck cancer in 2009, forcing Ms Andrews to act as a full-time carer, assisting with daily transport, medical treatments and emotional wellbeing.

    

    While Ms Andrews was able to take paid time off, her husband was self-employed, and wasn't eligible for assistance.

    

    She considers herself lucky though that she could take leave, adding that they wouldn't have been able to pay their mortgage if it wasn't for her supportive workplace.

    

    The family were rocked by cancer again in 2023, after Mr Andrews was diagnosed with non-Hodgkin's lymphoma, which they are still navigating.

    

    Although Mr Andrews remains in remission, the side effects of his treatment have proved costly.

    

    "There are issues that continue and the latest ongoing thing since 2010, is the severity of the radiation treatment had on his gums and teeth," she told AAP.

    

    "We have had a lot of dental costs, and we can't afford private health insurance."

    

    Ms Andrews said the side effects on his teeth was unexpected, with her husband now struggling to eat.

    

    "We are saving up for him to get dentures or plates," she said.

    

    "We didn't know this was going to happen and you don't think about that stuff at the time of treatment, because you are just trying to survive."

    

    Cancer Council analysis found that loss of income or disruption to employment is the most common financial cost, affecting more than a third of Australians diagnosed with cancer.

    

    Surgical expenses (23.7 per cent ) and insurance gap payments (23.6 per cent) represented the largest out-of-pocket cost for nearly one quarter of respondents.

    

    Advertisement

    

    "It is a huge burden for people," Cancer Council's Peter Diamond told AAP.

    

    "These costs can include both direct medical costs and indirect expenses, which people experience regardless of what stage (of) cancer diagnosis."

    

    Australians should be able to access high-quality cancer care, based on what best meets their needs, not what they can afford, Dr Diamond added.

    

    The research coincides with World Cancer Day and calls for increased access to financial counselling services for Australians affected by cancer through a mandatory industry-funded model, alongside greater investment from federal, state and territory governments.

    

    Australian Associated Press

Headline: Need new hospital

Headline: PEP left in waiting room at Healthscope’s Prince of Wales Hospital
Full Text:
Pacific Equity Partners' dealmakers will have to wait a bit longer before they can take the keys to Randwick's Prince of Wales hospital, the 174-year-old institution that delivers babies and tends to the physical ailments of Sydney's well-heeled set.

   A vote for Healthscope's lenders to decide on PEP's bid for the hospital was delayed last week, sources told Street Talk. It was slated to open Wednesday evening via an online portal and decide the fate of PEP's bid by Friday.

   However, it has been pushed back to a yet-to-be decided date, as Healthscope boss Tino La Spina and receiver McGrathNicol work through "other options", these sources said. Presumably top of the list of these "other options" is 

   pushing for Healthscope to morph into a not-for-profit

   , which would kibosh the lenders' payroll tax obligations for the hospital operator and give it greater bargaining power on the rents with landlords. (Former owner Brookfield lost ownership of the group to lenders last year.)

   The delay comes after 

   The Australian Financial Review

    last month revealed Healthscope's management valued Prince of Wales Private Hospital 

   at more than $240 million

    – about double what PEP was offering.

   For all the noise around Healthscope, only five of its 36 hospitals have found new owners-operators after more than two years of discussions on its future spanning politicians, insurance bosses, and sharp-nosed distressed debt funds.

   The sold list includes Gold Coast Private Hospital, acquired by Queensland not-for-profit Mater Health; Canberra's National Capital Private Hospital acquired by ASX-listed Ramsay Health Care; and the Hobart Private Hospital and Melbourne's Holmesglen Private Hospital.

   Of the remaining 31, a dozen are owned by Canadian REIT NorthWest, which has tried to bring in Calvary as the operator for $140 million but faced rejections from the lenders.

   A further 11 are owned by David Di Pilla's HealthCo REIT which has, since last year, said it has conditional terms to replace Healthscope with new operators – but is yet to sign a deal.

Headline: Inside the new $411 million ‘purpose-built' St George Hospital redevelopment
Full Text:
A southern Sydney hospital's $411 million redevelopment is complete, with a new nine-storey building and upgraded campus set to open within months and provide enhanced services.

    Thomas Sargeant

    

    less than 2 min read

    

    February 4, 2026 - 4:00AM

    

    A major hospital upgrade in Sydney's south has been completed to the tune of $411 million after a year of major construction.

    

    The third stage in the redevelopment of St George Hospital is complete, with its doors expected to open to the public within months.

    

    The new facilities in the hospital's redevelopment include pathology, aged care, behavioural support, palliative care, MRI and nuclear medicine.

    

    The nine-storey Kensington Street Building capped out the project, housing community and outpatient services.

    

    Previous stages of the development delivered a new Emergency Department, an acute services building, upgraded birthing suite and operating theatre. The initial stages of development began more than a decade ago.

    

    Health Minister Ryan Park said he's looking forward to opening the Kensington Street Building which will "bring together a range of ambulatory, outpatient and community services to provide better health outcomes".

    

    "It is so important to provide our dedicated health staff with a modern, purpose-built health facility, which caters to the specific needs of patients, families, and clinicians," he said.

    

    "We are getting on with the job by delivering more hospitals, more beds and more services to ensure patients and their families receive the care they need, when they need it."

    

    In the new facilities, patients recovering from brain injuries will be able to access structured rehabilitation for the first time at the hospital.

    

    The St George Hospital redevelopment is one of 32 new and upgraded hospital projects underway by the government, including Rouse Hill Hospital, Bankstown Hospital and Blacktown and Mount Druitt Hospital.

Headline: Worried Aussies put retirement on hold
Full Text:
Workers are delaying their retirement by up to six years as cost-of-living pressures force a grim outcome on the national workforce.

    Cost of living is still the biggest concern for workers' retirement planning, Equip Superannuation data based on 2000 Australians' responses has found.

    Forty-three per cent of workers say the high cost of living is their biggest retirement concern, while 25 per cent point to a lack of super savings and 18 per cent flagged healthcare affordability.

    Only one in five Australians are feeling more optimistic about their retirement compared with this time last year.

    Equip Super chief experience officer Carrie Norman told NewsWire older Australians were feeling the punch.

    She said Australians approaching their 50s or already in their 50s were the largest number of people delaying retirement.

Headline: Reserve Bank warns inflation to surge above 4 per cent despite rate rise
Full Text:
The nation's inflation bin fire will burn Aussie families for at least the next year after an interest rate rise on Tuesday. Treasurer Jim Chalmers tried to claim historically high government spending was not the culprit. 

    Shock new forecasts from the Reserve Bank of Australia indicate inflation will again surge above 4 per cent – even with a second rate hike later this year.

    

    A lift in household spending and business investment from previously low levels has combined with near-record government outlays to overheat an economy that can no longer grow at more than 2 per cent without prices spiralling upwards.

    

    Yesterday, the RBA monetary policy board raised borrowing costs by 0.25 per cent for the first time since 2023 to cool "private demand" as governor Michele Bullock repeatedly refused to offer any view on whether public-sector demand was also adding fuel to the inflation bin-fire.

    

    "I'm not going to tell the government what to do with fiscal policy. That's not my business," Ms Bullock said, even though other leading economists and business groups have lined up to argue fiscal policy is overstimulating the economy.

    

    "We are independent, they (the government) are independent, and we work with what we've got."

    

    The RBA's independence did not stop Treasurer Jim Chalmers from tearing into the RBA in 2024, when he said rate rises were "smashing the economy".

    

    Ms Bullock yesterday insisted the Albanese government had not told her to avoid criticising its spending.

    

    "No one has made that comment to me and the board obviously makes its decisions independently of the government," she said.

    

    In conjunction with announcing the rate increase, the RBA released a new set of forecasts which show it now expects the headline rate of consumer price index (CPI) to hit 4.2 per cent at the end of this financial year.

    

    In November, it had said the CPI would be 3.7 per cent on June 30. In August, it had predicted prices would be rising by just 3.1 per cent.

    

    The RBA also ratcheted up the projection for its preferred measure of inflation, called the "trimmed mean", to 3.7 per cent in June instead of 3.2 per cent. The central bank's goal is to get the trimmed mean down to 2.5 per cent.

    

    "The board now thinks it will take longer for inflation to return to target and this is not an acceptable outcome," Ms Bullock said.

    

    "I know this is not the news that Australians with mortgages want to hear ... (but) we cannot allow inflation to get away from us again."

    

    CBA was the first major lender to announce it was passing on the increase, from February 13, adding $80 a month to repayments on a $500,000 loan. Westpac then mirrored CommBank's move.

    

    The RBA's economic forecasts are built on a set of assumptions that include what money markets indicate will happen to the benchmark cash rate, which is set for another 25 basis point increase this year.

    

    Westpac chief economist Luci Ellis, who was the RBA's assistant governor for economics until 2023, last night said: "Absent a downside surprise in the March quarter inflation outcome, the monetary policy board is likely to hike again in May."

    

    Accountancy firm William Buck's chief economist Besa Deda went even further.

    

    "We also do not fully rule out the possibility of three rate hikes materialising in 2026 given the size of the upward revisions to inflation and the RBA's language," Ms Deda said.

    

    Following each of the three rate cuts last year, Treasurer Chalmers was quick to hold press conferences trumpeting the Albanese government's fiscal management.

    

    However, he did not front the media yesterday after the RBA's decision to hike rates.

    

    Dr Chalmers did tell parliament that price "pressure is coming from private demand, not public demand".

    

    Earlier in the day, he also told Sky News the "big driver" in the most recent CPI was holiday spending and said claims that government spending was making the RBA's job tougher were often more "politically motivated" than founded in facts.

    

    Last week, the Treasurer took AMP chief economist Shane Oliver to task.

    

    Yesterday, Dr Oliver told Sky News: "I'm not saying that government spending has boomed in the last six months. It's really the fact that it's boomed over the last six or seven years, taking public spending to levels that we've never, never been used to."

    

    Deloitte Access Economics partner Stephen Smith said it was not "controversial" to say "historically high" government spending was a "genuine driver of inflation".

    

    The Australian Industry Group's CEO Innes Willox suggested the Treasurer should also reflect on his own decisions. "The federal government can do its part by better controlling the recent high growth in public spending," he said.

    

    The Australian Chamber of Commerce and Industry said the government should cut more than $50bn a year from the budget by slashing spending on the NDIS, childcare, aged care, health and defence.

    

    Originally published as Reserve Bank warns inflation to surge above 4 per cent despite rate rise

Headline: Reserve Bank warns inflation to surge above 4 per cent despite rate rise
Full Text:
The nation's inflation bin fire will burn Aussie families for at least the next year after an interest rate rise on Tuesday. Treasurer Jim Chalmers tried to claim historically high government spending was not the culprit.

    Shock new forecasts from the Reserve Bank of Australia indicate inflation will again surge above 4 per cent – even with a second rate hike later this year.

    

    A lift in household spending and business investment from previously low levels has combined with near-record government outlays to overheat an economy that can no longer grow at more than 2 per cent without prices spiralling upwards.

    

    Yesterday, the RBA monetary policy board raised borrowing costs by 0.25 per cent for the first time since 2023 to cool "private demand" as governor Michele Bullock repeatedly refused to offer any view on whether public-sector demand was also adding fuel to the inflation bin-fire.

    

    'Smashing the economy': Jim Chalmers blasted after interest rates rise again

    

    Sky News host Andrew Bolt blasts Treasurer Jim Chalmers for "smashing the economy" following the RBA's decision to lift the cash rate to 3.85 per cent from 3.6 per cent.

    

    'Smashing the economy': Jim Chalmers blasted after interest rates rise again

    

    Sky News host Andrew Bolt blasts Treasurer Jim Chalmers for "smashing the economy" following the...

    

    "I'm not going to tell the government what to do with fiscal policy. That's not my business," Ms Bullock said, even though other leading economists and business groups have lined up to argue fiscal policy is overstimulating the economy.

    

    "We are independent, they (the government) are independent, and we work with what we've got."

    

    The RBA's independence did not stop Treasurer Jim Chalmers from tearing into the RBA in 2024, when he said rate rises were "smashing the economy".

    

    Ms Bullock yesterday insisted the Albanese government had not told her to avoid criticising its spending.

    

    "No one has made that comment to me and the board obviously makes its decisions independently of the government," she said.

    

    In conjunction with announcing the rate increase, the RBA released a new set of forecasts which show it now expects the headline rate of consumer price index (CPI) to hit 4.2 per cent at the end of this financial year.

    

    In November, it had said the CPI would be 3.7 per cent on June 30. In August, it had predicted prices would be rising by just 3.1 per cent.

    

    The RBA also ratcheted up the projection for its preferred measure of inflation, called the "trimmed mean", to 3.7 per cent in June instead of 3.2 per cent. The central bank's goal is to get the trimmed mean down to 2.5 per cent.

    

    "The board now thinks it will take longer for inflation to return to target and this is not an acceptable outcome," Ms Bullock said.

    

    "I know this is not the news that Australians with mortgages want to hear ... (but) we cannot allow inflation to get away from us again."

    

    CBA was the first major lender to announce it was passing on the increase, from February 13, adding $80 a month to repayments on a $500,000 loan. Westpac then mirrored CommBank's move.

    

    The RBA's economic forecasts are built on a set of assumptions that include what money markets indicate will happen to the benchmark cash rate, which is set for another 25 basis point increase this year.

    

    Westpac chief economist Luci Ellis, who was the RBA's assistant governor for economics until 2023, last night said: "Absent a downside surprise in the March quarter inflation outcome, the monetary policy board is likely to hike again in May."

    

    Accountancy firm William Buck's chief economist Besa Deda went even further.

    

    "We also do not fully rule out the possibility of three rate hikes materialising in 2026 given the size of the upward revisions to inflation and the RBA's language," Ms Deda said.

    

    Following each of the three rate cuts last year, Treasurer Chalmers was quick to hold press conferences trumpeting the Albanese government's fiscal management.

    

    However, he did not front the media yesterday after the RBA's decision to hike rates.

    

    Dr Chalmers did tell parliament that price "pressure is coming from private demand, not public demand".

    

    Earlier in the day, he also told Sky News the "big driver" in the most recent CPI was holiday spending and said claims that government spending was making the RBA's job tougher were often more "politically motivated" than founded in facts.

    

    Last week, the Treasurer took AMP chief economist Shane Oliver to task.

    

    Yesterday, Dr Oliver told Sky News: "I'm not saying that government spending has boomed in the last six months. It's really the fact that it's boomed over the last six or seven years, taking public spending to levels that we've never, never been used to."

    

    Deloitte Access Economics partner Stephen Smith said it was not "controversial" to say "historically high" government spending was a "genuine driver of inflation".

    

    The Australian Industry Group's CEO Innes Willox suggested the Treasurer should also reflect on his own decisions. "The federal government can do its part by better controlling the recent high growth in public spending," he said.

    

    The Australian Chamber of Commerce and Industry said the government should cut more than $50bn a year from the budget by slashing spending on the NDIS, childcare, aged care, health and defence.

    

    Originally published as Reserve Bank warns inflation to surge above 4 per cent despite rate rise

Headline: Inside the new $411 million ‘purpose-built' St George Hospital redevelopment
Full Text:
A southern Sydney hospital's $411 million redevelopment is complete, with a new nine-storey building and upgraded campus set to open within months and provide enhanced services.

    A major hospital upgrade in Sydney's south has been completed to the tune of $411 million after a year of major construction.

    

    The third stage in the redevelopment of St George Hospital is complete, with its doors expected to open to the public within months.

    

    The new facilities in the hospital's redevelopment include pathology, aged care, behavioural support, palliative care, MRI and nuclear medicine.

    

    The nine-storey Kensington Street Building capped out the project, housing community and outpatient services.

    

    Previous stages of the development delivered a new Emergency Department, an acute services building, upgraded birthing suite and operating theatre. The initial stages of development began more than a decade ago.

    

    Health Minister Ryan Park said he's looking forward to opening the Kensington Street Building which will "bring together a range of ambulatory, outpatient and community services to provide better health outcomes".

    

    "It is so important to provide our dedicated health staff with a modern, purpose-built health facility, which caters to the specific needs of patients, families, and clinicians," he said.

    

    "We are getting on with the job by delivering more hospitals, more beds and more services to ensure patients and their families receive the care they need, when they need it."

    

    In the new facilities, patients recovering from brain injuries will be able to access structured rehabilitation for the first time at the hospital.

    

    The St George Hospital redevelopment is one of 32 new and upgraded hospital projects underway by the government, including Rouse Hill Hospital, Bankstown Hospital and Blacktown and Mount Druitt Hospital.

    

    Originally published as Inside the new $411 million 'purpose-built' St George Hospital redevelopment

Headline: Financial toll hangs heavy over cancer patients
Full Text:
Jacinta Andrews knows all too well that the true cost of cancer extends far beyond medical bills or day treatment.

    Her husband has been battling multiple forms of cancer for decades, and although he is in remission the family continue to navigate the ongoing effects of his treatments, with some procedures costing thousands.

    

    While the public healthcare system covers a range of care, many patients still face the financial burden of cancer, research has found.

    

    Cancer Council Australia's national survey published on Wednesday found that nearly 99 per cent of Australians diagnosed with cancer incur out-of-pocket expenses, even when accessing public care.

    

    Tony Andrews was first diagnosed with head and neck cancer in 2009, forcing Ms Andrews to act as a full-time carer, assisting with daily transport, medical treatments and emotional wellbeing.

    

    While Ms Andrews was able to take paid time off, her husband was self-employed, and wasn't eligible for assistance.

    

    She considers herself lucky though that she could take leave, adding that they wouldn't have been able to pay their mortgage if it wasn't for her supportive workplace.

    

    The family were rocked by cancer again in 2023, after Mr Andrews was diagnosed with non-Hodgkin's lymphoma, which they are still navigating.

    

    Although Mr Andrews remains in remission, the side effects of his treatment have proved costly.

    

    "There are issues that continue and the latest ongoing thing since 2010, is the severity of the radiation treatment had on his gums and teeth," she told AAP.

    

    "We have had a lot of dental costs, and we can't afford private health insurance."

    

    Ms Andrews said the side effects on his teeth was unexpected, with her husband now struggling to eat.

    

    "We are saving up for him to get dentures or plates," she said.

    

    "We didn't know this was going to happen and you don't think about that stuff at the time of treatment, because you are just trying to survive."

    

    Cancer Council analysis found that loss of income or disruption to employment is the most common financial cost, affecting more than a third of Australians diagnosed with cancer.

    

    Surgical expenses (23.7 per cent ) and insurance gap payments (23.6 per cent) represented the largest out-of-pocket cost for nearly one quarter of respondents.

    

    Advertisement

    

    "It is a huge burden for people," Cancer Council's Peter Diamond told AAP.

    

    "These costs can include both direct medical costs and indirect expenses, which people experience regardless of what stage (of) cancer diagnosis."

    

    Australians should be able to access high-quality cancer care, based on what best meets their needs, not what they can afford, Dr Diamond added.

    

    The research coincides with World Cancer Day and calls for increased access to financial counselling services for Australians affected by cancer through a mandatory industry-funded model, alongside greater investment from federal, state and territory governments.

    

    Australian Associated Press

Headline: ‘We're all in the trenches': Friend reveals private struggle of Mosman Park family
Full Text:
A friend has shared the private struggles of the Perth family found dead in a suspected murder-suicide, amid criticisms they were "failed" by the NDIS.

    Four dead in suspected double murder-suicide

    

    Four people were found dead in a residence in Mosman Park Perth. The four individuals included a...

    

    A friend has shared the hidden struggles of the Perth family found dead in a suspected murder-suicide, describing them as "in the trenches trying to survive".

    

    Maiwenna 'Mai' Goasdoue, 49, and Jarrod Clune, 50, and their two teenage sons, Leon, 16, and Otis, 14, were found dead inside their Mosman Park home on Friday.

    

    Both boys had severe autism – Otis was non-verbal – and were previously enrolled at the Peter Moyes Centre, the highly sought-after special needs centre at the prestigious Christ Church Grammar School. The brothers stopped attending the school in recent years, reportedly after one of them was asked to leave.

    

    A carer for Leon and Otis alerted police to the tragedy early on Friday morning, after finding a note on the home's front door urging them to call authorities.

    

    It's understood that a second note found inside – which reportedly indicated the parents decided together to end their lives – led police to determine it a murder-suicide.

Headline: 'Win for patients and staff': Calvary Mater to face parliamentary inquiry after scandals
Full Text:
THE Calvary Mater Hospital will be the centre of a parliamentary inquiry following a string of high-profile scandals that ranged from mould to maggots.

    The inquiry was put forward by the Coalition on Wednesday, who pushed it through the NSW parliament with the support of the Greens despite Labor voting against it.

    

    The wide-ranging investigation will look at management, maintenance and operational issues at the hospital, including a specific focus on the "causes and management of incidents involving mould, water damage, pest infestations".

    

    The hospital has closed multiple wards in 2026, including shutting the intensive care unit over the weekend , due to deadly mould in the air-conditioning vents and maggots that fell from the ceiling onto patients.

    

    The inquiry will be conducted by the NSW Legislative Council Health Committee, which is expected to hold hearings in Newcastle.

    

    Opposition health spokeswoman Sarah Mitchell, who put forward the inquiry as a private member's bill, said the government had to learn what had happened at the Mater to restore the community's faith in the hospital.

    

    "This is a win for patients, staff and the wider community," Ms Mitchell said.

    

    "The establishment of a parliamentary inquiry means the serious issues at the Mater will finally be properly investigated, transparently and without spin."

    

    The inquiry will also look at "the effectiveness of the public-private partnership (PPP) model at the Calvary Mater".

    

    Greens health spokeswoman and committee chair Amanda Cohn said the hospital had serious issues that had been dealt with for months, and the reason why was clear.

    

    "It's the PPP," Dr Cohn said.

    

    "The Minns government has a strong stance on PPPs in healthcare. Unfortunately they are not walking the talk on this hospital.

    

    "I hope they walk the talk and end this PPP, but in the meantime this inquiry is a really important piece of work that can help unpick this mess in terms of what went wrong."

    

    Labor Minister for Finance Courtney Houssos said the government "does not support this inquiry, but not because we don't take this seriously".

    

    "It's really important that we fix the problems first, then we get to the bottom of what happened," she said.

    

    Ms Houssos also suggested answers about the Mater's ongoing issues could be sought by asking bureaucrats during budget estimates.

    

    Ms Mitchell and Dr Cohn raised concerns that in the most recent estimates, NSW Health Infrastructure staff told politicians the mould "risk on the ground is minimal" just two weeks before the hospital closed a ward due to dangerous mould levels.

    

    NSW Health Minister Ryan Park met with Calvary Mater staff in Newcastle and announced the government would intervene to fix the building's cladding, which has been leaking so badly chemotherapy treatments were cancelled

    

    It's understood rectification work to remove mould is also underway, but may take several months.

    

    The Calvary Mater is also at the centre of a class action being prepared by two law firms , calling for people who lost loved ones in the Calvary Mater Hospital's cancer wards between January 2024 and April 2025 to come forward.

    

    The legal action was launched after the Newcastle Herald revealed the private operator responsible for the hospital's maintenance, Honeywell, was informed in October 2024 that the air-conditioning ducts in its cancer ward had to be replaced as they were riddled with dangerous Aspergillus mould.

    

    Staff and patients were unaware of the issue for six months until the information was leaked in April last year.

    

    The mould has also been linked to pneumonia, with a 2021 paper stating " Aspergillus species are the typical etiologic cause of mould pneumonia".

    

    A recently released Bureau of Health Information study found the Calvary Mater had "higher than expected mortality" for pneumonia, despite a statewide decrease.

    Opposition health spokeswoman Sarah Mitchell, who put forward the inquiry as a private member's bill, said the government had to learn what happened at the Mater to restore the community's faith in the hospital.

    

    "This is a win for patients, staff and the wider community," Ms Mitchell said.

    

    "The establishment of a parliamentary inquiry means the serious issues at the Mater will finally be properly investigated, transparently and without spin."

    

    The inquiry will also look at "the effectiveness of the public-private partnership (PPP) model at the Calvary Mater".

    

    Greens health spokeswoman and committee chair Amanda Cohn said the hospital had serious issues that had been dealt with for months, and the reason why was clear.

    

    "It's the PPP," Dr Cohn said.

    

    "The Minns government has a strong stance on PPPs in healthcare. Unfortunately they are not walking the talk on this hospital.

    

    "I hope they walk the talk and end this PPP, but in the meantime this inquiry is a really important piece of work that can help unpick this mess in terms of what went wrong."

    

    Labor minister Courtney Husso said the government "does not support this inquiry, but not because we don't take this seriously".

    

    "It's really important that we fix the problems first, then we get to the bottom of what happened," she said.

    

    Ms Husso also suggested answers about the Mater's ongoing issues could be sought by asking bureaucrats during budget estimates.

    

    Ms Mitchell and Dr Cohn raised concerns that in the most recent estimates, NSW Health Infrastructure staff told politicians the mould "risk on the ground is minimal", just two weeks before the hospital closed a ward due to dangerous mould levels.

    

    Last week, NSW Health Minister Ryan Park met with Calvary Mater staff in Newcastle and announced the government would intervene to fix the building's cladding, which has been leaking so badly chemotherapy treatments were cancelled

    

    It's understood rectification work to mould is also underway, but may take several months.

    

    The Calvary Mater is also the centre of a class action being prepared by two law firms , calling for people who lost loved ones in the Calvary Mater Hospital's cancer wards between January 2024 and April 2025 to come forward.

    

    The legal action was launched, after the Newcastle Herald revealed the private operator responsible for the hospital's maintenance, Honeywell, was informed in October 2024 that the air-conditioning ducts in its cancer ward had to be replaced after they were found riddled with dangerous Aspergillus mould.

    

    However, staff and patients were unaware of the issue for six months, when the information was leaked in April last year.

    

    The mould has also been linked to pneumonia, with a 2021 paper stating " Aspergillus species are the typical etiologic cause of mould pneumonia".

    

    A recently released Bureau of Health Information study found the Calvary Mater had "higher than expected mortality" for pneumonia, despite a statewide decrease.

Headline: 'Lot of complaints': ex-minister's aged care warning
Full Text:
Older Australians are dealing with big price increases and longer waiting lists for care under major reforms to the aged care sector, a former Liberal minister warns.

    Christopher Pyne, who spent 26 years in federal parliament, has also warned against stereotyping older people as either wealthy baby boomers or frail retirees, describing both framings as "crude caricatures".

    

    Mr Pyne, now the chairman of advocacy group Council on the Ageing, said it would take time to iron out the kinks in the government's new aged care act.

    

    Advertisement

    

    The overhaul of the aged care sector took effect in November 2025, requiring wealthier retirees to pay more for their care and restructuring the way services are funded.

    

    Addressing the National Press Club to launch the council's State of the Older Nation report, the former cabinet minister said while it had only been a few months since the reforms kicked in, people in care had raised a number of complaints.

    

    They included longer waits for home care packages, pressure to sign care agreements quickly, and sharp price rises.

    

    "We expect the government will react to those over time," Mr Pyne said.

    

    He said older people were also being forced to pay for assistance showering.

    

    "Because that's such a big thing, of course, it gets a lot of coverage and a lot of complaints," Mr Pyne said.

    

    The State of the Older Nation report, released by Mr Pyne on Wednesday, includes alarming figures about the number of people experiencing age-based discrimination.

    

    Advertisement

    

    It found 38 per cent of people had experienced ageism since turning 50 and 36 per cent said they didn't want to return to the workforce because of ageism.

    

    "Too often, the story of older Australians is reduced to one of two basic plot lines," Mr Pyne said.

    

    "The first goes like this: older people - often framed as baby boomers - are wealthy, secure, asset rich, sitting on housing, insulated from economic pressure and blocking opportunities for younger generations.

    

    "The second sits at the opposite extreme. Older people, often those in their late 70s and 80s are portrayed as frail dependent in nursing homes, clogging hospital beds and placing an unsustainable burden on public resources."

    

    Mr Pyne said the stereotypes persisted due to convenience, not accuracy.

    

    "They ignore the community and economic contribution of older people, simplify complex policy changes and allow us to avoid uncomfortable conversations about inequality and disadvantage."

    

    Australian Associated Press

Headline: 'Lot of complaints': ex-minister's aged care warning
Full Text:
Older Australians are dealing with big price increases and longer waiting lists for care under major reforms to the aged care sector, a former Liberal minister warns.

    Christopher Pyne, who spent 26 years in federal parliament, has also warned against stereotyping older people as either wealthy baby boomers or frail retirees, describing both framings as "crude caricatures".

    

    Mr Pyne, now the chairman of advocacy group Council on the Ageing, said it would take time to iron out the kinks in the government's new aged care act.

    

    The overhaul of the aged care sector took effect in November 2025, requiring wealthier retirees to pay more for their care and restructuring the way services are funded.

    

    Addressing the National Press Club to launch the council's State of the Older Nation report, the former cabinet minister said while it had only been a few months since the reforms kicked in, people in care had raised a number of complaints.

    

    They included longer waits for home care packages, pressure to sign care agreements quickly, and sharp price rises.

    

    "We expect the government will react to those over time," Mr Pyne said.

    

    He said older people were also being forced to pay for assistance showering.

    

    "Because that's such a big thing, of course, it gets a lot of coverage and a lot of complaints," Mr Pyne said.

    

    The State of the Older Nation report, released by Mr Pyne on Wednesday, includes alarming figures about the number of people experiencing age-based discrimination.

    

    It found 38 per cent of people had experienced ageism since turning 50 and 36 per cent said they didn't want to return to the workforce because of ageism.

    

    "Too often, the story of older Australians is reduced to one of two basic plot lines," Mr Pyne said.

    

    "The first goes like this: older people - often framed as baby boomers - are wealthy, secure, asset rich, sitting on housing, insulated from economic pressure and blocking opportunities for younger generations.

    

    "The second sits at the opposite extreme. Older people, often those in their late 70s and 80s are portrayed as frail dependent in nursing homes, clogging hospital beds and placing an unsustainable burden on public resources."

    

    Mr Pyne said the stereotypes persisted due to convenience, not accuracy.

    

    "They ignore the community and economic contribution of older people, simplify complex policy changes and allow us to avoid uncomfortable conversations about inequality and disadvantage."

    

    Australian Associated Press

Headline: Queensland disability client Bridie Gilligan choked to death on Hungry Jacks burger, coroner finds
Full Text:
Coronial findings revealed the woman lost consciousness and later died after shoving the fast food chain's "Yumbo" burger into her mouth at her Cairns home. 

    A Queensland disability client choked to death on a Hungry Jacks burger after snatching it from her support worker, a coroner has found.

    

    had a history of choking and was on a restricted diet when she choked while eating a Hungry Jacks "Yumbo" burger at her Edmonton home on April 29, 2021.

    

    Coronial findings published on Tuesday by Deputy State Coroner Stephanie Gallagher revealed how Ms Gilligan, 42, lost consciousness and later died after shoving the burger into her mouth.

    

    The inquest heard Ms Gilligan's support worker Margaret Lowry had purchased a burger for each of them and had intended to cut Ms Gilligan's into small pieces - but Ms Gilligan quickly snatched it and ran outside first.

    

    "The support worker called an ambulance and Ms Gilligan was taken to hospital but later died," Ms Gallagher said.

    

    The inquest heard Ms Gilligan had suffered a catastrophic brain injury and was pronounced brain dead on May 3, 2021.

    

    Her family have since expressed concerns about whether it had been appropriate to give her the burger given her disability and dietary needs.

    

    Ms Gilligan had been living in accommodation provided by Endeavour Foundation at the time of her death, as she required support for her intellectual disability associated with Cornelia De Lange Syndrome.

    

    She had been largely non-verbal and "required assistance with all her activities of daily living", Ms Gallagher noted.

    

    Ms Gilligan's mother told police her daughter was supposed to be given soft-textured foods and also be "completely supervised and assisted with eating, as if she was left alone with food then it is likely a choking incident would occur".

    

    Ms Gilligan's death was investigated by the Office of the Work Health and Safety Prosecutor, who concluded they were "not satisfied there was sufficient evidence to commence a prosecution against either the Endeavour Foundation or its worker, Ms Lowry".

    

    Ms Gallagher said in her findings on Tuesday that she accepted Ms Gilligan's death was preventable; however, she found Ms Lowry's supervision and Endeavour's systems regarding diet were both appropriate.

    

    "The concerns expressed by Ms Gilligan's family must be balanced against the fact that Ms Gilligan was a challenging client who did not cope well with new support workers," Ms Gallagher said.

    

    Support workers had given varying views to the inquest about whether giving fast food to a client was appropriate - with one suggesting it may actually support their independence.

    

    However, the worker noted that the decision should still be communicated with and resolved with the appropriate decision-maker - which would have been Ms Gilligan's mother in this case.

    

    A dietitian had given evidence saying she would not consider a burger or bun appropriate for Ms Gilligan's specific meal plan.

    

    Ms Gallagher ultimately agreed that the burger, even if cut up into small pieces, would not have complied with the standards set out for Ms Gilligan's diet - but she pointed out that Ms Gilligan's meal plan had contained other foods that also didn't comply with the standards.

    

    She said this had been due to competing considerations surrounding Ms Gilligan's quality of life, and the foods included had been ones Ms Gilligan liked or ones that gave her some level of autonomy or independence.

    

    Ms Gallagher considered it was not unreasonable for Ms Lowry to have thought a burger was suitable under the circumstances, given she had not been given specific training on the requirements of Ms Gilligan's "soft and bite-sized" diet, and the meal plan had included sandwiches.

    

    "Ms Lowry's evidence, which was not challenged, was that she had given Ms Gilligan cheeseburgers before, and Ms Gilligan had been able to eat them when they had been cut up into small pieces," Ms Gallagher noted.

    

    The inquest heard Endeavour had multiple policies and procedures in place to ensure workers had adequate information and training to care for a person with eating and swallowing difficulties.

    

    Endeavour staff had emphasised during the inquest that the support worker's goal is to support their clients to live as independently as possible.

    

    They acknowledged that the introduction of the NDIS had created a separation between Supported Independent Living providers like Endeavour and their client's other health services, however.

    

    In Ms Gilligan's case, the court heard this resulted in delayed follow ups with her other health services, a lack of collaboration on meal plans, and no guidance or training being provided to Ms Gilligan's support workers about her specific diet requirements.

    

    Ms Gallagher found Endeavour had not received sufficient information from Ms Gilligan's health care providers to identify the "real risk" bread products posed to her, and acknowledged Ms Gilligan's risk of choking was not appropriately managed by her overall system of care.

    

    "Endeavour has made sensible and appropriate changes to its systems and processes since Ms Gilligan's death to ensure that a similar tragic outcome does not occur in the future," Ms Gallagher said.

    

    "... I do not accept, on the evidence before me, that the responsibility for the circumstances which led to Ms Gilligan's death lies with Endeavour Foundation or their employees and agents."

    

    She found four recommendations suggested by Endeavour to be practical - including that the NDIS or NDIA review the processes for Supported Independent Living providers to access information about allied health supports, and consider appropriate funding in NDIA plans to ensure training of Supported Independent Living staff.

    

    Originally published as

Deputy State Coroner Stephanie Gallagher identified systemic failures in communication and training under the NDIS framework. Headline: Neo-Nazi re-sentenced to jail after losing appeal bid
Full Text:
Jacob Hersant arrived at court with a suitcase before a judge dispatched him to jail. Photo: Michael Currie/AAP PHOTOS

    Far-right extremist Jacob Hersant has been jailed for one month after losing an appeal against his Nazi salute conviction.

    

    The 26-year-old looked straight ahead as Victorian County Court Judge Simon Moglia re-sentenced him on Wednesday for the "contemptuous" offending.

    

    Hersant was the first Australian handed a prison sentence over the offence in November 2024 but he immediately appealed his one-month jail term and conviction in the County Court.

    

    Advertisement

    

    Ad

    

    At a three-day appeal hearing, he argued he did not perform the sieg heil and, even if he did, the charge was constitutionally invalid.

    

    Jacob Hersant was the first Australian to be handed a prison sentence for a Nazi salute. (Michael Currie/AAP PHOTOS)

    

    But Judge Moglia disagreed and in December found Hersant guilty of intentionally performing the salute on October 27, 2023.

    

    Video played to the court showed Hersant standing alongside fellow neo-Nazi Thomas Sewell as he raised his arm to salute in front of media outside the County Court, about six days after Victorian laws banning the gesture came into effect.

    

    He was then captured on camera saying "nearly did it - it's illegal now" and "Australia for the white man, heil Hitler", before walking away.

    

    In re-sentencing, Judge Moglia noted Hersant performed the salute only minutes after being sentenced to a community corrections order over a violent affray in regional Victoria.

    

    The judge said the gesture was contemptuous and showed a disregard to the authority of the court.

    

    The offending was also in breach of Hersant's corrections order, Judge Moglia found.

    

    Hersant's lawyer Tim Smartt accepted the breach but argued his client should not be jailed, pointing to other salute cases where offenders were fined or given corrections orders.

    

    Hersant's offending was less serious because he did not perform the gesture directly at a particular group or in a place like a synagogue, Mr Smartt said.

    

    Video showed fellow neo-Nazi Thomas Sewell standing beside Jacob Hersant as he did the Nazi salute. (Michael Currie/AAP PHOTOS)

    

    Advertisement

    

    Ad

    

    The 26-year-old was also provoked by the media and his crime did not cause the same level of harm as offenders who commit family violence or sexual assault, the defence lawyer said.

    

    Prosecutor Daniel Gurvich KC argued Hersant performed the salute in a calculated fashion to achieve maximum impact.

    

    He was not provoked by reporters and his actions were harmful to the Jewish community and other minority groups, the prosecutor said.

    

    Judge Moglia agreed, finding Hersant made the decision to engage with the media and commit the offence.

    

    "He relished that opportunity at the time and his intention was to engage in the gesture in full knowledge it was being done ... in the presence of the wider community," the judge said.

    

    Parliament recognised the Nazi salute caused harm to Victoria's diverse community, Judge Moglia said

    

    Hersant's actions were ultimately offensive and worthy of a one-month jail term, the judge found.

    

    The 26-year-old was also convicted and fined $1000 for breaching his corrections order.

    

    He was taken into custody at the end of his re-sentencing hearing.

Headline: Young men need education to cope with rejection to keep women safe: DV experts
Full Text:
Teenage boys and young men should be given education and training in how to cope with rejection to help prevent murders like that of 21-year-old , national family violence experts say.

    They want respectful relationships programs extended beyond the mandatory age of 15 to include 16- to 24-year-olds, and say this would reduce the risk of retaliation by young men after sexual or relationship rejection, a pattern evident in many recent,

    

    Such programs would help young men learn to cope with a sense of humiliation and to avoid behaviours they may not recognise as common precursors to fatal violence, experts say.

    

    National women's safety leaders, including Monash University Professor Kate Fitz-Gibbon, chair of Respect Victoria, Jesuit Social Services Men's Project executive director Matt Tyler and DVNSW chief executive Delia Donovan say actions recommended by NSW coroner Teresa O'Sullivan in her findings on James' murder should already have been acknowledged and implemented.

    

    O'Sullivan found that the St Andrew's Cathedral School water polo coach was killed at the school by her colleague, sports assistant Paul Thijssen, with whom she had a brief relationship.

    

    Thijssen had used coercively controlling behaviours and tech-based abuse – including location-tracking James after she attempted to end the relationship – that peers did not recognise as abusive and indicating possible escalation.

    

    The coroner's noted "a particular need" for targeted relationships education for 16- to 25-year-olds at school, further education or work, and that young men should be provided with more relationship information and advice services.

    

    Comments by James' mother, Peta that "we must teach boys to respect and value women's opinions and choices, and to accept rejection" resonated deeply, O'Sullivan wrote. "It is my hope that Peta's message, and the lessons learnt from this tragedy, echo beyond this courtroom and contribute to meaningful change."

    

    Fitz-Gibbon, whom O'Sullivan commissioned to provide evidence, said Peta's statement "goes to the heart of action that is needed" and that it is "deeply concerning" that there has been no governmental response to findings that could prevent fatal violence.

    

    "Rejection, and more specifically the failure to cope with rejection, is all too often at the heart of intimate partner homicides," she said. "I've seen that repeatedly in over a decade of examining intimate partner homicide in Australia."

    

    Domestic homicide death reviews across Australia often identified "jealousy, relationship separation, infidelity and family court involvement as precipitating men's lethal violence".

    

    "The inability to deal safely with rejection all too often runs through cases of intimate partner homicide, yet we have not seen a substantive prevention effort, particularly with young men, that focuses specifically on addressing entitlement and the inability to cope with rejection. We need to teach boys and young men these skills."

    

    Matt Tyler, who has researched young men's attitudes to violence and masculinity expectations, said 16 per cent of adolescent boys he "agreed that they usually try to get back at someone if they are rejected".

    

    "The problematic behaviours identified in [the Lilie James] inquest – entitlement, digital surveillance, retaliation after rejection – are unfortunately not rare," he said.

    

    Mother of Lilie James gives heartbreaking tribute

    

    At the inquest into water polo coach Lilie James's death, her mother read out a gut-wrenching statement.

    

    "Based on our work with hundreds of schools each year, teachers as well as parents often need greater support to engage with young people on a range of issues, including equipping boys to cope with rejection," Tyler said.

    

    Fitz-Gibbon and Tyler's call for action on O'Sullivan's recommendations, including for community-wide education on coercive control, is backed by women's safety authorities, including , chief executive of Our Watch.

    

    Among the devastating inquest findings was how widely Thijssen's coercive behaviours were accepted by some friends and peers, Kinnersly said: "They did not seem to understand why location-tracking was so wrong and did not see it as a potential precursor for further forms of abuse."

    

    Delia Donovan, of DVNSW, said the inquest showed the need to better reach young men but that, "this is about calling men into the conversation – not shaming them – and giving them the tools to recognise and change harmful behaviours before they escalate".

    

    "For boys, it needs to start with loving and respecting themselves," she said. "There is an urgent need for education so young people understand the real risks of coercive and controlling behaviours, including technology-facilitated abuse like location-tracking and digital-monitoring."

    

    "Location-sharing should never be normalised as a sign of care or intimacy – it can be a serious red flag," said Donovan.

    

    Safe and Equal (formerly DV Vic) interim chief executive Christine Mathieson said though prevention work takes time, "men and boys learn entitlement, and they learn aggression. In too many cases this spills over into violence and the murder of women.

    

    "We need to support young men with relationship skills and emotional intelligence so that they do not harm others, but also so that they are happier and have better lives filled with care and love."

    

    Dr Tessa Boyd-Caine, chief executive of Australia's National Research Organisation for Women's Safety, said the findings reinforced that relationship separation was a time of risk, and for young people "post-separation abuse can escalate quickly".

    

    "Incorporating this reality into respectful relationships education is important because it supports earlier recognition of harm and safer decision-making," she said.

    

    "The absence of any public response to the recommendation is critical, too, because prevention only works when lessons are acted on, not simply noted."

    

    Schools and staff needed clear pathways for safe and consistent responses when students shared experiences of violence, Boyd-Caine said. "Curriculum content without support for disclosures risks placing responsibility back on young people or leaving staff to navigate high-risk situations without the competence or guidance they need."

    

    New curriculum should be paired with training, practice guidance and evaluation, she said.
