Headline: Cutting capital gains deductions opens door to bigger cuts
Full Text:
A reduction in the capital gains tax discount is firming as the expected centrepiece of Treasurer Jim Chalmers' May budget, alongside a broader

   suite of reforms including possible tax cuts aimed at helping younger Australians buy their own home.

    

    A senior government source, who asked not to be named so they could detail internal policy discussions, said possible changes to the capital gains tax in the forthcoming May budget were not advanced but confirmed a change had not been ruled out.

    

    Reducing the concession to 33 per cent from 50 per cent is estimated by Deloitte Access Economics to raise about $4 billion a year in extra tax by 2035-36.

    

    Crucially, the source said Prime Minister Anthony Albanese – who has at times been averse to some of the tax changes pushed internally by Chalmers – had not ruled out changes to the tax.

    

    "The key for housing is more supply, but there are perceptions as well about tax," one MP said.

    

    "Overwhelmingly, we are looking at supply, but we are also looking at it [CGT] and the budget situation."

    

    The government is under pressure to use the May budget to both reduce the overall rate of growth in spending and to find ways to lift the pace at which the economy can grow without adding to inflationary pressures.

    

    Changes to the capital gains tax, which is the focus of a Greens-led Senate inquiry due to report next month, have been canvassed within Labor for several months as one way to improve the tax system while helping younger Australians get into the property market.

    

    The extra revenue is likely to be channelled towards reducing taxes in other areas.

    

    The current 50 per cent concession for assets held for at least 12 months was introduced in 1999. At the time, proponents argued it would encourage people to buy shares, boosting Australian publicly listed companies.

    

    Instead, most investors went into property. Before the change, most landlords made money from their investments, but after the concession, most became negatively geared.

    

    This masthead revealed before the 2025 budget that the government had asked the Treasury to model the impact of concessions, though it later walked away from the proposal.

    

    Labor MP Mike Freelander, who holds the Sydney seat of Macarthur, said that "I have always felt it [the 50 per cent capital gains tax discount] is too generous".

    

    "I have benefited from it a lot, so it's a no-brainer to me. There are good economic reasons why it should be wound back a bit," he said.

    

    "I understand that people worry about the consequences, but I think it should certainly be considered."

    

    A second Labor MP, who asked not to be named, said that housing affordability "is only getting worse and the impatience of people locked out of the housing market will only grow as more people are locked out".

    

    "I think people overwhelmingly support us building homes, but structurally, they think that the housing sector is unfair," they said.

    

    The $4 billion estimate by Deloitte Access Economics is based on reducing the concession for all assets. One option being considered is reducing the concession on property, leaving it intact for other assets, such as shares.

    

    Another option is to cut the concession to 25 per cent, a position Labor took to the 2016 and 2019 elections. Other issues being examined include grandfathering any change to ensure investors who bought an asset under the current law would not be disadvantaged.

    

    One option being canvassed is to increase the size of income tax cuts that are due to start from July 1.

    

    Worth up to $5 a week, the current tax cut is modest, only a little more than the famous "sandwich and a milkshake" tax cuts delivered by the Howard government in 2003.

    

    Chalmers said the May budget would focus on ways to make the economy more productive.

    

    The treasurer, who last week told The Monthly magazine that the inability of young people to buy a home was one of Australia's defining issues, signalled that further tax reform was on the government's agenda.

    

    "Any further changes to taxes, beyond those we've already flagged, would be a matter for cabinet in the usual way, and would be consistent with the reform directions that we set out after the reform roundtable a few months ago," he said on Wednesday.

    

    "We've made it clear we've got other priorities when it comes to tax reform. The focus in tax reform is on cutting income taxes."

    

    While the Liberal Party has signalled opposition to a change in the CGT, it would probably win support from the Greens, who have long argued the concession encourages speculation by investors in the property market at the expense of young buyers.

    

    Teal independent MP Allegra Spender said Chalmers had to use the May budget to deliver real reform, including changes to the mix of federal taxes and cuts to government spending.

    

    She said policies such as the EV tax deal had to be on the chopping block, while spending growth on the NDIS was still too high.

    

    "Real reform has to have winners and losers. We're beyond the point where everyone can be a winner," she said.

    

    The current CGT discount applies to all assets, including shares, cryptocurrencies and those held within superannuation funds.

    

    The Association of Australian Superannuation Funds, in a submission to the current Senate inquiry into the capital gains tax, has warned that removing the 50 per cent discount for super funds would lift the tax payable on all unrealised capital gains from 10 per cent to 15 per cent.

    

    The association said the discount had been "instrumental in encouraging superannuation fund

    

    investment across a multitude of key areas across Australia's domestic economy".

Treasurer Jim Chalmers has highlighted the reform as a potential way to improve housing affordability for younger Australians and address tax system equity. According to Deloitte Access Economics, reducing the discount to 33% could raise an extra $4 billion annually by 2035-36. The proposed change, which Prime Minister Anthony Albanese has not ruled out, faces opposition from the Liberal Party but may gain support from the Greens.

story_title: Calvary Mater Hospital Faces Inquiry Over Maintenance Failures
story_summary: A New South Wales parliamentary inquiry has been established to investigate serious management and maintenance failures at the Calvary Mater Hospital in Newcastle. The inquiry follows numerous critical incidents, including deadly mould in air-conditioning vents, maggot infestations, and water damage, which led to ward closures and cancelled cancer treatments. Driven by the Coalition and Greens over Labor's opposition, the investigation will examine the effectiveness of the hospital's public-private partnership (PPP) with maintenance provider Honeywell (as part of Novacare). After visiting the hospital, NSW Health Minister Ryan Park apologized to staff and announced the government was stepping in to begin urgent rectification works and exploring legal options to hold the private contractors accountable.

Headline: Credibility? There's nothing in Reserve
Full Text:
As expected, the RBA board raised the cash rate by 0.25 per cent on Tuesday to 3.85 per cent, reversing one of the three premature cuts it signed off on last year.

   While this action will be welcomed by many economic commentators, it will not repair Michele Bullock and her board's damaged credibility.

    

    Why? Because they remain unwilling to admit they have made serious strategic and operational errors in the past two years – raising questions not only about their economic competence but also their commitment to price stability. As we know, they kept the cash rate too low in 2024 as they tested how low unemployment could go, even if this risked inflation never returning to target. An "experiment", in the words of loose-lipped deputy governor Andrew Hauser, that was inconsistent with the board's price stability mandate. And then there were the unwarranted rate cuts last year, which were kicked off last February before underlying inflation had returned to its 2-3 per cent target band, let alone having sustainably returned to it.

    

    This policy easing led to a surge in household spending over recent months that, given the economy's capacity constraints and too-high public spending, has seen inflation heading north again. To restore their battered reputation Bullock and her board needed to frankly admit to these errors in their public statements on Tuesday. They did no such thing.

    

    'No surprise' RBA raised interest rates after inflation spike

    

    Sky News host Peta Credlin says it is "no surprise" the Reserve Bank of Australia increased interest rates.

    

    'No surprise' RBA raised interest rates after inflation spike

    

    Sky News host Peta Credlin says it is "no surprise" the Reserve Bank of Australia increased interest...

    

    Bullock's press conference was particularly disappointing. It was the first tough one she has had. Time and time again, she was given an opportunity to acknowledge and take responsibility for her and her board's missteps.

    

    Time and time again, she refused, resorting to excuses and evasions. Asked what she would say to Australians who believed her when she said inflation was under control last year, she deflected, saying she had "empathy" for people hurt by inflation and higher interest rates. Asked whether she regretted easing policy last year, as recently as August, she said everyone was worried about "downside risks" to aggregate demand at that time, and added that she and her board acted "cautiously". And asked whether she would ditch her strategy of keeping the cash rate lower than it otherwise would be to preserve pandemic-era employment gains, she said it wouldn't change, even though it has left us with a persistent inflation problem.

    

    If Bullock is unwilling, Fonz-like, to admit to any error, her promises to do a better job on inflation in the future will ring hollow.

    

    At the very least she should have made clear her determination to knock our resurgent inflation on the head – while stopping short of signalling future rate changes – but she fell well short of that. Instead of providing reassurance that she will be a "tough cop on the beat" in this regard, she referred to "temporary" factors that might be behind our recent CPI growth and left open the possibility that a single 0.25 per cent hike might be enough.

    

    With preparations well under way for the May budget, Bullock and her board should also have sent a message to Jim Chalmers about the need for fiscal restraint.

    

    'When will the reckless spending end?': Ley calls out Labor government as RBA lifts rates

    

    Opposition Leader Sussan Ley calls out the Albanese government's "reckless spending".

    

    'When will the reckless spending end?': Ley calls out Labor government as RBA lifts rates

    

    Opposition Leader Sussan Ley calls out the Albanese government's "reckless spending".

    

   "The average...

    

    (With Chalmers' Treasury secretary, Jenny Wilkinson, on the RBA board, this is unlikely to happen, underscoring the fact this body is not "independent" of government, as Chalmers persists in claiming.)

    

    This could have been done in an understated way that respects the division of policy responsibility between Martin Place and Canberra. In an inflationary economy pressing hard against its capacity constraints, our short-term fiscal and monetary policy settings must work together to bring aggregate demand back into balance with supply.

    

    That's not a contestable or politicised proposition but a basic truism that any reasonable member of the public would understand.

    

    But it has completely escaped Chalmers, which begs the question what advice he has been getting from his two Treasury secretaries, Steven Kennedy (now in the role of head of Prime Minister and Cabinet) and Wilkinson.

    

    Now that the RBA board has acted to raise the cash rate, the ball is firmly in Chalmers' court. He had a golden opportunity to show economic leadership in December's MYEFO statement, which should have been a mini-budget to take pressure off inflation. With the RBA board's commitment to tackle inflation still in question, the May budget assumes even greater importance in this regard.

    

    Since Chalmers rejects the idea that public demand is making any contribution to excessive price growth, he is highly unlikely to do what is needed. Indeed, if anything, he is likely to make things worse by announcing further of cost-of-living subsidies to take the political heat off the government.

    

    RBA lifts cash rate by 25 basis points to 3.85 per cent

    

    The Reserve Bank of Australia has lifted the cash rate to 3.85 per cent from 3.6 per cent.

    

    RBA lifts cash rate by 25 basis points to 3.85 per cent

    

    The Reserve Bank of Australia has lifted the cash rate to 3.85 per cent from 3.6 per cent. Sky News...

    

    A competent Labor treasurer, such as Paul Keating or even Bill Hayden (who delivered a strong budget under Gough Whitlam), would know what action should be taken when the inflation fire is burning. They would commit to a hard spending envelope, capping real spending growth at no more than 1 per cent. This has averaged over 4 per cent each year under Chalmers.

    

    They would close the NDIS to new entrants while this failed program was redesigned from scratch. They would announce means-testing of all untargeted payments and subsidies, focusing help on those in need.

    

    And they would call on their state and territory counterparts to tighten their fiscal belts, threatening the recalcitrant with less access to federal funding.

    

    A final word on Bullock. While I believe her heart is in the right place, I worry she has fallen into the trap of seeing monetary policy as a narrow numbers game, where all that matters are the data and the forecasts. Too many private sector economists make the same mistake.

    

    Instead, central banking turns on trust and credibility, as indeed all leadership does.

    

    It is a pity she did not have that thought in mind when she walked into her press conference on Tuesday. A bit of humility and contrition would have gone a long way.

    

    David Pearl is a former Treasury assistant secretary.

story_title: RBA Raises Interest Rates Amid Inflation Debate
story_summary: The Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA), under Governor Michele Bullock, raised the official cash rate and revised its forecasts to show inflation is set to surge above 4%, taking longer to return to target. The move has intensified a public debate over the causes of inflation. Treasurer Jim Chalmers insists that strong private demand is the primary driver and that government spending is restrained. However, this view is contested by numerous economists from institutions like Deloitte Access Economics and AMP, as well as business groups, who argue that historically high levels of government spending are a significant inflationary factor. The rate hike was passed on by major banks, increasing financial pressure on mortgage holders.
Sub_Cluster_Title: Political Fallout and Institutional Credibility Questioned
Sub_Cluster_Summary: This group of articles covers the political and institutional consequences of the rate hike. The focus is on the partisan blame game between the government and opposition, as well as critiques of the credibility and past performance of both the RBA and government leaders.

Headline: Nine years after concerns were raised, Mater staff feel action will occur
Full Text:
Calvary Mater hospital staff feel action will occur on mould  Staff rally at the Calvary Mater Newcastle to fix the mould problem.

   Inset: A closed ward due to mould. Picture by Peter Lorimer

    

    NSW Minister for Health Ryan Park at the Calvary Mater Hospital to deal with the mould debacle on Monday. Picture by Peter Lorimer

    

    A ward closed on level 5 due to mould in early January. Picture supplied

    

    NSW Minister for Health Ryan Park at the Calvary Mater Hospital to deal with the mould debacle on Monday. Picture by Peter Lorimer

    

    A ward closed on level 5 due to mould in early January. Picture supplied

    

    Mould at the Calvary Mater Newcastle.

    

    Water leaks at the Calvary Mater Newcastle.

    

    A sign at last week's rally at the Calvary Mater Newcastle. Picture supplied

    

    The auxiliary ladies store room door, where mould was found. Picture supplied

    

    A ward closed on level 5 due to mould in early January. Picture supplied

    

    A ward closed on level 5 due to mould in early January. Picture supplied

    

    Staff rally at the Calvary Mater Newcastle to fix the mould problem. Picture by Peter Lorimer

    

    Calvary Mater Newcastle staff feel for the first time that action will be taken to fix the mould fiasco at the hospital, after almost a decade of frustration over maintenance trouble. In NSW Parliament on Tuesday, the Opposition and Greens put pressure on the Minns government to ensure it follows through on promised action. Greens MP Amanda Cohn said "there were critical safety and maintenance issues at the Calvary Mater hospital that were reported by staff through the proper channels as early as 2017". "It has taken years for there to be the attention on this issue that it deserves," Dr Cohn said. Staff estimate the systemic mould issue has been occurring for at least a year, but water leaks and other maintenance trouble had been happening for years. Shadow Health Minister Sarah Mitchell said "the problem we have is there are repeated incidents every week". "The staff up there literally told the Newcastle Herald it feels like an episode of Stranger Things. This is not what a health service should look like in 2026." After meeting NSW Health Minister Ryan Park at the hospital on Monday, staff "felt heard for the first time and that action was going to be taken", one staffer said. "His remarks and obvious frustration are exactly our experience when dealing with the PPP [public-private partnership]." As for fixing the mould problem, Mr Park said "it has to happen". "I'm determined to do it as fast as I possibly can," he said. The Newcastle Herald reported on Monday that most of the intensive care unit and rooms in three wards were shut at the weekend for "deep cleaning". These areas had "slightly elevated levels" of the mould Aspergillus fumigatus. The hospital also closed two wards in January due to mould in the air-conditioning ducts and maggots falling from the ceiling onto patients. This included the closure of a haematology day ward - also known as ward 5D - with cancer patients moved to another ward. Mr Park said, "I am deeply angry and sorry that staff are in this position". "They shouldn't have to be worrying about trying to manage this situation with private contractors who should be doing their job." The Mater is a public hospital run by a private company called Novacare, which is responsible for the building maintenance and cleaning. Honeywell is also responsible for the hospital's maintenance, as one of four companies that make up Novacare, along with Westpac Bank, Medirest and Abigroup. Mr Park said the government was looking at legal options and would "hold the contractors to account". Late last year, the minister "brought in the contractors on multiple occasions at my office in Sydney and at Parliament House". "I'd been given the clear impression and undertaking that there was a pathway forward to deal particularly with the cladding, which will go a long way to deal with the water and mould-related issues. "Over the summer break, it was clear - although there'd been scaffolding put up - that work was not happening." Mr Park said he and NSW Health secretary Susan Pearce made a decision to "step into" the PPP contract and "begin the cladding rectification works". Staff say the cladding will help, but the mould was "being caused by the air-conditioning system and humidity". Wallsend MP Sonia Hornery said "works on the mould infested air-conditioning have begun".

Headline: Inside the new $411 million ‘purpose-built' St George Hospital redevelopment
Full Text:
A southern Sydney hospital's $411 million redevelopment is complete, with a new nine-storey building and upgraded campus set to open within months and provide enhanced services.

    A major hospital upgrade in Sydney's south has been completed to the tune of $411 million after a year of major construction.

    

    The third stage in the redevelopment of St George Hospital is complete, with its doors expected to open to the public within months.

    

    The new facilities in the hospital's redevelopment include pathology, aged care, behavioural support, palliative care, MRI and nuclear medicine.

    

    The nine-storey Kensington Street Building capped out the project, housing community and outpatient services.

    

    Previous stages of the development delivered a new Emergency Department, an acute services building, upgraded birthing suite and operating theatre. The initial stages of development began more than a decade ago.

    

    Health Minister Ryan Park said he's looking forward to opening the Kensington Street Building which will "bring together a range of ambulatory, outpatient and community services to provide better health outcomes".

    

    "It is so important to provide our dedicated health staff with a modern, purpose-built health facility, which caters to the specific needs of patients, families, and clinicians," he said.

    

    "We are getting on with the job by delivering more hospitals, more beds and more services to ensure patients and their families receive the care they need, when they need it."

    

    In the new facilities, patients recovering from brain injuries will be able to access structured rehabilitation for the first time at the hospital.

    

    The St George Hospital redevelopment is one of 32 new and upgraded hospital projects underway by the government, including Rouse Hill Hospital, Bankstown Hospital and Blacktown and Mount Druitt Hospital.

    

    Originally published as

Headline: What could the government do to help battle inflation?
Full Text:
The Reserve Bank's decision to hike interest rates has brought fresh attention to the question of whether the federal budget is helping or hindering the quest to vanquish inflation.

    Treasurer Jim Chalmers is insistent that inflation's resurgence is not the government's fault, pointing to the fact that private sector spending has been growing faster than public sector spending in the last few months.

    

    But the government exerts substantial influence over both halves of the economy. When it gives money to households to help with the cost of living, for example, that bolsters spending in the private sector.

    

    And regardless of who caused the inflation, there are plenty of options available to the government to help contain it. Chalmers has already hinted that the May budget will be "restrained" with a renewed focus on inflation.

    

    He has described all of his budgets that way, an assessment economists have not always agreed with. Now that inflation has taken a turn for the worse, the bar will be set even higher. Here are some things the government might consider.

    

   How the government can influence inflation

     Inflation is what results when there is more demand (appetite to spend) in an economy than it can supply. That pushes prices up, and when the price growth is excessive and unpredictable, it can erode people's purchasing power and be very destabilising.

    

    Whatever its cause, high inflation can only be fixed by some combination of reducing demand or boosting supply. Boosting supply — such as by expanding an economy into new areas or making it run more efficiently — is typically slower and more difficult.

    

    So when there is a burst of inflation, the immediate policy responses are all about demand. The Reserve Bank raises its cash rate, which flows through to higher interest rates for borrowers all across the economy, including on home loans.

    

    The aim is to contain spending by just as much as is needed to tame inflation, without chilling the economy so much that a recession results.

    

    The federal government has split responsibilities with its own budget. Responsive to the needs of voters, it will often try to ease the pain and may wish to target those least able to absorb higher prices.

    

    But it can also help the Reserve Bank by boosting supply in the long term with policies to support growth, and to reduce demand in the short term by raising taxes or cutting spending.

    

    Neither option is popular, which is why governments often set a more limited aspiration to avoid adding to demand and making the problem worse.

    

   Has the Albanese government helped or hindered?

     This has been the stated strategy of the Albanese government, which has framed its budgets as "responsible" and "restrained", balancing the need to provide cost of living relief with the need to be mindful about inflation.

    

    In two of the three years of its first term, it delivered budget surpluses, spending less than the taxes it collected. But while there were savings in those budgets, for the most part the surpluses were due to higher tax collections, not because of government policies.

    

    In fact, new policy decisions since Labor took office have added more than $100 billion to the budget overall.

    

    Economists critical of the government's approach, and the federal opposition, say figures like that one undermine claims of "restraint" and that interest rates would be lower if the government had spent less.

    

    But overall figures like this one have limited usefulness. For example, spending on aged care home packages or on foreign aid would have little or no inflation impact, whereas giving money to households or building major projects might be highly inflationary.

    

    Still, some specific policies — including those badged as cost of living relief — have been viewed by economists as inflationary. That includes universal or widely available supports such as tax cuts and childcare subsidies.

    

    "The government, entirely understandably, essentially gave people money … That didn't really help," economist Chris Richardson told the ABC.

    

    It also includes subsidies that tend to benefit those on higher incomes, including the government's EV tax discount, which was originally slated to cost just $405 million over four years when it was introduced in 2022, but will cost $8.1 billion over the next four.

    

    Another example is the government's subsidy for home batteries for those with rooftop solar, which within months, had blown its initial budget of $2.3 million over four years and will now cost $7.2 billion even after a dramatic reduction in its generosity.

    

    Richardson has previously suggested $7 billion of government spending is equivalent to one 25-basis-point interest rate movement when the economy is straining against its limits.

    

    "If the increase in spending is greater than the ability of the economy to support it, you can't have much extra spending without the economy starting to blow some smoke," he said.

    

   What might the government do next?

     Those programs might be among the first the government considers scaling back on ahead of the May budget. Energy Minister Chris Bowen last year hinted that the EV tax discount could be changed or scrapped after a scheduled review of the policy.

    

    Chalmers and Finance Minister Katy Gallagher also confirmed last year that they had asked all public service departments to identify 5 per cent of their budgets as "low priority", which could then be used to find savings in future budgets.

    

    And the prime minister confirmed last week that the government now plans to contain growth in the NDIS to 6 per cent a year, a more ambitious target than the previous 8 per cent.

    

    The newly announced Thriving Kids program, which would pursue early interventions for young children with mild to moderate autism, is designed to support this cost-saving drive by reducing the number of children who will be eligible for NDIS support later in life.

    

    The government has also signalled an appetite to expand the economy's capacity to supply with a range of reforms designed to bolster productivity and to boost the supply of housing, although there is little conclusive success on either front yet.

    

    Richardson said progress on that was urgent, noting the RBA this week downgraded its forecasts for economic growth and for housing construction, underlining the urgency.

    

    "The long and winding road to recovering our lost living standards is looking longer and windier," he said.

    

    He added the government could go much further with its budget in a way that would help inflation, but not without political risk.

    

    "The government has to run the ruler over the budget and look for areas where it can be tougher," he said, saying both tax hikes and spending cuts should be considered.

    

    Chalmers said as recently as last week that the government remained ambitious to pursue tax reform, but the government has shown no firm inclination yet that it plans to revisit ideas such as negative gearing and capital gains tax reform, as it has championed in the past.

    

    "There are all sorts of things that could be done, but they're all politically challenging … Politicians don't like risking votes."

The rate hike was passed on by major banks, increasing financial pressure on mortgage holders.

Headline: Financial toll hangs heavy over cancer patients
Full Text:
Jacinta Andrews knows all too well that the true cost of cancer extends far beyond medical bills or day treatment.

    Her husband has been battling multiple forms of cancer for decades, and although he is in remission the family continue to navigate the ongoing effects of his treatments, with some procedures costing thousands.

    

    While the public healthcare system covers a range of care, many patients still face the financial burden of cancer, research has found.

    

    Cancer Council Australia's national survey published on Wednesday found that nearly 99 per cent of Australians diagnosed with cancer incur out-of-pocket expenses, even when accessing public care.

    

    Tony Andrews was first diagnosed with head and neck cancer in 2009, forcing Ms Andrews to act as a full-time carer, assisting with daily transport, medical treatments and emotional wellbeing.

    

    While Ms Andrews was able to take paid time off, her husband was self-employed, and wasn't eligible for assistance.

    

    She considers herself lucky though that she could take leave, adding that they wouldn't have been able to pay their mortgage if it wasn't for her supportive workplace.

    

    The family were rocked by cancer again in 2023, after Mr Andrews was diagnosed with non-Hodgkin's lymphoma, which they are still navigating.

    

    Although Mr Andrews remains in remission, the side effects of his treatment have proved costly.

    

    "There are issues that continue and the latest ongoing thing since 2010, is the severity of the radiation treatment had on his gums and teeth," she told AAP.

    

    "We have had a lot of dental costs, and we can't afford private health insurance."

    

    Ms Andrews said the side effects on his teeth was unexpected, with her husband now struggling to eat.

    

    "We are saving up for him to get dentures or plates," she said.

    

    "We didn't know this was going to happen and you don't think about that stuff at the time of treatment, because you are just trying to survive."

    

    Cancer Council analysis found that loss of income or disruption to employment is the most common financial cost, affecting more than a third of Australians diagnosed with cancer.

    

    Surgical expenses (23.7 per cent ) and insurance gap payments (23.6 per cent) represented the largest out-of-pocket cost for nearly one quarter of respondents.

    

    "It is a huge burden for people," Cancer Council's Peter Diamond told AAP.

    

    "These costs can include both direct medical costs and indirect expenses, which people experience regardless of what stage (of) cancer diagnosis."

    

    Australians should be able to access high-quality cancer care, based on what best meets their needs, not what they can afford, Dr Diamond added.

    

    The research coincides with World Cancer Day and calls for increased access to financial counselling services for Australians affected by cancer through a mandatory industry-funded model, alongside greater investment from federal, state and territory governments.

    

    Australian Associated Press

Headline: Financial toll hangs heavy over cancer patients
Full Text:
Jacinta Andrews knows all too well that the true cost of cancer extends far beyond medical bills or day treatment.

    Her husband has been battling multiple forms of cancer for decades, and although he is in remission the family continue to navigate the ongoing effects of his treatments, with some procedures costing thousands.

    

    While the public healthcare system covers a range of care, many patients still face the financial burden of cancer, research has found.

    

    Cancer Council Australia's national survey published on Wednesday found that nearly 99 per cent of Australians diagnosed with cancer incur out-of-pocket expenses, even when accessing public care.

    

    Tony Andrews was first diagnosed with head and neck cancer in 2009, forcing Ms Andrews to act as a full-time carer, assisting with daily transport, medical treatments and emotional wellbeing.

    

    While Ms Andrews was able to take paid time off, her husband was self-employed, and wasn't eligible for assistance.

    

    She considers herself lucky though that she could take leave, adding that they wouldn't have been able to pay their mortgage if it wasn't for her supportive workplace.

    

    The family were rocked by cancer again in 2023, after Mr Andrews was diagnosed with non-Hodgkin's lymphoma, which they are still navigating.

    

    Although Mr Andrews remains in remission, the side effects of his treatment have proved costly.

    

    "There are issues that continue and the latest ongoing thing since 2010, is the severity of the radiation treatment had on his gums and teeth," she told AAP.

    

    "We have had a lot of dental costs, and we can't afford private health insurance."

    

    Ms Andrews said the side effects on his teeth was unexpected, with her husband now struggling to eat.

    

    "We are saving up for him to get dentures or plates," she said.

    

    "We didn't know this was going to happen and you don't think about that stuff at the time of treatment, because you are just trying to survive."

    

    Cancer Council analysis found that loss of income or disruption to employment is the most common financial cost, affecting more than a third of Australians diagnosed with cancer.

    

    Surgical expenses (23.7 per cent ) and insurance gap payments (23.6 per cent) represented the largest out-of-pocket cost for nearly one quarter of respondents.

    

    "It is a huge burden for people," Cancer Council's Peter Diamond told AAP.

    

    "These costs can include both direct medical costs and indirect expenses, which people experience regardless of what stage (of) cancer diagnosis."

    

    Australians should be able to access high-quality cancer care, based on what best meets their needs, not what they can afford, Dr Diamond added.

    

    The research coincides with World Cancer Day and calls for increased access to financial counselling services for Australians affected by cancer through a mandatory industry-funded model, alongside greater investment from federal, state and territory governments.

    

    Australian Associated Press

Headline: Tencent Cloud EdgeOne Launches Free AI Crawler Control: Empowering Developers to Reclaim Content Sovereignty
Full Text:
Published: 04/02/2026 16:00  SHENZHEN, China Feb. 4, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- As the demand for data to train Generative AI models surges, developers

   are facing a critical dilemma: how to manage aggressive AI scraping while protecting their original content and infrastructure costs. To address this, Tencent Cloud EdgeOne has officially released its Basic Bot Management capabilities to all users—including those on the Free Plan. This update introduces two core features: AI Crawler Control and the CAPTCHA Page.

    

    Ending the Crawler "Whac-A-Mole" Game

    

    Unlike traditional web crawlers, modern AI crawlers are stealthier, operate on a massive scale, and are increasingly difficult to distinguish from legitimate traffic. Developers often find themselves trapped in an endless game of "Whac-A-Mole"—blocking one crawler IP only to see dozens of new ones appear the next day under different guises. This results in unauthorized scraping of original content and wasted server bandwidth.

    

    Tencent Cloud EdgeOne's new AI Crawler Control solves this by utilizing advanced User-Agent feature recognition. The system continuously updates its identification rules to pinpoint mainstream AI bots, including GPTBot, ClaudeBot, and Google-Extended

    

    Developers no longer need to write complex scripts to defend their sites. With a simple configuration in the EdgeOne console, users can apply diverse actions based on their specific business needs:

    

    Monitor: Observe crawler behavior without interference.

    

    Block: Instantly stop unauthorized data harvesting.

    

    Allow: Permit friendly bots to access your site.

    

    Challenge: Trigger a verification step for suspicious traffic.

    

    This flexible strategy ensures developers can block malicious data harvesting while still embracing a friendly AI ecosystem when beneficial.

    

    Intelligent Defense for High-Value Scenarios

    

    In addition to crawler management, the update includes the CAPTCHA Page feature designed to mitigate automated attacks. This function intelligently identifies suspicious Bot behavior and triggers a human-machine verification CAPTCHA.

    

    Crucially, this is done without degrading the experience for real users. It builds a robust security line for high-stakes scenarios—such as digital content rights protection, e-commerce flash sales, and financial transactions—ensuring that automated scripts cannot manipulate metrics or disrupt services.

    

    From Passive Defense to Active Management

    

    "With the rapid evolution of AI technology, the governance of content and data access is a long-term challenge," said Yun Li, Head of Tencent Cloud EdgeOne Product Team. "By making our Basic Bot Management capabilities free, we aim to help every developer shift from passive defense to active management, giving them full control over their digital assets."

    

    The new features are available now in the EdgeOne console. Tencent Cloud EdgeOne is committed to continuously refining its Bot Management and security capabilities, helping developers establish clear boundaries between AI technology application and content rights protection.

    

    About Tencent Cloud EdgeOne: EdgeOne is Tencent Cloud's integrated platform for edge security and acceleration.

    

    Click the link to learn more about EdgeOne: https://edgeone.ai/?from=ai_crawler_control

    

    About Tencent Cloud:

    

    Tencent Cloud, one of the world's leading cloud companies, is committed to creating innovative solutions to resolve real-world issues and enabling digital transformation for smart industries. Through our extensive global infrastructure, Tencent Cloud provides businesses across the globe with stable and secure industry-leading cloud products and services, leveraging technological advancements such as cloud computing, Big Data analytics, AI, IoT, and network security. It is our constant mission to meet the needs of industries across the board, including the fields of gaming, media and entertainment, finance, healthcare, property, retail, travel, and transportation.

story_summary: Tencent Cloud has launched free 'Basic Bot Management' capabilities for its EdgeOne platform to help developers combat aggressive AI web crawlers from entities like GPTBot and ClaudeBot. Headline: 'Lot of complaints': ex-minister's aged care warning
Full Text:
Older Australians are dealing with big price increases and longer waiting lists for care under major reforms to the aged care sector, a former Liberal minister warns.

    Christopher Pyne, who spent 26 years in federal parliament, has also warned against stereotyping older people as either wealthy baby boomers or frail retirees, describing both framings as "crude caricatures".

    

    Mr Pyne, now the chairman of advocacy group Council on the Ageing, said it would take time to iron out the kinks in the government's new aged care act.

    

    The overhaul of the aged care sector took effect in November 2025, requiring wealthier retirees to pay more for their care and restructuring the way services are funded.

    

    Addressing the National Press Club to launch the council's State of the Older Nation report, the former cabinet minister said while it had only been a few months since the reforms kicked in, people in care had raised a number of complaints.

    

    They included longer waits for home care packages, pressure to sign care agreements quickly, and sharp price rises.

    

    "We expect the government will react to those over time," Mr Pyne said.

    

    He said older people were also being forced to pay for assistance showering.

    

    "Because that's such a big thing, of course, it gets a lot of coverage and a lot of complaints," Mr Pyne said.

    

    The State of the Older Nation report, released by Mr Pyne on Wednesday, includes alarming figures about the number of people experiencing age-based discrimination.

    

    Advertisement

    

    It found 38 per cent of people had experienced ageism since turning 50 and 36 per cent said they didn't want to return to the workforce because of ageism.

    

    "Too often, the story of older Australians is reduced to one of two basic plot lines," Mr Pyne said.

    

    "The first goes like this: older people - often framed as baby boomers - are wealthy, secure, asset rich, sitting on housing, insulated from economic pressure and blocking opportunities for younger generations.

    

    "The second sits at the opposite extreme. Older people, often those in their late 70s and 80s are portrayed as frail dependent in nursing homes, clogging hospital beds and placing an unsustainable burden on public resources."

    

    Mr Pyne said the stereotypes persisted due to convenience, not accuracy.

    

    "They ignore the community and economic contribution of older people, simplify complex policy changes and allow us to avoid uncomfortable conversations about inequality and disadvantage."

    

    Australian Associated Press

Headline: 'Lot of complaints': ex-minister's aged care warning
Full Text:
Older Australians are dealing with big price increases and longer waiting lists for care under major reforms to the aged care sector, a former Liberal minister warns.

    Christopher Pyne, who spent 26 years in federal parliament, has also warned against stereotyping older people as either wealthy baby boomers or frail retirees, describing both framings as "crude caricatures".

    

    Mr Pyne, now the chairman of advocacy group Council on the Ageing, said it would take time to iron out the kinks in the government's new aged care act.

    

    The overhaul of the aged care sector took effect in November 2025, requiring wealthier retirees to pay more for their care and restructuring the way services are funded.

    

    Addressing the National Press Club to launch the council's State of the Older Nation report, the former cabinet minister said while it had only been a few months since the reforms kicked in, people in care had raised a number of complaints.

    

    They included longer waits for home care packages, pressure to sign care agreements quickly, and sharp price rises.

    

    "We expect the government will react to those over time," Mr Pyne said.

    

    He said older people were also being forced to pay for assistance showering.

    

    "Because that's such a big thing, of course, it gets a lot of coverage and a lot of complaints," Mr Pyne said.

    

    The State of the Older Nation report, released by Mr Pyne on Wednesday, includes alarming figures about the number of people experiencing age-based discrimination.

    

    It found 38 per cent of people had experienced ageism since turning 50 and 36 per cent said they didn't want to return to the workforce because of ageism.

    

    "Too often, the story of older Australians is reduced to one of two basic plot lines," Mr Pyne said.

    

    "The first goes like this: older people - often framed as baby boomers - are wealthy, secure, asset rich, sitting on housing, insulated from economic pressure and blocking opportunities for younger generations.

    

    "The second sits at the opposite extreme. Older people, often those in their late 70s and 80s are portrayed as frail dependent in nursing homes, clogging hospital beds and placing an unsustainable burden on public resources."

    

    Mr Pyne said the stereotypes persisted due to convenience, not accuracy.

    

    "They ignore the community and economic contribution of older people, simplify complex policy changes and allow us to avoid uncomfortable conversations about inequality and disadvantage."

    

    Australian Associated Press

Headline: Perth siblings' court case in limbo over NDIS director's overseas bankruptcy
Full Text:
A Supreme Court fight between siblings at the centre of a troubled NDIS provider has taken a fresh turn after a lawyer told the court he could not take instructions from a company's acting director after learning of his overseas bankruptcy.

    

    The proceedings were brought in December by Bila Counselling and Support Services on behalf of director Lenard Swanepoel-Trollip, against his sister Jennifer McAlpine, a former employee and current shareholder of the NDIS-funded disability provider.

    

    The court developments come after this masthead revealed last week that Lenard and his husband Christiaan Swanepoel-Trollip were declared bankrupt in South Africa in 2017 after the collapse of their real estate business, and have since acted as directors and shareholders of several NDIS-related companies in Australia, which is not allowed under Australian law.

    

    The directors were accused of moving client funds from trust accounts to other accounts, and the company entered liquidation before the pair moved to Perth.

    

    The couple have since been connected to two further business collapses in Perth. Lenard, on behalf of the couple, has previously denied any allegation of wrongdoing, fund misappropriation or phoenixing allegations.

    

    In the Supreme Court proceedings, Bila Counselling and Support Services, under Lenard's control, alleged McAlpine misused confidential client information to take clients to a separate company last year.

    

    McAlpine denied the allegations and defended the case, telling this masthead Lenard's bankruptcy history made him ineligible to manage or direct Australian companies.

    

    At the hearing on Wednesday, Supreme Court Justice Matthew Howard granted permission for barrister Ed Fearis to appear for Lenard, who was not in court, for the purpose of explaining the procedural difficulties faced.

    

    Fearis told the court that because he had discovered Lenard was an undischarged bankrupt overseas, he was "incapable of acting as a director" under Australian law.

    

    "Mr Swanepoel-Trollip is unable to authorise [Bila] to authorise proceedings ... the proceedings are currently an irregularity," Fearis told the court.

    

    He told the court that because both Lenard and McAlpine owned 50 per cent of shares each, there was a "deadlock" that complicated things, but would see whether Lenard could advise lawyers as a shareholder.

    

    McAlpine's lawyer Jaeger Man told the court many questions remained, and they would need a "full and frank disclosure" from Lenard, including his residential status.

    

    "It appears on the face of it, [Lenard] managing the company is an offence," Man told the court.

    

    "What is happening with [Bila] at present and NDIS participants? My instructions are that Mr Swanepoel-Trollip is referring people to yet another organisation. There's a lot going on."

    

    The court was told mediation would not proceed unless it was first clarified who was lawfully entitled to act for Bila Counselling and Support Services.

    

    The case was adjourned until February 13.

Headline: Thriving Kids explained: What Labor's NDIS alternative means for you
Full Text:
Finer details of the $4bn Thriving Kids program designed to shift many kids off the NDIS have finally been released. So what does it mean for families, and what questions remain? 

    State and Territory leaders are considering a secret NDIS report as Federal Labor looks to lock in plans for its Thriving Kids program.

    

    Albanese to move children with mild autism off NDIS funding

    

    State and Territory leaders are considering a secret NDIS report as Federal Labor looks to lock in plans for...

    

    The Labor government has finally revealed the proposed model for its $4bn Thriving Kids plan, but parents, stakeholders and educators are still calling for more details.

    

    Thriving Kids will support children aged 8 and under with developmental delay and/or autism with low to moderate support needs.

    

    Health and Disability Minister Mark Butler said the new program would be "easy to access" and won't require a costly and lengthy formal diagnosis.

    

    Its four-pillar design places parents as children's "most important teacher and support".

    

    But questions still remain on the implications for the potentially hundreds of thousands of children set to be on the program, as the ball sits in the states' and territories' court.

    

    The states and territories will deliver Thriving Kids and Mr Butler said the government plans to "wrap up" all bilateral agreements with them by the end of February.

    

    What is Thriving Kids?

    

    Thriving Kids was announced in August 2025 in response to the cost blowout of the National Disability Insurance Scheme (NDIS), which is projected to reach $63.4bn by 2028-29.

    

    It will shift young children aged 8 and under with developmental delay and/or autism with low to moderate support needs from the NDIS into state-led support streams.

    

    The Albanese government says it is confident the program will deliver children the support they need to thrive.

    

    But parents of children on the scheme fear Thriving Kids will remove their agency and control over their child's disability support and place them in a one-size-fits-all model.

    

    Thriving Kids will roll out from October 1 this year, following a three-month delay, with full implementation by January 2028.

    

    The $4bn price tag will be split 50/50 between the federal government and states and territories.

    

    Who will be on Thriving Kids?

    

    While Thriving Kids is designed to ease the pressure on the NDIS by for young children with low to moderate support needs, Mr Butler could not say how many children will be on the scheme.

    

    "In terms of numbers, the obvious place to start is broadly the number of children on the NDIS under the age of nine now with low to moderate support needs, and we can quantify that broadly," he said.

    

    "I want to stress that until 1 January 2028, when Thriving Kids is fully operational, NDIS is still open for those children. There won't be access changes made until the beginning of 2028."

    

    According to the latest NDIS quarterly report, there were 170,786 children younger than nine with an NDIS plan.

    

    But a proportion of these children have a disability beyond the coverage of Thriving Kids.

    

    Children with permanent and significant disability and children aged 8 and under with high support needs will remain eligible for the NDIS.

    

    How will it work?

    

    Mr Butler said Thriving Kids will have "essentially four pillars".

    

    "The first pillar is to raise awareness and early identification of children with developmental issues," Mr Butler said.

    

    "The second is to provide ready access to information, advice, and navigation for parents in particular.

    

    "The third is to build the skills of parents.

    

    "And the fourth pillar is to provide access to targeted support for children and parents who need it.

    

    "Those targeted supports will be provided by trained workers, usually allied health professionals like speech pathologists, occupational therapists, physiotherapists, and the like."

    

    States and territories will be responsible for delivering these supports and services. ?

    

    But the pillars place more responsibility on parents to access and provide support for their children.

    

    What do stakeholders say?

    

    Children and Young People with Disability Australia (CYDA) chief executive Skye Kakoschke-Moore said her organisation was glad to see the final report, but still needed more details.

    

    "The decision-makers are the Commonwealth, states and territories, and they will ultimately decide what will and won't be funded," she said.

    

    "We're waiting for the details of the bilateral agreements to be released.

    

    "What would be really helpful is more information from the states and territories about the sorts of support they want to build on.

    

    "And more information about gaps they should have identified in service type and location."

    

    Autism Association of Australia chief executive Nicole Rogerson said families were still seeking information of what Thriving Kids will look like "on the ground".

    

    "The community have wanted to know more about what Thriving Kids actually means in practice," Ms Rogerson said.

    

    "Today we do have more information, which is welcome, but it also raises new and important questions about how this will work on the ground."

    

    The Royal Australian College of GPs welcomed the release of the plan.

    

    "This is a welcome shift back to putting children and families at the heart of early support," said its president, Dr Michael Wright.

    

    "But this system will only succeed if general practice is properly supported to play its central role in early identification and navigation.

    

    "The model is built on key principles: child and family-centred care, early identification, evidence-based supports, cultural safety, flexible access, and services delivered in children's everyday environments."

    

    Teachers and early educators will be major players in the new model.

    

    Australian Education Union president Correna Haythorpe said she fears the model may increase teachers' workload.

    

    "The AEU welcomes the intent of the Thriving Kids program, but its success will depend entirely on how it is implemented," Ms Haythorpe said.

    

    "Thriving Kids must support teachers, not shift the burden of under-resourced services onto schools."

    

    What's your NDIS experience and will your family be affected by Thriving Kids? Share your story with us at education@news.com.au

story_title: Government Unveils 'Thriving Kids' NDIS Alternative Program
story_summary: The Albanese government has released the model for its $4 billion 'Thriving Kids' program, a major reform designed to shift children aged eight and under with developmental delays or mild autism from the National Disability Insurance Scheme (NDIS) to state-led support systems. Championed by Health Minister Mark Butler, the program aims to provide community-based support and easier access to early intervention without requiring a formal medical diagnosis, in an effort to make the NDIS more sustainable. While the move has been welcomed by some stakeholders, disability groups, parent advocates, and the Australian Education Union have called for more operational details and raised concerns about implementation, parental control, and potential increased workloads for teachers. The program is set for a phased rollout starting in late 2024, with full implementation by 2028.

    "What would be really helpful is more information from the states and territories about the sorts of support they want to build on.

    

    "And more information about gaps they should have identified in service type and location."

    

    Autism Association of Australia chief executive Nicole Rogerson said families were still seeking information of what Thriving Kids will look like "on the ground".

    

    "The community have wanted to know more about what Thriving Kids actually means in practice," Ms Rogerson said.

    

    "Today we do have more information, which is welcome, but it also raises new and important questions about how this will work on the ground."

    

    The Royal Australian College of GPs welcomed the release of the plan.

    

    "This is a welcome shift back to putting children and families at the heart of early support," said its president, Dr Michael Wright.

    

    "But this system will only succeed if general practice is properly supported to play its central role in early identification and navigation.

    

    "The model is built on key principles: child and family-centred care, early identification, evidence-based supports, cultural safety, flexible access, and services delivered in children's everyday environments."

    

    Teachers and early educators will be major players in the new model.

    

    Australian Education Union president Correna Haythorpe said she fears the model may increase teachers' workload.

    

    "The AEU welcomes the intent of the Thriving Kids program, but its success will depend entirely on how it is implemented," Ms Haythorpe said.

    

    "Thriving Kids must support teachers, not shift the burden of under-resourced services onto schools."

    

    What's your NDIS experience and will your family be affected by Thriving Kids? Share your story with us at education@news.com.au

    

    Originally published as Thriving Kids explained: What Labor's NDIS alternative means for you

story_title: Government Unveils 'Thriving Kids' NDIS Alternative Program
story_summary: The Albanese government has released the model for its $4 billion 'Thriving Kids' program, a major reform designed to shift children aged eight and under with developmental delays or mild autism from the National Disability Insurance Scheme (NDIS) to state-led support systems. Championed by Health Minister Mark Butler, the program aims to provide community-based support and easier access to early intervention without requiring a formal medical diagnosis, in an effort to make the NDIS more sustainable. While the move has been welcomed by some stakeholders, disability groups, parent advocates, and the Australian Education Union have called for more operational details and raised concerns about implementation, parental control, and potential increased workloads for teachers. The program is set for a phased rollout starting in late 2024, with full implementation by 2028.

Headline: Boy swims four hours to shore to save family swept out to sea
Full Text:
A mother who sent her 13-year-old son to swim four kilometres back to shore after the family were swept out to sea in rough conditions off WA's

   South West says it was "one of the hardest decisions" she has ever made. The family were holidaying at Quindalup, 250 kilometres south of Perth, when their inflatable paddleboards and kayak were pushed offshore by strong winds on Friday evening. Austin tried to paddle back to shore on his kayak before it took on water in rough seas. He then swam for four more hours to shore and successfully raised the alarm. Naturaliste Marine Rescue commander Paul Bresland said the 47-year-old mother, her 12-year-old son and eight-year-old daughter were eventually found at 8:30pm, clinging to a paddleboard after drifting about 14km offshore.

    

    Reporter: Sinead Mangan

    

    The Australian Government says its Pacific Australia Labour Mobility scheme is a 'win-win" but a new report has found it must address persistent issues of exploitation, lack of basic rights, and policy shortfalls. The scheme employs more than thirty thousand workers from the Pacific and Timor Leste to fill jobs in industries such as agriculture, meat processing and aged care, in regional Australia. Reporter: Lucy Cooper (Townsville)

    

    While Canberra's all abuzz this week with leadership speculation and the contest of ideas, there's another group of Australians headed to Parliament House with ideas of their own. ABC's Heywire summit brings young adults from all across regional Australia to share their stories within the halls of power. And there are two Tasmanians who may very likely have the prime minister's ear as he looks to keep kids off social media, and they look to get more kids outdoors.

    

    Reporter: Michael Black

    

    For thousands of years, First Nations people have journeyed to south east Queensland, to feast on the nuts of a native tree that has survived since the Jurassic period. Today the Kabi Kabi people host Bunya Dreaming, a bi-annual celebration of the iconic Bunya Tree. Reporter: Jeniffer Nichols (Sunshine Coast)

    

    Image Details

    

    Austin swam 4 kilometres to shore to raise the alarm after he and his mother, brother and sister were swept out to sea on their inflatable paddleboards and kayak at Quindalup in WA's South West. ABC News: Briana Shepherd

Headline: NHS bosses called in exorcist to one of its hospices over ‘ghost girl haunting'
Full Text:
NHS chiefs called in an exorcist to one of its hospices – after staff became convinced they were being haunted by the ghost of a young girl.

    Workers at the end-of-life facility, which was previously used as a kids' hospital, feared it was being haunted by spirits of former residents after a string of scary incidents.

    

    The NHS then drafted in the help of "deliverance ministers", commonly known as exorcists, to carry out a religious purging service involving the use of holy oil.

    

    There are about 40 of the holy experts employed by the Church of England.

    

    Their official role is to "rid a person or place of an evil spirit by which they are possessed".

    

    They became a household concept in the 1970s thanks to The Exorcist horror film, which told the tale of a young girl played by Linda Blair who was possessed by a demon who is faced with priests in a battle to rid her of the poltergeist.

    

    The Church of England was contacted about apparent paranormal sightings at the former Priscilla Bacon Lodge, near Colman Hospital in Norwich, where Norfolk Community Health and Care NHS Trust provides specialist end-of-life care.

    

    Up to 1975 the site was Jenny Lind Children's Hospital.

    

    A hospital source has now said some staff believe it was haunted by spirits.

    

    The hospice chaplain emailed the Diocese of Norwich in 2023 and warned "some 'paranormal' incidents" had been "really upsetting staff".

    

    They added they were "awaiting advice or support from the deliverance team".

    

    The chaplain said the ghouls had made it a "difficult time" for employees and asked to obtain holy oil from Norwich Cathedral to carry out a religious ceremony involving staff and patients.

    

    In another email to the diocese sent days later it was confirmed the hospice was in talks with an exorcist.

    

    Details of the communication were uncovered via a Freedom of Information request.

    

    A trust spokesman confirmed a blessing of the building was carried out after the incidents – but they refused to comment on what staff had reported seeing.

    

    They also denied exorcists had visited the site in person.

    

    The Church of England is said to be wary of the practice of carrying out exorcisms.

    

    Strict guidance on them was issued by the Archbishop of Canterbury after a murder in 1974 in Ossett, West Yorkshire.

    

    It saw a butcher slaughter his wife after undergoing an all-night exorcism at his local church.

    

    The guidance ordered deliverance ministers to consult with "medical practitioners, psychologists and psychiatrists" before getting involved in exorcism cases.

    

    A Church of England spokesman said: "Deliverance ministry is a specialist form of pastoral care... rooted in the church's commitment to healing, wholeness and peace for those experiencing distress, whether physical, mental or spiritual.

    

    "It is offered in the context of prayer, sacrament and scripture, and is embedded within the wider ministry of healing."

Headline: Hastie Libs' best chance to halt slide into irrelevance
Full Text:
The Liberal Party is losing support not because Australians have radically changed, but because the Liberals have stopped articulating a clear, confident position on immigration and public safety.

    Recent events have reinforced concerns about screening, enforcement and social cohesion. Yet at precisely this moment, the Liberals appear consumed by internal matters and cautious messaging, leaving the field open to others.

    

    Labor's poor handling of the Bondi tragedy, its support of Palestine, and a pathetic approach to antisemitism have made people less trusting of Labor. The teals and Greens are chasing clouds and hugging trees.

    

    This should be an opportunity for the Liberals, but instead, One Nation is capitalising on voter frustration by offering certainty and conviction.

    

    That drift is not inevitable. The Liberals can reassert relevance by backing firm but fair migration limits, stronger security checks, faster removal of serious offenders, and a clear defence of the law and values.

    

    If the party continues to hesitate, it risks surrendering core ground that it once owned. It also risks falling into the well of irrelevance.

    

    Keith Whiteside, Sippy Downs, Qld

    

    The Australian Labor Party set out to reorganise Australian society along its preferred socialist lines.

    

    Where once families could rear their own children, assist their ageing parents, run school tuck shops, etc, the state will now step in, however poorly.

    

    Both parents are economically forced to join the workforce to house and feed their diminished families, to increase the tax take to provide aged care and daycare for babies and small children, in which many will now be employed for a low wage. Women are postponing pregnancy past optimal age for child-bearing.

    

    Looks like social disintegration. When so much of the workforce is concentrated in social care and government, there are fewer people employed in productive industry to support a socialist state.

    

    A disintegrating society is not likely to fight to preserve itself, and with increasing outside threats, we risk losing everything.

    

    Is there a way back? This is where opposition politicians should be united and concentrating, with a loudly positive strong voice, instead of offering more of the same. We need a rebuild.

    

    Suzanne Blumer, Wembley, WA

    

    Regardless of the leadership position, I want a leader who puts in the hard work, is across the minutiae of policy and knows how to communicate this clearly to the voters.

    

    No amount of charm, good looks or general amiability can replace knowledge, skill and commitment. We desperately need leaders, in all fields, who are prepared to work hard for the common good.

    

    Ann Rennie, Surrey Hills, Vic

    

    Angus Taylor is undoubtedly smart, solid, conservative and all those good things, but what the Liberals need to recapture national support is an intrepid, bold leader with cut-through and charisma. These attributes Taylor does not have but Andrew Hastie does.

    

    This is the time for a leader who provides a stark contrast to Anthony Albanese, a leader prepared to say and stand for the policies most want, including lower immigration, recovery of national pride, respect for our past, economic common sense, equal treatment of all, and protection of our values and traditions.

    

    Brian Barker, Bulimba, Qld

    

    With the unrelenting march of former Coalition supporters to One Nation, it's time for the Liberal Party to exercise the nuclear option.

    

    A leadership transition from Sussan Ley to Angus Taylor to Andrew Hastie is no longer tenable ("Why Angus Taylor is not the answer to the Liberals crisis", 3/2). The party has to demonstrate it is serious about stemming the bleeding on the right and elect Hastie as its leader forthwith.

    

    Kim Keogh, Claremont, WA

    

    It's unbelievable that Sussan Ley has decided on the imposition of a six-month penalty on the three Nationals senators who resigned. This is "go stand in a corner" primary school stuff.

    

    Ley had so little support for her position two days ago – she has even less now. Anthony Albanese wins again.

    

    R. Cooper, Fig Tree Pocket, Qld

Headline: Financial toll hangs heavy over cancer patients
Full Text:
Jacinta Andrews knows all too well that the true cost of cancer extends far beyond medical bills or day treatment.

    Her husband has been battling multiple forms of cancer for decades, and although he is in remission the family continue to navigate the ongoing effects of his treatments, with some procedures costing thousands.

    

    While the public healthcare system covers a range of care, many patients still face the financial burden of cancer, research has found.

    

    Cancer Council Australia's national survey published on Wednesday found that nearly 99 per cent of Australians diagnosed with cancer incur out-of-pocket expenses, even when accessing public care.

    

    Tony Andrews was first diagnosed with head and neck cancer in 2009, forcing Ms Andrews to act as a full-time carer, assisting with daily transport, medical treatments and emotional wellbeing.

    

    While Ms Andrews was able to take paid time off, her husband was self-employed, and wasn't eligible for assistance.

    

    She considers herself lucky though that she could take leave, adding that they wouldn't have been able to pay their mortgage if it wasn't for her supportive workplace.

    

    The family were rocked by cancer again in 2023, after Mr Andrews was diagnosed with non-Hodgkin's lymphoma, which they are still navigating.

    

    Although Mr Andrews remains in remission, the side effects of his treatment have proved costly.

    

    "There are issues that continue and the latest ongoing thing since 2010, is the severity of the radiation treatment had on his gums and teeth," she told AAP.

    

    "We have had a lot of dental costs, and we can't afford private health insurance."

    

    Ms Andrews said the side effects on his teeth was unexpected, with her husband now struggling to eat.

    

    "We are saving up for him to get dentures or plates," she said.

    

    "We didn't know this was going to happen and you don't think about that stuff at the time of treatment, because you are just trying to survive."

    

    Cancer Council analysis found that loss of income or disruption to employment is the most common financial cost, affecting more than a third of Australians diagnosed with cancer.

    

    Surgical expenses (23.7 per cent ) and insurance gap payments (23.6 per cent) represented the largest out-of-pocket cost for nearly one quarter of respondents.

    

    "It is a huge burden for people," Cancer Council's Peter Diamond told AAP.

    

    "These costs can include both direct medical costs and indirect expenses, which people experience regardless of what stage (of) cancer diagnosis."

    

    Australians should be able to access high-quality cancer care, based on what best meets their needs, not what they can afford, Dr Diamond added.

    

    The research coincides with World Cancer Day and calls for increased access to financial counselling services for Australians affected by cancer through a mandatory industry-funded model, alongside greater investment from federal, state and territory governments.

    

    Australian Associated Press

Headline: Financial toll hangs heavy over cancer patients
Full Text:
Jacinta Andrews knows all too well that the true cost of cancer extends far beyond medical bills or day treatment.

    Her husband has been battling multiple forms of cancer for decades, and although he is in remission the family continue to navigate the ongoing effects of his treatments, with some procedures costing thousands.

    

    While the public healthcare system covers a range of care, many patients still face the financial burden of cancer, research has found.

    

    Cancer Council Australia's national survey published on Wednesday found that nearly 99 per cent of Australians diagnosed with cancer incur out-of-pocket expenses, even when accessing public care.

    

    Advertisement

    

    Tony Andrews was first diagnosed with head and neck cancer in 2009, forcing Ms Andrews to act as a full-time carer, assisting with daily transport, medical treatments and emotional wellbeing.

    

    While Ms Andrews was able to take paid time off, her husband was self-employed, and wasn't eligible for assistance.

    

    She considers herself lucky though that she could take leave, adding that they wouldn't have been able to pay their mortgage if it wasn't for her supportive workplace.

    

    The family were rocked by cancer again in 2023, after Mr Andrews was diagnosed with non-Hodgkin's lymphoma, which they are still navigating.

    

    Although Mr Andrews remains in remission, the side effects of his treatment have proved costly.

    

    "There are issues that continue and the latest ongoing thing since 2010, is the severity of the radiation treatment had on his gums and teeth," she told AAP.

    

    "We have had a lot of dental costs, and we can't afford private health insurance."

    

    Ms Andrews said the side effects on his teeth was unexpected, with her husband now struggling to eat.

    

    "We are saving up for him to get dentures or plates," she said.

    

    "We didn't know this was going to happen and you don't think about that stuff at the time of treatment, because you are just trying to survive."

    

    Cancer Council analysis found that loss of income or disruption to employment is the most common financial cost, affecting more than a third of Australians diagnosed with cancer.

    

    Surgical expenses (23.7 per cent ) and insurance gap payments (23.6 per cent) represented the largest out-of-pocket cost for nearly one quarter of respondents.

    

    Advertisement

    

    "It is a huge burden for people," Cancer Council's Peter Diamond told AAP.

    

    "These costs can include both direct medical costs and indirect expenses, which people experience regardless of what stage (of) cancer diagnosis."

    

    Australians should be able to access high-quality cancer care, based on what best meets their needs, not what they can afford, Dr Diamond added.

    

    The research coincides with World Cancer Day and calls for increased access to financial counselling services for Australians affected by cancer through a mandatory industry-funded model, alongside greater investment from federal, state and territory governments.

    

    Australian Associated Press
