story_title: NDIS Provider Court Case Stalls Over Director's Bankruptcy
story_summary: A Supreme Court dispute involving the troubled NDIS provider Bila Counselling and Support Services has been complicated by the revelation that its acting director, Lenard Swanepoel-Trollip, is an undischarged bankrupt from South Africa. This makes him legally ineligible to manage or direct Australian companies. The legal proceedings, initiated by the company against his sister and shareholder Jennifer McAlpine over alleged misuse of client data, were deemed an 'irregularity' as he could not lawfully provide instructions. The case has been adjourned as the court seeks to clarify who can legally act for the company, amid concerns about the management of NDIS participants' funds.

Headline: Thriving Kids explained: What Labor's NDIS alternative means for you
Full Text:
Finer details of the $4bn Thriving Kids program designed to shift many kids off the NDIS have finally been released. So what does it mean for families, and what questions remain?

    The Labor government has finally revealed the proposed model for its $4bn Thriving Kids plan, but parents, stakeholders and educators are still calling for more details.

    

    Thriving Kids will support children aged 8 and under with developmental delay and/or autism with low to moderate support needs.

    

    Health and Disability Minister Mark Butler said the new program would be "easy to access" and won't require a costly and lengthy formal diagnosis.

    

    Its four-pillar design places parents as children's "most important teacher and support".

    

    But questions still remain on the implications for the potentially hundreds of thousands of children set to be on the program, as the ball sits in the states' and territories' court.

    

    The states and territories will deliver Thriving Kids and Mr Butler said the government plans to "wrap up" all bilateral agreements with them by the end of February.

    

    What is Thriving Kids?

    

    Thriving Kids was announced in August 2025 in response to the cost blowout of the National Disability Insurance Scheme (NDIS), which is projected to reach $63.4bn by 2028-29.

    

    It will shift young children aged 8 and under with developmental delay and/or autism with low to moderate support needs from the NDIS into state-led support streams.

    

    The Albanese government says it is confident the program will deliver children the support they need to thrive.

    

    But parents of children on the scheme fear Thriving Kids will remove their agency and control over their child's disability support and place them in a one-size-fits-all model.

    

    Thriving Kids will roll out from October 1 this year, following a three-month delay, with full implementation by January 2028.

    

    The $4bn price tag will be split 50/50 between the federal government and states and territories.

    

    Who will be on Thriving Kids?

    

    While Thriving Kids is designed to ease the pressure on the NDIS by for young children with low to moderate support needs, Mr Butler could not say how many children will be on the scheme.

    

    "In terms of numbers, the obvious place to start is broadly the number of children on the NDIS under the age of nine now with low to moderate support needs, and we can quantify that broadly," he said.

    

    "I want to stress that until 1 January 2028, when Thriving Kids is fully operational, NDIS is still open for those children. There won't be access changes made until the beginning of 2028."

    

    According to the latest NDIS quarterly report, there were 170,786 children younger than nine with an NDIS plan.

    

    But a proportion of these children have a disability beyond the coverage of Thriving Kids.

    

    Children with permanent and significant disability and children aged 8 and under with high support needs will remain eligible for the NDIS.

    

    How will it work?

    

    Mr Butler said Thriving Kids will have "essentially four pillars".

    

    "The first pillar is to raise awareness and early identification of children with developmental issues," Mr Butler said.

    

    "The second is to provide ready access to information, advice, and navigation for parents in particular.

    

    "The third is to build the skills of parents.

    

    "And the fourth pillar is to provide access to targeted support for children and parents who need it.

    

    "Those targeted supports will be provided by trained workers, usually allied health professionals like speech pathologists, occupational therapists, physiotherapists, and the like."

    

    States and territories will be responsible for delivering these supports and services. ?

    

    But the pillars place more responsibility on parents to access and provide support for their children.

    

    What do stakeholders say?

    

    Children and Young People with Disability Australia (CYDA) chief executive Skye Kakoschke-Moore said her organisation was glad to see the final report, but still needed more details.

    

    "The decision-makers are the Commonwealth, states and territories, and they will ultimately decide what will and won't be funded," she said.

    

    "We're waiting for the details of the bilateral agreements to be released.

    

    "What would be really helpful is more information from the states and territories about the sorts of support they want to build on.

    

    "And more information about gaps they should have identified in service type and location."

    

    Autism Association of Australia chief executive Nicole Rogerson said families were still seeking information of what Thriving Kids will look like "on the ground".

    

    "The community have wanted to know more about what Thriving Kids actually means in practice," Ms Rogerson said.

    

    "Today we do have more information, which is welcome, but it also raises new and important questions about how this will work on the ground."

    

    The Royal Australian College of GPs welcomed the release of the plan.

    

    "This is a welcome shift back to putting children and families at the heart of early support," said its president, Dr Michael Wright.

    

    "But this system will only succeed if general practice is properly supported to play its central role in early identification and navigation.

    

    "The model is built on key principles: child and family-centred care, early identification, evidence-based supports, cultural safety, flexible access, and services delivered in children's everyday environments."

    

    Teachers and early educators will be major players in the new model.

    

    Australian Education Union president Correna Haythorpe said she fears the model may increase teachers' workload.

    

    "The AEU welcomes the intent of the Thriving Kids program, but its success will depend entirely on how it is implemented," Ms Haythorpe said.

    

    "Thriving Kids must support teachers, not shift the burden of under-resourced services onto schools."

    

    What's your NDIS experience and will your family be affected by Thriving Kids? Share your story with us at education@news.com.au

    

    Originally published as Thriving Kids explained: What Labor's NDIS alternative means for you

The program is set for a phased rollout starting in late 2024, with full implementation by 2028.

Headline: Cutting capital gains deductions opens door to bigger cuts
Full Text:
A reduction in the capital gains tax discount is firming as the expected centrepiece of Jim Chalmers' May budget, alongside a broader suite of

   reforms including possible tax cuts aimed at helping younger Australians buy their own home.

    

    A senior government source, who asked not to be named so they could detail internal policy discussions, said possible changes to the capital gains tax in the forthcoming May budget were not advanced but confirmed a change had not been ruled out.

    

    Reducing the concession to 33 per cent from 50 per cent is estimated by Deloitte Access Economics to raise about $4 billion a year in extra tax by 2035-36.

    

    Crucially, the source said Prime Minister Anthony Albanese – who has at times been averse to some of the tax changes pushed internally by Treasurer Jim Chalmers – had not ruled out changes to the tax.

    

    "The key for housing is more supply, but there are perceptions as well about tax," one MP said.

    

    "Overwhelmingly, we are looking at supply, but we are also looking at it [CGT] and the budget situation."

    

    The government is under pressure to use the May budget to both reduce the overall rate of growth in spending and to find ways to lift the pace at which the economy can grow without adding to inflationary pressures.

    

    Changes to the capital gains tax, which is the focus of a Greens-led Senate inquiry due to report next month, have been canvassed within Labor for several months as one way to improve the tax system while helping younger Australians get into the property market.

    

    The extra revenue is likely to be channelled toward reducing taxes in other areas.

    

    The current 50 per cent concession for assets held for at least 12 months was introduced in 1999. At the time, proponents argued it would encourage people to buy shares, boosting Australian publicly listed companies.

    

    Instead, most investors went into property. Before the change, most landlords made money from their investments, but after the concession, most became negatively geared.

    

    This masthead revealed before the 2025 budget that the government had asked the Treasury to model the impact of concessions, though it later walked away from the proposal.

    

    Labor MP Mike Freelander, who holds the Sydney seat of Macarthur, said that "I have always felt it [the 50 per cent capital gains tax discount] is too generous".

    

    "I have benefited from it a lot, so it's a no-brainer to me. There are good economic reasons why it should be wound back a bit," he said.

    

    "I understand that people worry about the consequences, but I think it should certainly be considered."

    

    A second Labor MP, who asked not to be named, said that housing affordability "is only getting worse and the impatience of people locked out of the housing market will only grow as more people are locked out".

    

    "I think people overwhelmingly support us building homes, but structurally, they think that the housing sector is unfair," they said.

    

    The $4 billion estimate by Deloitte Access Economics is based on reducing the concession for all assets. One option being considered is reducing the concession on property, leaving it intact for other assets, such as shares.

    

    Another option is to cut the concession to 25 per cent, a position Labor took to the 2016 and 2019 elections. Other issues being examined include grandfathering any change to ensure investors who bought an asset under the current law would not be disadvantaged.

    

    One option being canvassed is to increase the size of income tax cuts that are due to start from July 1.

    

    Worth up to $5 a week, the current tax cut is modest, only a little more than the famous "sandwich and a milkshake" tax cuts delivered by the Howard government in 2003.

    

    Chalmers said the May budget would focus on ways to make the economy more productive.

    

    The treasurer, who last week told The Monthly magazine that the inability of young people to buy a home was one of Australia's defining issues, signalled that further tax reform was on the government's agenda.

    

    "Any further changes to taxes, beyond those we've already flagged, would be a matter for cabinet in the usual way, and would be consistent with the reform directions that we set out after the reform roundtable a few months ago," he said on Wednesday.

    

    "We've made it clear we've got other priorities when it comes to tax reform. The focus in tax reform is on cutting income taxes."

    

    While the Liberal Party has signalled opposition to a change in the CGT, it would probably win support from the Greens, who have long argued the concession encourages speculation by investors in the property market at the expense of young buyers.

    

    Teal independent MP Allegra Spender said Chalmers had to use the May budget to deliver real reform, including changes to the mix of federal taxes and cuts to government spending.

    

    She said policies such as the EV tax deal had to be on the chopping block, while spending growth on the NDIS was still too high.

    

    "Real reform has to have winners and losers. We're beyond the point where everyone can be a winner," she said.

    

    The current CGT discount applies to all assets, including shares, cryptocurrencies and those held within superannuation funds.

    

    The Association of Australian Superannuation Funds, in a submission to the current Senate inquiry into the capital gains tax, has warned that removing the 50 per cent discount for super funds would lift the tax payable on all unrealised capital gains from 10 per cent to 15 per cent.

    

    The association said the discount had been "instrumental in encouraging superannuation fund

    

    investment across a multitude of key areas across Australia's domestic economy".

    

    Cut through the noise of federal politics with news, views and expert analysis.

Headline: AUKUS proves expensive, sovereignty-losing mistake
Full Text:
February 3, 2026 — 8:00pm

    

    To submit a letter to The Age , email letters@theage.com.au . Please include your home address and telephone number below your letter. No attachments. See here for our rules and tips on getting your letter published.

    

    DEFENCE

    

    Dr Margaret Beavis' opinion article ? ?Let's stop pretending AUKUS makes us safer ??, 3/2 presents clear and evidence based reasoning for review of AUKUS.

    

    The issue is complex, but one thing is clear. There is an unprecedented number of thoughtful, independent and experienced people, from different professions, backgrounds and political sympathies calling for review.

    

    Why are all these different people asking these same questions? It is time for political leadership to show respect and address these concerns with a mature and evidence-based review.

    

    Ann Birrell, St Kilda West

    

    You wouldn't buy a car on these terms

    

    I completely agree with the points made about AUKUS by Margaret Beavis in her opinion article. I don't think we should proceed. An additional, important point in my mind is that there appears to be no clear contractual arrangements in place concerning price and delivery dates.

    

    I do not believe one should be making any large purchase without those arrangements, nor an understanding of having clear sovereign ownership once the submarines are delivered.

    

    I would certainly not buy a car, for example, without those understandings.

    

    Phil Ritchie, Balaclava

    

    If Trump is furious, so be it

    

    Dr Beavis is surely correct in her assessment of the value of AUKUS to Australia There is none. The question is, how can the government extricate itself from this worst ever defence 'deal' without making Trump furious.

    

    First, I suggest it's time we borrowed from Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney's recent approach and start telling the truth and stop deluding ourselves. Second, tell the Americans we're not doing it: Not doing the Virginia class, not doing the bases, not doing the nuclear armed subs in our ports.

    

    They will be furious, but at the end of the day it's our country to defend in the best way possible. AUKUS is a dangerous, expensive and a sovereignty-losing mistake.

    

    Greg Gardiner, Brunswick

    

    A deal unlikely to yield real benefit

    

    Congratulations to Dr Beavis on her assessment of AUKUS. The treaty will never yield any meaningful benefit to Australia and will cost our taxpayers billions that could be better spent.

    

    We should cut our losses and stop pretending that the US will spend any effort or money on defending Australia until the unlikely day that it has all the military hardware it thinks it needs for itself.

    

    Juliet Flesch, Kew

    

    Time to stand up to bullies

    

    Canada's Prime Minister Carney recently showed courage and leadership at Davos, Switzerland, in standing up to Trump and calling out to other middle powers to join ranks.

    

    Our prime minister, in his dithering on whether or not to join Trump's "Board of Peace" in case Trump is offended, is beyond pathetic.

    

    You stand up to bullies. Trump may rage and threaten to put 1000 per cent tariffs on Australia, a country that has a trade deficit with America, but he has moved on by the morning.

    

    Besides, does anyone think that he would want to forego the billion or so in tributes (AUKUS) that this vassal state pays to the emperor every few months?

    

    Barry Lizmore, Ocean Grove

    

    Australia not alone

    

    Your correspondent (Letters, ??Dumb luck??, ) repeats the line, "Australia is a lucky country run by second-rate people". Can anyone name a country that is presently run by first-rate people?

    

    Jim Picot, Altona

    

    THE FORUM

    

    Irreconcilable differences

    

    For a successful coalition of two political parties, that union needs to be underpinned by a degree of common ground and similar values, with negotiation taking place in the grey areas.

    

    Once upon a time, this may have been true for the Liberal Party and the Nationals, but in recent years their values and policies have grown further and further apart, making it a coalition in name only, and a recipe for irreconcilable conflict over almost every issue.

    

    As much as it might seem politically expedient in order "to get the numbers", the expression "flogging a dead horse" springs to mind.

    

    Claire Merry, Wantirna

    

    Liberals guiding star

    

    George Brandis correctly points out that currently the Liberal Party has never been so weak. "Today it holds only eight of the 89 seats the Australian Electoral Commission classifies as "metropolitan"; just four in Sydney, two in Melbourne, one in Brisbane and one in Perth" ("Nats, not Libs, face existential threat", 2/2).

    

    Meanwhile, most of us know the reasoning for this extremely embarrassing situation – a total lack of coherent, relevant, and much-needed policies. This is nothing new.

    

    Yet Brandis does not offer up a single worthwhile policy, or even conceding that herein lay a problem. When all said is done, the one solitary policy that connects this tenuous union is the only guiding star in their very small galaxy: it is the Liberals' one and only aim, keeping Labor out of power.

    

    Keith Brown, Southbank

    

    Rates and rises

    

    The Reserve Bank's interest rate decision, in part determined by the unemployment rate, is flawed due to the use of selective data. Unlike the Roy Morgan Index, which measures unemployment as anyone else would - by who has entered or left a job - the ABS relies upon narrower definitions. 4.1 per cent sounds good when you've excluded many unemployed people, labelling them as "out of the labour force".

    

    Therefore, the rate sometimes includes only a fifth of those un- or underemployed. The last Roy Morgan data, published in September 2025, held the "real" unemployment rate at 11.1 per cent. If this was what the Reserve Bank used, we might see a fairer society.

    

    Anders Ross, Heidelberg

    

    Labor's donations

    

    The AEC data 3/2) on last financial year's political donations highlight the current funding balance, Coalition/Labor: $221m/$160m. Much of Labor's financial support comes from unions. However, its connection to unions, some of which, such as the CFMEU, have been seen as corrupt.

    

    Some Age correspondents encourage the formation of a centre-right party to challenge Labor, particularly with this in mind (Letters, ). Yet, many current working-class retirees will remember the support for pay and conditions their decent union provided and will most likely continue voting for Labor.

    

    Most current workers on big projects are also appreciative of this protection and should be aware of the Labor connection. I'm sure Labor will continue to fund a campaign with this generous union support.

    

    Andrew Smith, Leongatha

    

    Keep VicHealth

    

    Re " Vow to save VicHealth causes headache for state government ", 3/2.

    

    What a relief that the state government might be blocked from abolishing VicHealth.

    

    This beacon of preventive medicine, with its world-class programs such as the Quit campaign, has helped save many thousands of lives. Not to mention the massive slap over the knuckles this inflicted on greedy, ruthless big tobacco companies.

    

    I remember meeting Tim Read, MP for Brunswick, when he was a young medical student in the early 1980s. Even then he was a compassionate campaigner for public health and preventive medicine. Under Tim's watch, no garish billboard advertising cigarette smoking was safe from quippy, thoughtful, eye-catching graffiti bombardment. Well done, Tim and the best of luck with your own health now.

    

    Annie Mullarvey, Brunswick East

    

    NDIS tragedy

    

    Re " NDIS funding cut from children found dead in Perth, friends say " (1/2). Funding problems are no surprise since many patients are dumped into the system from state-funded hospitals onto the federally funded NDIS.

    

    Having had distressing personal experience of the system of attempts to move an incurable patient from a hospital into the NDIS – and the manpower spent manufacturing reports and attending hearings – there is great need for reform.

    

    Problems with the current system could arguably be sheeted home to the state hospital system of dumping patients into the NDIS, thus weakening it.

    

    Doris LeRoy, Altona

    

    City protesters

    

    Re Letters. ??City protest??, . I wonder if an elderly Jewish person shares your correspondent's sense of safety if they too, were to walk home from the CBD due to the inconvenience of the protesters?

    

    Michael Carroll, Kensington

    

    Trump the time waster

    

    When the US president hangs a photo of himself with war criminal Vladimir Putin in the White House, he is asking to be treated accordingly. Perhaps in his mind, he is a fellow strong man, but in our minds, he is a lost cause.

    

    Donald Trump has got away with things because he has never been held to account. It's time to back Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney and humour him no more.

    

    He is wasting everyone's time doing deals over Ukraine that Putin will never accept as he continues to bomb maternity wards and schools with impunity.

    

    Tony Newport, Hillwood, WA

    

    Europe must act

    

    Putin started the invasion of Ukraine 12 years ago when he took over Crimea. The failure of Europe to put a stop to that invasion has led to the continuing invasion of the rest of Ukraine. This will continue until western Europe uses its superior numbers to take the fight to Putin without worrying about whatever thought Trump may have from one day to the next.

    

    Loch Wilson, Northcote

    

    Dream time

    

    Wouldn't it be wonderful if world leaders stopped forcing actions on other countries and instead engaged in ??rational discussions?? leading to ??agreed outcomes??.

    

    Meg Paul, Camberwell

    

    Virtue signalling

    

    The continual flow of letters to The Age from progressive voters decrying the 'deplorables' who have created Pauline Hanson, Donald Trump and Nigel Farage, are obviously virtue signalling their higher moral status. These correspondents need the above trio to validate their moral perfection.

    

    Progressives' incessant demands for correcting perceived inequality, mindless acceptance of 'identity' over fact and endless calls to shut-down debate by calling it divisive, or inventing words that tack on 'phobia' has created these populists. Although the progressive media never use the term, the far-left is the harbinger of the far-right. For every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction.

    

    Mike Pantzopoulos, Ashburton

    

    Move it to MCG

    

    After reading ?? Radical plan to reshape Open ?? 2/2, I was inspired by Craig Tiley's plans for the Australian Open. Imagine having a Cosm Dallas-style dome in Melbourne!

    

    Tiley did not mention who is going to pay for the ??interactive glass court for fans, an immersive dome with 270-degree screens, and an even greater emphasis on fashion, beauty and wellness??. My guess is it will be the public taxpayer who build the rest of the tennis complex.

    

    Unfortunately, the average taxpayer will have little or no chance of attending the men's or women's finals given the high ticket prices. What about moving the finals to the MCG where 100,000 can attend at reasonable prices rather than the A-listers featured prominently in an affiliated publication to The Age?

    

    Sandy McKinnon, Patterson Lakes

    

    Goodbye, good luck

    

    Australian Open chief Craig Tiley wants to turn the Australian Open venue into a replica of 'domes' like those in Dallas, Los Angeles and Las Vegas (?? Radical plan to reshape Open ??, 2/2 ).

    

    He wants to grab more real estate on Swan Street and Olympic Boulevard, to expand onto Gosch's Paddock, AAMI Park and the MCG.

    

    I hope he gets to realise his ambition to modernise the US Open – and just leave Yarra Park as it is.

    

    Goodbye Craig, and good luck in the Trump USA.

    

    John Robert, Surrey Hills

    

    Not a grand slam

    

    Congratulations to Tennis Australia and Channel Nine for a very entertaining Australian Open. The opening ceremony re-engaged me with tennis, but I have a gripe with some commentators (and former players) with their regular reference to players winning grand slams.

    

    One commentator stated Carlos Alcaraz had won seven. If this were true, Alcaraz would have won every grand slam tournament/title (Australian Open, French Open, Wimbledon, and US Open) for the last seven years running – from when he was aged 15.

    

    Such inconsistent and inaccurate use of the term 'grand slam' only serves to confuse the public. A grand slam is winning the four major titles in one calendar year. Commentators, please stop diminishing the achievement of those who have actually accomplished a grand slam. Who knows, Alcaraz may go on to win the three further major titles this year and indeed achieve the grand slam himself.

    

    Wendi Kruger, Croydon South

    

    First Lady

    

    There's probably a very interesting documentary to be made about the wife of the US president but I suspect "Melania" is not it.

    

    Terry Kelly, Coburg

    

    AND ANOTHER THING

    

    Politics

    

    Can you imagine Cory Bernardi, Barnaby Joyce and Pauline Hanson all sticking to the same party line? Only time will tell. What chance of success for One Nation? I thought minuscule, until I saw that Angus Taylor is a chance

    

    to take over leadership of the Liberal Party.

    

    Paul Gooley, Ringwood East

    

    Re Letters ( ): I'm happy that Toto's master is the man standing without a strong and robust political opposition. Imagine if we had Dutton, Abbott, Morrison, Barnaby or Hanson in his place? Count your blessings folks.

    

    Catherine Ross, Sandringham

    

    Your correspondent (Letters, ) seeks a "movement grounded in social justice, ethical politics, climate change??. Luckily, such a political party already exists! It's called the Greens.

    

    Simon Thornton, Alphington

    

    Furthermore

    

    One-hundred police for five days to find a suspect they presume dead? What a total waste of resources and money. This sort of decision does nothing to give me faith in the new police commissioner.

    

    Jan Salib, Moonee Ponds

    

    Prime Minister Albanese, your decision to invite President Isaac Herzog could be a positive one if you can convince him to have Israel return to the fold of international law-abiding countries.

    

    Roger Christiansz, Wheelers Hill

    

    Re " Exclusive Brethren, Rinehart donate to right-wing Advance " (3/2): If the coal lobby can afford to flood politics with donations, it can afford to clean up its climate pollution.

    

    Larni Dibben, Glen Iris

    

    There has been much talk about "Karens" (Letters, ). It always strikes me as very odd and not a little unfair that this put-down

    

    only ever applies to women. It's time we started applying a similar sobriquet to male Karens of whom there are many. May I suggest the diminutive of Richard?

    

    Judy Hungerford, Kew

    

    Finally

    

    A statue of the Djoker? In the words of inimitable John McEnroe, "You cannot be serious!"

    

    Barry Greer, Balnarring

Headline: Nine years after concerns were raised, Mater staff feel action will occur
Full Text:
Staff rally at the Calvary Mater Newcastle to fix the mould problem. Inset: A closed ward due to mould. Picture by Peter Lorimer

    NSW Minister for Health Ryan Park at the Calvary Mater Hospital to deal with the mould debacle on Monday. Picture by Peter Lorimer

    

    A ward closed on level 5 due to mould in early January. Picture supplied

    

    NSW Minister for Health Ryan Park at the Calvary Mater Hospital to deal with the mould debacle on Monday. Picture by Peter Lorimer

    

    A ward closed on level 5 due to mould in early January. Picture supplied

    

    Mould at the Calvary Mater Newcastle.

    

    Water leaks at the Calvary Mater Newcastle.

    

    A sign at last week's rally at the Calvary Mater Newcastle. Picture supplied

    

    The auxiliary ladies store room door, where mould was found. Picture supplied

    

    A ward closed on level 5 due to mould in early January. Picture supplied

    

    A ward closed on level 5 due to mould in early January. Picture supplied

    

    Staff rally at the Calvary Mater Newcastle to fix the mould problem. Picture by Peter Lorimer

    

    Login or signup to continue reading

    

    $1/week for the first 4 weeks

    

    In NSW Parliament on Tuesday, the Opposition and Greens put pressure on the Minns government to ensure it follows through on promised action.

    

    Greens MP Amanda Cohn said "there were critical safety and maintenance issues at the Calvary Mater hospital that were reported by staff through the proper channels as early as 2017".

    

    "It has taken years for there to be the attention on this issue that it deserves," Dr Cohn said.

    

    Staff estimate the systemic mould issue has been occurring for at least a year, but water leaks and other maintenance trouble had been happening for years.

    

    Shadow Health Minister Sarah Mitchell said "the problem we have is there are repeated incidents every week".

    

    "The staff up there literally told the Newcastle Herald it feels like an episode of Stranger Things . This is not what a health service should look like in 2026."

    

    After meeting NSW Health Minister Ryan Park at the hospital on Monday, staff "felt heard for the first time and that action was going to be taken", one staffer said.

    

    "His remarks and obvious frustration are exactly our experience when dealing with the PPP [public-private partnership]."

    

    As for fixing the mould problem, Mr Park said "it has to happen".

    

    "I'm determined to do it as fast as I possibly can," he said.

    

    The Newcastle Herald reported on Monday that most of the intensive care unit and rooms in three wards were shut at the weekend for "deep cleaning".

    

    These areas had "slightly elevated levels" of the mould Aspergillus fumigatus

    

    The hospital also closed two wards in January due to mould in the air-conditioning ducts and maggots falling from the ceiling onto patients.

    

    This included the closure of a haematology day ward - also known as ward 5D - with cancer patients moved to another ward.

    

    Mr Park said, "I am deeply angry and sorry that staff are in this position".

    

    "They shouldn't have to be worrying about trying to manage this situation with private contractors who should be doing their job."

    

    The Mater is a public hospital run by a private company called Novacare, which is responsible for the building maintenance and cleaning.

    

    Honeywell is also responsible for the hospital's maintenance, as one of four companies that make up Novacare, along with Westpac Bank, Medirest and Abigroup.

    

    Mr Park said the government was looking at legal options and would "hold the contractors to account".

    

    Late last year, the minister "brought in the contractors on multiple occasions at my office in Sydney and at Parliament House".

    

    "I'd been given the clear impression and undertaking that there was a pathway forward to deal particularly with the cladding, which will go a long way to deal with the water and mould-related issues.

    

    "Over the summer break, it was clear - although there'd been scaffolding put up - that work was not happening."

    

    Mr Park said he and NSW Health secretary Susan Pearce made a decision to "step into" the PPP contract and "begin the cladding rectification works".

    

    Staff say the cladding will help, but the mould was "being caused by the air-conditioning system and humidity".

    

    Wallsend MP Sonia Hornery said "works on the mould infested air-conditioning have begun".

Headline: Inside the new $411 million ‘purpose-built' St George Hospital redevelopment
Full Text:
A southern Sydney hospital's $411 million redevelopment is complete, with a new nine-storey building and upgraded campus set to open within months and provide enhanced services.

    A major hospital upgrade in Sydney's south has been completed to the tune of $411 million after a year of major construction.

    

    The third stage in the redevelopment of St George Hospital is complete, with its doors expected to open to the public within months.

    

    The new facilities in the hospital's redevelopment include pathology, aged care, behavioural support, palliative care, MRI and nuclear medicine.

    

    The nine-storey Kensington Street Building capped out the project, housing community and outpatient services.

    

    Previous stages of the development delivered a new Emergency Department, an acute services building, upgraded birthing suite and operating theatre. The initial stages of development began more than a decade ago.

    

    Health Minister Ryan Park said he's looking forward to opening the Kensington Street Building which will "bring together a range of ambulatory, outpatient and community services to provide better health outcomes".

    

    "It is so important to provide our dedicated health staff with a modern, purpose-built health facility, which caters to the specific needs of patients, families, and clinicians," he said.

    

    "We are getting on with the job by delivering more hospitals, more beds and more services to ensure patients and their families receive the care they need, when they need it."

    

    In the new facilities, patients recovering from brain injuries will be able to access structured rehabilitation for the first time at the hospital.

    

    The St George Hospital redevelopment is one of 32 new and upgraded hospital projects underway by the government, including Rouse Hill Hospital, Bankstown Hospital and Blacktown and Mount Druitt Hospital.

    

    Originally published as Inside the new $411 million 'purpose-built' St George Hospital redevelopment

Headline: ‘Chicken feed': Renewables' tiny ESVF tax contribution revealed
Full Text:
Victoria's renewable energy giants will contribute just $600,000 to emergency services funding while households and businesses pay over $1.5 billion this year.

    Victoria's renewable energy giants will contribute just $600,000 to fund Victoria's emergency services this financial year, while the state's drought-affected farmers will cough up about $71m.

    

    On July 1 last year, the Allan Government rebadged the fire services property levy as a new emergency services volunteer fund, doubling the variable component of the charge and lifting its tax take on all Victorian households and businesses from $1.03bn to $1.55bn.

    

    But the government left the levy rate on wind, solar and battery developers unchanged, at 5.7 cents per $1000 of their assets' capital improved value - the same discounted public benefit rate that is applied to schools, hospitals, libraries and fire stations.

    

    It means the state's wind and solar farms will contribute about $570,000 to $630,000 in ESVF tax this financial year, based on a combined CIV of their projects of between $10bn and $11bn.

    

    Glendemar sheep producer Ben Duxson said the government was "rolling out the diamond encrusted red carpet" for predominantly internationally-owned wind and solar developers.

    

    Meanwhile, the government has cranked up the levy on every Victorian business from 66.4 cents per $1000 of their properties' value in 2024-25 to $1.33/$1000 CIV and for households from 8.7c/$1000 CIV to 17.3c/$1000 CIV.

    

    Even Victoria's drought-affected farmers, who succeeded in getting the government to back down on lifting their levy rate from 28.7c/$1000 CIV to 83c/$1000 CIV this financial year, must still pay out about $71m in ESVF tax.

    

    It's the second time the government has decided to keep the renewables sector's contribution to the emergency services at such low levels, despite protests from farmers and CFA volunteers.

    

    The Clean Energy Council was asked if it believed it was equitable for solar, wind and battery developers to pay just 5.7c/$1000 CIV.

    

    CEC chief policy and impact officer William Churchill said "we remain committed to contributing our share to the Emergency Services Fund in support of regional communities.

    

    "Our sector is part of the community and many projects make additional contributions to the CFA through benefit sharing schemes as well as staff also volunteering for local CFA brigades."

    

    But Traynors Lagoon CFA volunteer Colin Coates said the renewable sector's contribution was "chicken feed".

    

    "The government gives these companies an easy ride, while we provide the trucks and time."

    

    Energy Minister Lily D'Ambrosio failed to respond to questions on the issue, but the government has previously stated that applying a lower levy rate to the renewables sector was all about "keeping the lights on and power prices low as our ageing coal-fired generators close".

    

    If the renewables sector was paying the same levy rate as commercial and industrial property owners, the government would have raked in another $13.3m and $14.6m - or, enough to replace about 28 of the CFA's ageing tankers.

    

    Warracknabeal grain grower Ross Johns said farmers just could not accept "any industry that requires subsidies to survive".

    

    Apart from the ESVF discount, developers have also received up to $200m a year in renewable energy subsidies in Victoria , which are mostly collected as hidden charges on households' and business' electricity bills.

    

    Mr Johns said he would rather see those funds go towards healthcare, education and roads, rather than boosting the profits of multinational companies.

Headline: Reserve Bank warns inflation to surge above 4 per cent despite rate rise
Full Text:
The nation's inflation bin fire will burn Aussie families for at least the next year after an interest rate rise on Tuesday. Treasurer Jim Chalmers tried to claim historically high government spending was not the culprit.

    Shock new forecasts from the Reserve Bank of Australia indicate inflation will again surge above 4 per cent – even with a second rate hike later this year.

    

    A lift in household spending and business investment from previously low levels has combined with near-record government outlays to overheat an economy that can no longer grow at more than 2 per cent without prices spiralling upwards.

    

    Yesterday, the RBA monetary policy board raised borrowing costs by 0.25 per cent for the first time since 2023 to cool "private demand" as governor Michele Bullock repeatedly refused to offer any view on whether public-sector demand was also adding fuel to the inflation bin-fire.

    

    Sky News host Andrew Bolt blasts Treasurer Jim Chalmers for "smashing the economy" following the...

    

    more less

    

    "I'm not going to tell the government what to do with fiscal policy. That's not my business," Ms Bullock said, even though other leading economists and business groups have lined up to argue fiscal policy is overstimulating the economy.

    

    "We are independent, they (the government) are independent, and we work with what we've got."

    

    The RBA's independence did not stop Treasurer Jim Chalmers from tearing into the RBA in 2024, when he said rate rises were "smashing the economy".

    

    Ms Bullock yesterday insisted the Albanese government had not told her to avoid criticising its spending.

    

    "No one has made that comment to me and the board obviously makes its decisions independently of the government," she said.

    

    In conjunction with announcing the rate increase, the RBA released a new set of forecasts which show it now expects the headline rate of consumer price index (CPI) to hit 4.2 per cent at the end of this financial year.

    

    In November, it had said the CPI would be 3.7 per cent on June 30. In August, it had predicted prices would be rising by just 3.1 per cent.

    

    The RBA also ratcheted up the projection for its preferred measure of inflation, called the "trimmed mean", to 3.7 per cent in June instead of 3.2 per cent. The central bank's goal is to get the trimmed mean down to 2.5 per cent.

    

    "The board now thinks it will take longer for inflation to return to target and this is not an acceptable outcome," Ms Bullock said.

    

    "I know this is not the news that Australians with mortgages want to hear ... (but) we cannot allow inflation to get away from us again."

    

    CBA was the first major lender to announce it was passing on the increase, from February 13, adding $80 a month to repayments on a $500,000 loan. Westpac then mirrored CommBank's move.

    

    The RBA's economic forecasts are built on a set of assumptions that include what money markets indicate will happen to the benchmark cash rate, which is set for another 25 basis point increase this year.

    

    Westpac chief economist Luci Ellis, who was the RBA's assistant governor for economics until 2023, last night said: "Absent a downside surprise in the March quarter inflation outcome, the monetary policy board is likely to hike again in May."

    

    Accountancy firm William Buck's chief economist Besa Deda went even further.

    

    "We also do not fully rule out the possibility of three rate hikes materialising in 2026 given the size of the upward revisions to inflation and the RBA's language," Ms Deda said.

    

    Following each of the three rate cuts last year, Treasurer Chalmers was quick to hold press conferences trumpeting the Albanese government's fiscal management.

    

    However, he did not front the media yesterday after the RBA's decision to hike rates.

    

    Dr Chalmers did tell parliament that price "pressure is coming from private demand, not public demand".

    

    Earlier in the day, he also told Sky News the "big driver" in the most recent CPI was holiday spending and said claims that government spending was making the RBA's job tougher were often more "politically motivated" than founded in facts.

    

    Last week, the Treasurer took AMP chief economist Shane Oliver to task.

    

    Yesterday, Dr Oliver told Sky News: "I'm not saying that government spending has boomed in the last six months. It's really the fact that it's boomed over the last six or seven years, taking public spending to levels that we've never, never been used to."

    

    Deloitte Access Economics partner Stephen Smith said it was not "controversial" to say "historically high" government spending was a "genuine driver of inflation".

    

    The Australian Industry Group's CEO Innes Willox suggested the Treasurer should also reflect on his own decisions. "The federal government can do its part by better controlling the recent high growth in public spending," he said.

    

    The Australian Chamber of Commerce and Industry said the government should cut more than $50bn a year from the budget by slashing spending on the NDIS, childcare, aged care, health and defence.

story_title: RBA Raises Interest Rates Amid Inflation Debate
story_summary: The Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA), under Governor Michele Bullock, raised the official cash rate and revised its forecasts to show inflation is set to surge above 4%, taking longer to return to target. The move has intensified a public debate over the causes of inflation. Treasurer Jim Chalmers insists that strong private demand is the primary driver and that government spending is restrained. However, this view is contested by numerous economists from institutions like Deloitte Access Economics and AMP, as well as business groups, who argue that historically high levels of government spending are a significant inflationary factor. The rate hike was passed on by major banks, increasing financial pressure on mortgage holders.

Headline: Can we even call US an ally if we're too scared to criticise it?
Full Text:
The Albanese government has done some excellent things, including billions more for Medicare, a National Anti Corruption Commission, massive wage

   increases for aged care workers, a forest logging ban, a social media ban, an increased minimum wage among dozens of other achievements.

    

    In reality they're the only game in town. The band formerly known as the Coalition has gone the way of One Direction. Sadly for them the wrong one, pointing to the hard right. There's no logical reason to vote for the Liberals, Nationals or One Nation. Albanese has completely stolen the centre-right of politics, being our most conservative Labor prime minister.

    

    On foreign policy his timidity is shocking. No criticism of Israel despite "alleged" breaches of the peace agreement. The Albanese government has declined to sign up to an international statement expressing concern about Israel demolishing an aid agency's headquarters.

    

    The foreign ministers of 11 nations, including the UK, Canada and France, strongly condemned Israel for demolishing the East Jerusalem headquarters of the United Nations Relief and Works Agency for Palestine Refugees (UNRWA).

    

    One of Australia's biggest social problems is lives being ruined by gambling. Peta Murphy spent her career fighting for a gambling advertising ban as has Andrew Wilkie. Albanese's silence is deafening. He is an incredible disappointment to many.

    

    Reports and pictorial evidence would suggest to an outsider that most of Gaza has been reduced to rubble or at least severe damage affecting all of its two million-plus inhabitants, causing as well profound long-term psychological harm to all.

    

    Mr Morris ("A simple reason Gaza's facilities were targets", January 26) suggests that Israeli destruction of Gaza civilian facilities is because Hamas was illegally using them for military purposes. Is he seriously suggesting that all of Gaza, it having been destroyed in whole or part as depicted, has been so used thereby rendering such as legitimate and necessary targets for the IDF?

    

    I stand to be corrected but neither Mr Morris nor other apologists in their correspondence regarding Israel's brutal war has ever expressed any empathy for what must surely be a great number of innocent Palestinians, nor any objectivity in addressing the gross disproportionality in the measures taken.

    

    No one condones the conduct of Hamas on October 7. However, it is simplistic to view it as a stand-alone event. The 80 years of Middle East conflict is complex but one truth is evident - there is deep and abiding animus stemming from certain factions on both sides.

    

    Mr Morris would be well aware of the encouraging and ever-widening opposition to and revulsion for the war exhibited by Israeli citizens, to say nothing of the worldwide condemnation. As a spokesman it would be appropriate for him to acknowledge these matters and to comment with greater objectivity.

    

    Well spotted, Rod Smith ("Letters", February 2). Even without the benefit of many of my letters that have gone unpublished you've accurately discerned that I prefer Albo and the Labor Party to anything the Libs, Nats or any of their feuding leaders past and present have to offer.

    

    Is Albo perfect? No. Has he done everything he promised? Not yet. Would I like him to do more on things like gambling advertising, affordable housing or climate change? Absolutely.

    

    But in the end it's the difference between hope and good intentions versus fear and nastiness. It's about which side sets out to try and build people up and which actively sets out to put them down. It's an easy choice.

    

    US warships are converging on Iran, and President Trump promises to unleash an attack "far worse" than the attack he ordered previously on Iran's nuclear facilities, unless Iran pledges not to become a nuclear power ( The Canberra Times , January 30).

    

    Most surprising is the unquestioned power of the US president, who evidently can wield the mighty US military machine just like the kings of old. Has Congress been abolished? Does a rules-based international order no longer exist?

    

    The danger is that with growing unpopularity domestically, with midterm elections later this year sure to alter Congress in the Democrat's favour and impeachment looming, the floundering US president may well order a military strike as a distraction.

    

    It's the economy, stupid? If you want to encapsulate the crisis that is upon us, this neoliberal slogan says it neatly. The stupidity is to elevate the economy to the pinnacle of human achievement, where pumping GDP becomes the highest goal, and we reward politicians who increase consumption.

    

    But that growth requires extracting resources and, in our current thinking, that means population growth. The real stupidity is that it inverts the relationship between the economy, society and the environment. That requires us to wilfully ignore the fact that the economy is a wholly-owned subsidiary of the other two.

    

    You doubt that? Have a look at what's happening this week as temperatures soar. Shopping centres are quiet because nobody wants to go outside. Multiply this across the planet and imagine what that does to the economy. Now Broken Hill shot past 50 degrees, at the limit of what a human can endure.

    

    How can an economy function in that situation? How can we grow enough to feed ourselves? The myth of climate change is that renewable energy will save us. While there's no doubt we must stop burning fossil fuels, renewables also use resources.

    

    It taps into the idea that we can consume our way out of disaster while ploughing on as usual. On our current path, the environment will continue to unravel while social inequality increases. And, as they fail, the economy will go with them.

    

    For those who think we're doing fine, imagine businesses folding because of interrupted supply chains. Heat disrupts economic activity, and people can't pay their bills. This is what an existential crisis looks like.

    

    Mokhles Sidden ("History can't be rewritten", January 30) is wrong in insisting Israel is an apartheid state rather than a democracy. All Israeli citizens have equal, democratic rights, whatever their religion or ethnic background. The six million Palestinians he cites are not Israeli citizens. Those in the West Bank are almost all under the governance of the Palestinian Authority. Those in Gaza were governed by Hamas.

    

    If they lack political representation, it's because the PA hasn't held elections for 20 years. If they lack water, it's because their Palestinian governments have squandered it. Hamas even tore up water pipes to use in constructing rockets.

    

    Israel applies different law to West Bank and Gaza Palestinians because to apply Israeli law would be annexation. Mokhles says, rightly, that history cannot be rewritten by repetition, but that's exactly what those accusing Israel of apartheid are trying to do.

    

    Clive Dye ("Republicans are destroying democracy", January 30) got to the heart of the current induced chaos in the (former) USA. The presidential and GOP Party objective is to foment sufficient unrest and even rebellion to justify the "temporary postponement" meaning the cancellation, of the midterm elections next November.

    

    These were deliberately inserted into the Constitution to put a brake on a berserk power-gorging president.

    

    The 18th-century founding fathers foresaw this deadly danger and acted to limit it. Alas their descendants in America, suggestible, naive and gullible, cannot see the danger.

    

    Australians, with their built-in BS detector from the convict days, can. Will someone please tell the Americans what is going on.

    

    I entirely agree with Rod Smith ("Letters", February 2) when he criticises the Albanese government for its performance failures, and porky-telling to date.

    

    This Pinocchio-led government has excelled in only two things, its excessive travel commitments allegedly for valid reasons, and conning the public at every opportunity it gets.

    

    Keith Hill as an ardent ALP supporter is cherry picking in his over-the-top praise of the government, as for every good policy it implements, it appears to be followed by a bad one. Under the circumstances this weak, spendthrift Albanese government at taxpayer's expense, has got a lot to answer for.

    

    The only thing that is functioning reliably and well this year for each of the National and Liberal parties' 69 MPs and senators who are working hard at huddling, counting and making party political phone calls, is the automatic transfer of thousands of dollars into their bank accounts every fortnight ("Ley adrift in conservative chaos", February 1).

    

    Sue Dyer, Downer

    

    At what point do we stop calling all the MyWay+ troubles "glitches" and admit the whole thing has been a complete and utter failure by the Barr government? ("New MyWay+ glitch causes timetable confusion", February 2) Perhaps the Greens and Liberals could try again to reach some form of agreement - anything to hold this tired and complacent government to account and see services improve.

    

    Andrew Laird, McKellar

    

    I am distressed to hear that the dingoes on K'gari will be killed. I have not heard that people are being told "do not swim alone".

    

    Auriel Barlow, Dickson

    

    Martha Kinsman (Letters, January 28) blithely blames Hamas and pro-Palestine supporters for the protracted war in Gaza. Apologists for Israel's behaviour do not refer to the terror being perpetrated by Israeli settlers on Palestinian residents of the West Bank, often with the backing of the IDF. How do they explain this without the same victim-blaming mentality that frames their view of the Gaza war?

    

    Martha Kinsman, Kambah

    

    To a layman, increasing interest rates does not seem likely to reduce or restrain prices such as electricity, and indeed may well increase them. Does the bank explain which prices it is seeking to restrain when it increases rates? I think a more specific set of tools should be developed to home in on the elastic price setters, rather than to punish the whole borrowing community.

    

    Chris Mobbs, Torrens

    

    There's a simple way for the Libs to take over government in the ACT, either by themselves at the next election or in cahoots with the Greens. They should credibly promise to retain the social policies built up by Labor over the years and focus strongly on fulfilling government's neglected municipal responsibilities. I refer, for example, to the embarrassing peeling and fading road signage, shoddy footpaths, graffiti, inadequate street lighting, and stronger oversight of private building certifiers. I'm sure readers can add other pet peeves about our terrible "council" services.

    

    Peter Burns, Watson

    

    The Multicultural Festival program includes a session on Russian creative culture at Civic Library. The optics of this are completely wrong when we are providing weapons to our Ukrainian allies to repel Russian imperialism. Whoever thought this was a good idea should have a good hard look at themselves.

    

    Rohan Goyne, Evatt

    

    To ensure comprehensive coverage of organisations with terror-like modus operandi the UN should include the IDF and ICE as qualified candidates.

    

    Patrick Robertson, Rivett

    

    Letters to the editor should be kept to 250 or fewer words. To the Point letters should not exceed 50 words. Reference to The Canberra Times reports should include a date and page number. Provide a phone number and address (only your suburb will be published). Responsibility for election comment is taken by John-Paul Moloney of 121 Marcus Clarke Street, Canberra. Published by Federal Capital Press of Australia Pty Ltd.

Headline: Financial toll hangs heavy over cancer patients
Full Text:
Jacinta Andrews knows all too well that the true cost of cancer extends far beyond medical bills or day treatment.

    Her husband has been battling multiple forms of cancer for decades, and although he is in remission the family continue to navigate the ongoing effects of his treatments, with some procedures costing thousands.

    

    While the public healthcare system covers a range of care, many patients still face the financial burden of cancer, research has found.

    

    Cancer Council Australia's national survey published on Wednesday found that nearly 99 per cent of Australians diagnosed with cancer incur out-of-pocket expenses, even when accessing public care.

    

    Tony Andrews was first diagnosed with head and neck cancer in 2009, forcing Ms Andrews to act as a full-time carer, assisting with daily transport, medical treatments and emotional wellbeing.

    

    While Ms Andrews was able to take paid time off, her husband was self-employed, and wasn't eligible for assistance.

    

    She considers herself lucky though that she could take leave, adding that they wouldn't have been able to pay their mortgage if it wasn't for her supportive workplace.

    

    The family were rocked by cancer again in 2023, after Mr Andrews was diagnosed with non-Hodgkin's lymphoma, which they are still navigating.

    

    Although Mr Andrews remains in remission, the side effects of his treatment have proved costly.

    

    "There are issues that continue and the latest ongoing thing since 2010, is the severity of the radiation treatment had on his gums and teeth," she told AAP.

    

    "We have had a lot of dental costs, and we can't afford private health insurance."

    

    Ms Andrews said the side effects on his teeth was unexpected, with her husband now struggling to eat.

    

    "We are saving up for him to get dentures or plates," she said.

    

    "We didn't know this was going to happen and you don't think about that stuff at the time of treatment, because you are just trying to survive."

    

    Cancer Council analysis found that loss of income or disruption to employment is the most common financial cost, affecting more than a third of Australians diagnosed with cancer.

    

    Surgical expenses (23.7 per cent ) and insurance gap payments (23.6 per cent) represented the largest out-of-pocket cost for nearly one quarter of respondents.

    

    "It is a huge burden for people," Cancer Council's Peter Diamond told AAP.

    

    "These costs can include both direct medical costs and indirect expenses, which people experience regardless of what stage (of) cancer diagnosis."

    

    Australians should be able to access high-quality cancer care, based on what best meets their needs, not what they can afford, Dr Diamond added.

    

    The research coincides with World Cancer Day and calls for increased access to financial counselling services for Australians affected by cancer through a mandatory industry-funded model, alongside greater investment from federal, state and territory governments.

story_title: Cancer Patients Face Major Out-of-Pocket Costs Survey Finds

    Australians should be able to access high-quality cancer care, based on what best meets their needs, not what they can afford, Dr Diamond added.

    

    The research coincides with World Cancer Day and calls for increased access to financial counselling services for Australians affected by cancer through a mandatory industry-funded model, alongside greater investment from federal, state and territory governments.

    

    Australian Associated Press

Headline: Providers, tech vendors wanted for trial
Full Text:
The Digital Health CRC is recruiting aged care stakeholders to trial Kinnexus – a platform that aims to deliver a nationally standardised approach to functional assessments and evidence-based quality indicator data.

    The Digital Health Cooperative Research Centre is inviting aged care providers and technology vendors to trial Kinnexus and be part of the national shift toward smarter, standardised, insight-driven care.

    

    Kinnexus is a DHCRC initiative focused on improving how functional assessments and quality indicator data are generated and used.

    

    Data, digital capability, and frontline experience must converge to deliver the quality, safety, and insight older Australians deserve, and Kinnexus is one of the most significant steps forward in this transformation journey, said Meagan Snewin, a senior program manager and aged care strategy lead at DHCRC.

    

    "Built on years of rigorous research through our Aged Care Data Compare project , Kinnexus offers something the sector has long needed: a nationally standardised approach to functional assessments and evidence-based quality indicator data."

    

    The platform is much more than another incremental improvement, she said.

    

    "It's the foundation for a future-ready aged care data ecosystem – one designed to reduce burdens on the workforce, support providers to meet rising regulatory expectations, and open the door to more timely, actionable, and meaningful insights.

    

    DHCRC is seeking aged care providers and technology vendors to help shape its development by participating in a trial and codesigning new features.

    

    The platform matters because the aged care workforce is stretched, documentation demands are growing, and the sector continues to grapple with fragmented systems, Ms Snewin said.

    

    She said Kinnexus promises:

    

    reduced manual data entry and real-time generation of quality indicators, freeing nurses and clinicians to focus on direct resident care

    

    deep insights into workflow and productivity, supported by automated, standardised QI reporting

    

    hands-on experimentation through the Kinnexus clinical information system simulator or deployment in participants' own development environments with full support

    

    real-world benefits, such as nurses, quality managers, and clinical leaders identifying improvements in efficiency, consistency, and decision-making in current trials.

    

    Ms Snewin said the sector must act now or Australia will continue to face the same fragmented systems, rising documentation burden, and missed opportunities for meaningful insight that have held back aged care for years.

    

    "For organisations committed to high-quality care, efficient workflows, and a digitally capable workforce, this is a chance to be part of something that will influence the sector for years to come," she said.

    

    Expressions of interest to trial Kinnexus close 13 February. Submit your EOI via the Kinnexus website or email Meagan Snewin at kinnexus@dhcrc.com to join or refer a colleague.

Headline: ‘We're all in the trenches': Friend reveals private struggle of Mosman Park family
Full Text:
A friend has shared the private struggles of the Perth family found dead in a suspected murder-suicide, amid criticisms they were "failed" by the NDIS.

    Natalie Brown

    

    @natalieisbrown

    

    4 min read

    

    February 4, 2026 - 8:26AM

    

    A friend has shared the hidden struggles of the Perth family found dead in a suspected murder-suicide, describing them as "in the trenches trying to survive".

    

    Maiwenna 'Mai' Goasdoue, 49, and Jarrod Clune, 50, and their two teenage sons, Leon, 16, and Otis, 14, were found dead inside their Mosman Park home on Friday.

    

    Both boys had severe autism – Otis was non-verbal – and were previously enrolled at the Peter Moyes Centre, the highly sought-after special needs centre at the prestigious Christ Church Grammar School. The brothers stopped attending the school in recent years, reportedly after one of them was asked to leave.

    

    A carer for Leon and Otis alerted police to the tragedy early on Friday morning, after finding a note on the home's front door urging them to call authorities.

    

    It's understood that a found inside – which reportedly indicated the parents decided together to end their lives – led police to determine it a murder-suicide.

    

    In the days since, support workers and friends have said they were "failed" by the National Disability Insurance Scheme (NDIS), and that Mai and Jarrod "they had no other choice".

    

    One family friend met Ms Goasdoue through Perth's autism community, with the two supporting each other and "talked about our respective challenges" as full-time carers of children with "extreme behaviours".

    

    "You reach out to other people who are in similar situations … But the people who understand you best are in the least position to give you support," the friend, who asked not to be named, told

    

    "We're all in the trenches trying to survive. And that tends to lead to increasing isolation."

    

    Detectives have said the family engaged with care workers, but declined to comment further.

    

    The friend said there was "no doubt" the boys' behaviour would have taken a toll.

    

    "You're talking about violence, meltdowns, vicious behaviour that could leave you with injuries," they said.

    

    "And these kids with these extreme behaviours are let down the most. There really isn't support for families like ours. There's nothing meaningful."

    

    Asked whether the case would influence future reforms to the NDIS, Federal Health Minister Mark Butler on Tuesday on the nature of care given to the family.

    

    "It's an awful tragedy at so many different levels but particularly the loss of two children's lives who obviously did not choose to die is an unspeakable tragedy," Mr Butler told reporters.

    

    "I know there's been a bit of commentary over in Western Australia about the NDIS packages that these children were on. I want to be really clear. First of all, I don't want to say anything that would impact the police investigation.

    

    "I want to give people the confidence that the NDIA, the agency, is cooperating fully with the police to the extent that they have any useful inputs to the police investigation.

    

    "I also want to be clear that there are very clear legal restrictions on our ability, my ability, the ability of the NDIA, to say anything about the packages that the children were on, so I'm not in a position to respond to some of the speculation that I've seen in Western Australian media.

    

    "All I want to say is that this is just an awful tragedy, just an unspeakable tragedy for this family, but for the broader community, and I know the NDIA will do everything it can to assist the police in its inquiries."

    

    An NDIA spokesperson said the organisation would work closely with authorities to support their investigations.

    

    "We extend our deepest sympathies to the Clune Goasdoue's family, their loved ones and all those affected by this tragic loss," the spokesperson said.

    

    "This matter is currently under investigation by Western Australian authorities.

    

    "The NDIA will work closely with the Western Australian authorities to support their inquiries or investigations."

    

    A former support worker for Leon and Otis, Maddie Page, said that despite the "love and resilience" Mai and Jarrod showed to their sons "during times of extreme difficulty and countless obstacles", the parents may have felt "they had no other choice".

    

    "The consistent dedication and deep love Mai and Jarrod showed for their boys, and the ways they never stopped fighting for them to feel seen and heard in this world will never be forgotten," Ms Page said.

    

    "May they rest in peace together as a family, NDIS – do better."

    

    Developmental Disability WA chief executive Mary Butterworth said the tragedy showed it was "fairly obvious" the family were not getting enough support.

    

    More investment in extra training of staff and support would alleviate stress so families do not feel it is "all up to them to try and survive", she said.

    

    "The reality is, in Western Australia we do not have enough trained support workers who can provide support to families," Ms Butterworth told the ABC.

    

    "I feel sorry for the support providers; they do not have enough funding in their plans that allows for them to provide deep and thorough training and mentoring to their staff."

Headline: Labor defends public spending as pressure mounts after rate rise
Full Text:
Housing Minister Clare O'Neil has slammed criticism of Labor's fiscal policy as "outrageous" as the government scrambles to defend itself from political attacks following the RBA's interest rates hike.

    The Albanese government is facing further pressure on its handling of the economy after the RBA on Tuesday because of sticky inflation.

    

    For the first time in two years on Tuesday, the Reserve Bank lifted the official cash rate by 25 basis points to 3.85 per cent following its first meeting of 2026.

    

    Speaking to Seven's Sunrise, Ms O'Neil rejected claims made by Nationals senator Bridget McKenzie, who branded Labor as having "failed to make the tough decisions" to cut government spending and hurting Australian households as a result.

    

    Clare O'Neil cited the RBA's claim the government was contributing less to inflation over time. NewsWire / Martin Ollman

    

    Credit: News Corp Australia

    

    Bridget McKenzie has criticised the Albanese government's spending. NewsWire / Martin Ollman

    

    Credit: News Corp Australia

    

    "That is an outrageous comment from someone who's in a political party that's coming to Canberra every week not even thinking about the people they represent here, but instead playing ridiculous parlour games, focusing on careers and ambitions," Ms O'Neil said on Wednesday, referencing the dramatic split in the Coalition.

    

    Ms O'Neil also claimed Labor did not need to reassess its cost of living relief measures, after being pressed on whether they were not adequately capturing Australians in need.

    

    NED-9108-Monthly-Inflation-Indicator

    

    But the Liberals have demanded the government curb its spending by making cuts.

    

    On Wednesday, treasury spokesman Ted O'Brien told the ABC: "They should go line item by line item through their budget. They should get rid of waste and when they need to spend they have the right to do so, but that comes with the responsibility to find commensurate offsets and savings."

    

    He highlighted net zero policy and electric vehicles subsidies as potential targets for savings under a Liberal government, which he predicted would save $5bn.

    

    Earlier, Opposition Leader Sussan Ley echoed the same sentiment, with a two-word response to questions about what Labor should do differently: "Stop spending."

    

    "And when you've got respected economists all saying there are no fiscal guardrails on this government … when you've got corporate Australia almost demanding a $50 billion cut to spending, it is everywhere in terms of what the government has been told about what it needs to do," Ms Ley told the ABC.

    

    But Finance Minister Katy Gallagher later told the broadcaster it was difficult for the government to find cuts, amid high pressure on areas such defence, healthcare, and aged care.

    

    She maintained the government was "mindful" of public spending and had made responsible decisions.

    

    "We've found $114 billion in savings – I know it rolls off the tongue, but that is much more that any government that I can recall ever has," Ms Gallagher said.

    

    Treasurer Jim Chalmers rejected accusations about government spending. NewsWire / Martin Ollman

    

    Credit: News Corp Australia

    

    The RBA's decision to on Tuesday was unanimous, hinting there may be more hikes as its latest forecasts point to inflation being unlikely to fall before sometime in 2027.

    

    RBA governor Michele Bullock said she realised Australian households would be disappointed by the rate decision, but said it was the right decision overall to get inflation back to the 2-3 per cent target.

    

    Ms Bullock also said she would not call on the government to rein in spending to end the stickiness of the inflation.

    

    "That's not my business," she said, emphasising the RBA's independence from government.

    

    The Liberal Party and Nationals have been targeting Mr Chalmers and government spending for keeping inflation higher than it should be.

    

    "Treasurer, when will you start living by your own words and take responsibility?" Shadow Treasurer Ted O'Brien said in question time on Tuesday.

    

    Mr Chalmers hit back, defending government spending and its role in the rate rise.

    

    "The statement released by the independent Reserve Bank explaining the decision that they have taken today does not mention government spending at all," he said.

    

    He said the government was focused on easing the cost-of-living pressures on Australians.

Headline: Knoxfield brings the community spirit
Full Text:
Despite only welcoming residents in mid-January, Infinite Care's Knoxfield home in Victoria's capital has cemented itself as an intergenerational community hub.

    In the first two weeks of opening, Infinite Care's flagship aged care home in Knoxfield is celebrating its emergence as a central hub for the eastern Melbourne suburb.

    

    There has been an average of six admissions a week to the 177-bed, five-level home. But the open-door philosophy of the home and the public-facing café has seen the residence fill a social void for the community too.

    

    An adjoining park has made Café Eight a go-to destination for the local community, with mothers' groups, families and residents contributing to the formation of an intergenerational place of social connectivity.

    

    Chief executive officer of the aged care provider Luke Greive said the vision for Knoxfield was always to create a space where residents can remain connected to the community, but community engagement so far has exceeded all of Infinite Care's internal projections.

    

    "Seeing locals walk their dogs to our door, mothers' groups coming together, families sharing a coffee, and children playing while our residents look on – that is exactly the one community spirit we aimed to build," Mr Greive said.

    

    In particular, the home's commitment to a hotel-style service model has proven popular with families, who have praised the food and the visibly present management team, which includes the operations manager and general manager contributing to lunch services.

    

    Small touches like the dedicated community dog bowl have also contributed to the positive sentiment surrounding the new home, while the daily interactions between local children and residents have enhanced wellbeing and social connectivity overall.

    

    Infinite Care remains focused on ensuring Knoxfield maintains a high standard of care and community engagement, in the hopes it will prove that modern aged care can be a vibrant, welcoming and essential part of local neighbourhoods, the provider said.

    

    As 2026 continues, Infinite Care intends to further establish itself in Victoria, with plans to open a new home in the inner-city Melbourne suburb of Prahran. It also has several acquisitions across the state in the pipeline.

    

    Read about other expansions or openings and send details and an image to editorial@australianageingagenda.com.au for any we have missed
