Headline: 'Lot of complaints': ex-minister's aged care warning
Full Text:
Older Australians are dealing with big price increases and longer waiting lists for care under major reforms to the aged care sector, a former Liberal minister warns.

    Loginor signup to continue reading

    

    Christopher Pyne, who spent 26 years in federal parliament, has also warned against stereotyping older people as either wealthy baby boomers or frail retirees, describing both framings as "crude caricatures".

    

    Mr Pyne, now the chairman of advocacy group Council on the Ageing, said it would take time to iron out the kinks in the government's new aged care act.

    

    The overhaul of the aged care sector took effect in November 2025, requiring wealthier retirees to pay more for their care and restructuring the way services are funded.

    

    Addressing the National Press Club to launch the council's State of the Older Nation report, the former cabinet minister said while it had only been a few months since the reforms kicked in, people in care had raised a number of complaints.

    

    They included longer waits for home care packages, pressure to sign care agreements quickly, and sharp price rises.

    

    "We expect the government will react to those over time," Mr Pyne said.

    

    He said older people were also being forced to pay for assistance showering.

    

    "Because that's such a big thing, of course, it gets a lot of coverage and a lot of complaints," Mr Pyne said.

    

    The State of the Older Nation report, released by Mr Pyne on Wednesday, includes alarming figures about the number of people experiencing age-based discrimination.

    

    It found 38 per cent of people had experienced ageism since turning 50 and 36 per cent said they didn't want to return to the workforce because of ageism.

    

    "Too often, the story of older Australians is reduced to one of two basic plot lines," Mr Pyne said.

    

    "The first goes like this: older people - often framed as baby boomers - are wealthy, secure, asset rich, sitting on housing, insulated from economic pressure and blocking opportunities for younger generations.

    

    "The second sits at the opposite extreme. Older people, often those in their late 70s and 80s are portrayed as frail dependent in nursing homes, clogging hospital beds and placing an unsustainable burden on public resources."

    

    Mr Pyne said the stereotypes persisted due to convenience, not accuracy.

    

    "They ignore the community and economic contribution of older people, simplify complex policy changes and allow us to avoid uncomfortable conversations about inequality and disadvantage."

    

    Australian Associated Press

Headline: Tencent Cloud EdgeOne Launches Free AI Crawler Control: Empowering Developers to Reclaim Content Sovereignty
Full Text:
Tencent Cloud EdgeOne Launches Free AI Crawler Control: Empowering Developers to Reclaim Content Sovereignty  Published: 04/02/2026 16:00

    SHENZHEN, China Feb. 4, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- As the demand for data to train Generative AI models surges, developers are facing a critical dilemma: how to manage aggressive AI scraping while protecting their original content and infrastructure costs. To address this, Tencent Cloud EdgeOne has officially released its Basic Bot Management capabilities to all users—including those on the Free Plan. This update introduces two core features: AI Crawler Control and the CAPTCHA Page.

    

    Ending the Crawler "Whac-A-Mole" Game

    

    Unlike traditional web crawlers, modern AI crawlers are stealthier, operate on a massive scale, and are increasingly difficult to distinguish from legitimate traffic. Developers often find themselves trapped in an endless game of "Whac-A-Mole"—blocking one crawler IP only to see dozens of new ones appear the next day under different guises. This results in unauthorized scraping of original content and wasted server bandwidth.

    

    Tencent Cloud EdgeOne's new AI Crawler Control solves this by utilizing advanced User-Agent feature recognition. The system continuously updates its identification rules to pinpoint mainstream AI bots, including GPTBot, ClaudeBot, and Google-Extended

    

    Developers no longer need to write complex scripts to defend their sites. With a simple configuration in the EdgeOne console, users can apply diverse actions based on their specific business needs:

    

    Monitor: Observe crawler behavior without interference.

    

    Block: Instantly stop unauthorized data harvesting.

    

    Allow: Permit friendly bots to access your site.

    

    Challenge: Trigger a verification step for suspicious traffic.

    

    This flexible strategy ensures developers can block malicious data harvesting while still embracing a friendly AI ecosystem when beneficial.

    

    Intelligent Defense for High-Value Scenarios

    

    In addition to crawler management, the update includes the CAPTCHA Page feature designed to mitigate automated attacks. This function intelligently identifies suspicious Bot behavior and triggers a human-machine verification CAPTCHA.

    

    Crucially, this is done without degrading the experience for real users. It builds a robust security line for high-stakes scenarios—such as digital content rights protection, e-commerce flash sales, and financial transactions—ensuring that automated scripts cannot manipulate metrics or disrupt services.

    

    From Passive Defense to Active Management

    

    "With the rapid evolution of AI technology, the governance of content and data access is a long-term challenge," said Yun Li, Head of Tencent Cloud EdgeOne Product Team. "By making our Basic Bot Management capabilities free, we aim to help every developer shift from passive defense to active management, giving them full control over their digital assets."

    

    The new features are available now in the EdgeOne console. Tencent Cloud EdgeOne is committed to continuously refining its Bot Management and security capabilities, helping developers establish clear boundaries between AI technology application and content rights protection.

    

    About Tencent Cloud EdgeOne: EdgeOne is Tencent Cloud's integrated platform for edge security and acceleration.

    

    Click the link to learn more about EdgeOne: https://edgeone.ai/?from=ai_crawler_control

    

    About Tencent Cloud:

Headline: 'Lot of complaints': ex-minister's aged care warning
Full Text:
Older Australians are dealing with big price increases and longer waiting lists for care under major reforms to the aged care sector, a former Liberal minister warns.

    Christopher Pyne, who spent 26 years in federal parliament, has also warned against stereotyping older people as either wealthy baby boomers or frail retirees, describing both framings as "crude caricatures".

    

    Mr Pyne, now the chairman of advocacy group Council on the Ageing, said it would take time to iron out the kinks in the government's new aged care act.

    

    The overhaul of the aged care sector took effect in November 2025, requiring wealthier retirees to pay more for their care and restructuring the way services are funded.

    

    Addressing the National Press Club to launch the council's State of the Older Nation report, the former cabinet minister said while it had only been a few months since the reforms kicked in, people in care had raised a number of complaints.

    

    They included longer waits for home care packages, pressure to sign care agreements quickly, and sharp price rises.

    

    "We expect the government will react to those over time," Mr Pyne said.

    

    He said older people were also being forced to pay for assistance showering.

    

    "Because that's such a big thing, of course, it gets a lot of coverage and a lot of complaints," Mr Pyne said.

    

    The State of the Older Nation report, released by Mr Pyne on Wednesday, includes alarming figures about the number of people experiencing age-based discrimination.

    

    It found 38 per cent of people had experienced ageism since turning 50 and 36 per cent said they didn't want to return to the workforce because of ageism.

    

    "Too often, the story of older Australians is reduced to one of two basic plot lines," Mr Pyne said.

    

    "The first goes like this: older people - often framed as baby boomers - are wealthy, secure, asset rich, sitting on housing, insulated from economic pressure and blocking opportunities for younger generations.

    

    "The second sits at the opposite extreme. Older people, often those in their late 70s and 80s are portrayed as frail dependent in nursing homes, clogging hospital beds and placing an unsustainable burden on public resources."

    

    Mr Pyne said the stereotypes persisted due to convenience, not accuracy.

    

    "They ignore the community and economic contribution of older people, simplify complex policy changes and allow us to avoid uncomfortable conversations about inequality and disadvantage."

    

    Australian Associated Press

Headline: Tencent Cloud EdgeOne Launches Free AI Crawler Control: Empowering Developers to Reclaim Content Sovereignty
Full Text:
Published: 04/02/2026 16:00  SHENZHEN, China Feb. 4, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- As the demand for data to train Generative AI models surges, developers

   are facing a critical dilemma: how to manage aggressive AI scraping while protecting their original content and infrastructure costs. To address this, Tencent Cloud EdgeOne has officially released its Basic Bot Management capabilities to all users—including those on the Free Plan. This update introduces two core features: AI Crawler Control and the CAPTCHA Page.

    

    Ending the Crawler "Whac-A-Mole" Game

    

    Unlike traditional web crawlers, modern AI crawlers are stealthier, operate on a massive scale, and are increasingly difficult to distinguish from legitimate traffic. Developers often find themselves trapped in an endless game of "Whac-A-Mole"—blocking one crawler IP only to see dozens of new ones appear the next day under different guises. This results in unauthorized scraping of original content and wasted server bandwidth.

    

    Tencent Cloud EdgeOne's new AI Crawler Control solves this by utilizing advanced User-Agent feature recognition. The system continuously updates its identification rules to pinpoint mainstream AI bots, including GPTBot, ClaudeBot, and Google-Extended

    

    Developers no longer need to write complex scripts to defend their sites. With a simple configuration in the EdgeOne console, users can apply diverse actions based on their specific business needs:

    

    This flexible strategy ensures developers can block malicious data harvesting while still embracing a friendly AI ecosystem when beneficial.

    

    Intelligent Defense for High-Value Scenarios

    

    In addition to crawler management, the update includes the CAPTCHA Page feature designed to mitigate automated attacks. This function intelligently identifies suspicious Bot behavior and triggers a human-machine verification CAPTCHA.

    

    Crucially, this is done without degrading the experience for real users. It builds a robust security line for high-stakes scenarios—such as digital content rights protection, e-commerce flash sales, and financial transactions—ensuring that automated scripts cannot manipulate metrics or disrupt services.

    

    From Passive Defense to Active Management

    

    "With the rapid evolution of AI technology, the governance of content and data access is a long-term challenge," said Yun Li, Head of Tencent Cloud EdgeOne Product Team. "By making our Basic Bot Management capabilities free, we aim to help every developer shift from passive defense to active management, giving them full control over their digital assets."

    

    The new features are available now in the EdgeOne console. Tencent Cloud EdgeOne is committed to continuously refining its Bot Management and security capabilities, helping developers establish clear boundaries between AI technology application and content rights protection.

    

    About Tencent Cloud EdgeOne: EdgeOne is Tencent Cloud's integrated platform for edge security and acceleration.

    

    Click the link to learn more about EdgeOne: https://edgeone.ai/?from=ai_crawler_control

    

    About Tencent Cloud:

    

    Tencent Cloud, one of the world's leading cloud companies, is committed to creating innovative solutions to resolve real-world issues and enabling digital transformation for smart industries. Through our extensive global infrastructure, Tencent Cloud provides businesses across the globe with stable and secure industry-leading cloud products and services, leveraging technological advancements such as cloud computing, Big Data analytics, AI, IoT, and network security. It is our constant mission to meet the needs of industries across the board, including the fields of gaming, media and entertainment, finance, healthcare, property, retail, travel, and transportation.

Headline: Costly by-election as high-profile Liberal quits early
Full Text:
Ex-Victorian deputy opposition leader Sam Groth is leaving politics to pursue "other opportunities". Photo: Joel Carrett/AAP PHOTOS

    Taxpayers will be on the hook for millions of dollars to run a by-election triggered by a once-rising Liberal star quitting state parliament.

    

    Victorian MP Sam Groth announced in early January he would stand down as the party's deputy leader and not recontest his Mornington Peninsula seat of Nepean at the state election in November.

    

    The former tennis ace has since decided to pull the pin next week to pursue "other opportunities", triggering a by-election.

    

    Advertisement

    

    Ad

    

    In a statement, Mr Groth wished Opposition Leader Jess Wilson and the party the "very best" for the November election.

    

    "Most importantly, I thank my family, my wife Britt and our twin boys, for their unwavering love and support," he said.

    

    Mr Groth was not in Victorian parliament's lower house for question time on Wednesday afternoon, with Premier Jacinta Allan making fun of his desertion.

    

    Ms Wilson, who replaced Brad Battin as state Liberal leader in a snap coup in November, said she spoke to Mr Groth on Tuesday and did not try to change his mind.

    

    Jess Wilson says she didn't try to change Sam Groth's mind on quitting. (James Ross/AAP PHOTOS)

    

    "Not at all," she said.

    

    "I've spoken to Sam many times over the last few weeks ... this is a decision that Sam's taken in the best interests of his family."

    

    Mr Groth previously revealed party infighting and public pressure on his family had become unbearable.

    

    The 38-year-old and his wife settled legal action in November with Melbourne's Herald Sun newspaper over articles relating to when the pair began their relationship.

    

    Sam Groth and wife Brittany settled legal action against the Herald Sun newspaper. (Joel Carrett/AAP PHOTOS)

    

    Advertisement

    

    Ad

    

    During the court process, it was revealed a Liberal MP had contacted the outlet with a "piece of gossip".

    

    Liberal stalwart David Southwick easily won a vote over backbencher Chris Crewther to replace Mr Groth as deputy leader at a recent meeting.

    

    Outside, Mr Groth refused to rule out leaving politics for a better job offer but insisted his intention was to serve out the remainder of his term.

    

    "As I stand here right now, there is a zero per cent chance of me causing a by-election in Nepean," he said on January 28.

    

    Mr Groth's life in politics followed a tennis career that included a world record fastest serve in 2012 and a career-high ATP singles ranking of 53 in 2015.

    

    The former Davis Cup representative reclaimed the then ultra-marginal seat from Labor in 2022 on a margin of more than six per cent.

    

    Sam Groth turned to politics after a long career as a professional tennis player. (Glenn Hunt/AAP PHOTOS)

    

    Advertisement

    

    Ad

    

    His impending resignation means Nepean voters will face the polls twice in 2026, with taxpayers to foot the extra bill for conducting the by-election.

    

    Victoria's by-elections for the seats of Prahran and Werribee a year ago collectively cost $4.22 million.

    

    Deputy Premier Ben Carroll would not be drawn on whether Labor should run a candidate in the Nepean by-election, saying the decision rests with the party's head office.

    

    "Every part of this state, we fight hard for," he said.

    

    The Victorian Liberals have been in opposition for all but four years this century and will need to net 16 seats to form majority government in November.

    

    Australian Associated Press

Headline: Thriving Kids explained: What Labor's NDIS alternative means for you
Full Text:
Finer details of the $4bn Thriving Kids program designed to shift many kids off the NDIS have finally been released. So what does it mean for families, and what questions remain?

    @Drcyftz

    

    The Labor government has finally revealed the proposed model for its $4bn Thriving Kids plan, but parents, stakeholders and educators are still calling for more details.

    

    Thriving Kids will support children aged 8 and under with developmental delay and/or autism with low to moderate support needs.

    

    Health and Disability Minister Mark Butler said the new program would be "easy to access" and won't require a costly and lengthy formal diagnosis.

    

    Its four-pillar design places parents as children's "most important teacher and support".

    

    But questions still remain on the implications for the potentially hundreds of thousands of children set to be on the program, as the ball sits in the states' and territories' court.

    

    The states and territories will deliver Thriving Kids and Mr Butler said the government plans to "wrap up" all bilateral agreements with them by the end of February.

    

    What is Thriving Kids?

    

    Thriving Kids was announced in August 2025 in response to the cost blowout of the National Disability Insurance Scheme (NDIS), which is projected to reach $63.4bn by 2028-29.

    

    It will shift young children aged 8 and under with developmental delay and/or autism with low to moderate support needs from the NDIS into state-led support streams.

    

    The Albanese government says it is confident the program will deliver children the support they need to thrive.

    

    But parents of children on the scheme fear Thriving Kids will remove their agency and control over their child's disability support and place them in a one-size-fits-all model.

    

    Thriving Kids will roll out from October 1 this year, following a three-month delay, with full implementation by January 2028.

    

    The $4bn price tag will be split 50/50 between the federal government and states and territories.

    

    Who will be on Thriving Kids?

    

    While Thriving Kids is designed to ease the pressure on the NDIS by for young children with low to moderate support needs, Mr Butler could not say how many children will be on the scheme.

    

    "In terms of numbers, the obvious place to start is broadly the number of children on the NDIS under the age of nine now with low to moderate support needs, and we can quantify that broadly," he said.

    

    "I want to stress that until 1 January 2028, when Thriving Kids is fully operational, NDIS is still open for those children. There won't be access changes made until the beginning of 2028."

    

    According to the latest NDIS quarterly report, there were 170,786 children younger than nine with an NDIS plan.

    

    But a proportion of these children have a disability beyond the coverage of Thriving Kids.

    

    Children with permanent and significant disability and children aged 8 and under with high support needs will remain eligible for the NDIS.

    

    How will it work?

    

    Mr Butler said Thriving Kids will have "essentially four pillars".

    

    "The first pillar is to raise awareness and early identification of children with developmental issues," Mr Butler said.

    

    "The second is to provide ready access to information, advice, and navigation for parents in particular.

    

    "The third is to build the skills of parents.

    

    "And the fourth pillar is to provide access to targeted support for children and parents who need it.

    

    "Those targeted supports will be provided by trained workers, usually allied health professionals like speech pathologists, occupational therapists, physiotherapists, and the like."

    

    States and territories will be responsible for delivering these supports and services. ?

    

    But the pillars place more responsibility on parents to access and provide support for their children.

    

    What do stakeholders say?

    

    Children and Young People with Disability Australia (CYDA) chief executive Skye Kakoschke-Moore said her organisation was glad to see the final report, but still needed more details.

    

    "The decision-makers are the Commonwealth, states and territories, and they will ultimately decide what will and won't be funded," she said.

    

    "We're waiting for the details of the bilateral agreements to be released.

    

    "What would be really helpful is more information from the states and territories about the sorts of support they want to build on.

    

    "And more information about gaps they should have identified in service type and location."

    

    Autism Association of Australia chief executive Nicole Rogerson said families were still seeking information of what Thriving Kids will look like "on the ground".

    

    "The community have wanted to know more about what Thriving Kids actually means in practice," Ms Rogerson said.

    

    "Today we do have more information, which is welcome, but it also raises new and important questions about how this will work on the ground."

    

    The Royal Australian College of GPs welcomed the release of the plan.

    

    "This is a welcome shift back to putting children and families at the heart of early support," said its president, Dr Michael Wright.

    

    "But this system will only succeed if general practice is properly supported to play its central role in early identification and navigation.

    

    "The model is built on key principles: child and family-centred care, early identification, evidence-based supports, cultural safety, flexible access, and services delivered in children's everyday environments."

    

    Teachers and early educators will be major players in the new model.

    

    Australian Education Union president Correna Haythorpe said she fears the model may increase teachers' workload.

    

    "The AEU welcomes the intent of the Thriving Kids program, but its success will depend entirely on how it is implemented," Ms Haythorpe said.

    

    "Thriving Kids must support teachers, not shift the burden of under-resourced services onto schools."

    

    What's your NDIS experience and will your family be affected by Thriving Kids? Share your story with us at education@news.com.au

    

    Originally published as Thriving Kids explained: What Labor's NDIS alternative means for you

story_title: Government Unveils 'Thriving Kids' NDIS Alternative Program
story_summary: The Albanese government has released the model for its $4 billion 'Thriving Kids' program, a major reform designed to shift children aged eight and under with developmental delays or mild autism from the National Disability Insurance Scheme (NDIS) to state-led support systems. Championed by Health Minister Mark Butler, the program aims to provide community-based support and easier access to early intervention without requiring a formal medical diagnosis, in an effort to make the NDIS more sustainable. While the move has been welcomed by some stakeholders, disability groups, parent advocates, and the Australian Education Union have called for more operational details and raised concerns about implementation, parental control, and potential increased workloads for teachers. The program is set for a phased rollout starting in late 2024, with full implementation by 2028.

Headline: The new 'highly transmissible' COVID-19 variant: what Aussies need to know
Full Text:
A new COVID-19 variant is spreading quietly - and older Australians are paying the price, experts say.

    The dominant strain, NB.1.8.1 - a sub-lineage of Omicron - is now circulating widely across Australia, although it is flying under the radar.

    

    It is not causing the dramatic hospital surges seen in 2022, which is why it rarely makes headlines.

    

    NB.1.8.1 is highly transmissible, spreads faster than earlier Omicron variants, and has a shorter incubation period.

    

    In the most recent NSW reporting week (ending late Jan 2026), there were 426 new laboratory-confirmed cases, with roughly one in four of those cases being someone over the age of 70.

    

    Read more on The Senior

    

    Nationally, only 13.4 per cent of Australians over 75 have had a booster in the last six months.

    

    NB.1.8.1 has one signature symptom, the stabbing throat. Many patients report a sharp, stabbing pain when swallowing, often before other symptoms appear.

    

    Professor Peter Collignon, a microbiologist at the Australian National University, said age and underlying illness remain the biggest risk factors when it comes to COVID-19.

    

    "Older people often have heart disease, lung disease or other chronic conditions," he told The Senior . "COVID-19 can be the straw that breaks the camel's back."

    

    He said most Australians over 50 have built up some immunity through vaccinations and infections, but protection weakens with age.

    

    "Your immunity tends to decline as you get older," he said.

    

    Professor Collignon also cautioned against alarmism, noting that dire predictions about new variants have often failed to materialise.

    

    But he stressed that vaccine protection is short-lived.

    

    "A booster only gives strong protection for about six months," he said.

    

    Current advice from the Australian Technical Advisory Group on Immunisation (ATAGI), issued in February 2026, reflects that reality.

    

    Australians aged 75 and over are advised to receive a COVID-19 booster every six months

    

    Those aged 65 to 74 are recommended to get one every 12 months, but are eligible every six months if they choose.

    

    Anyone immunocompromised is advised to get a booster once every 12 months.

    

    Placing additional pressure on older immune systems is the so-called "Super-K" flu strain.

    

    Unlike typical flu that peaks in July, Super-K began surging in late 2025 and has persisted straight through the Australian summer.

Headline: Queensland disability client Bridie Gilligan choked to death on Hungry Jacks burger, coroner finds
Full Text:
Coronial findings revealed the woman lost consciousness and later died after shoving the fast food chain's "Yumbo" burger into her mouth at her Cairns home.

    4 min read

    

    A Queensland disability client choked to death on a Hungry Jacks burger after snatching it from her support worker, a coroner has found.

    

    had a history of choking and was on a restricted diet when she choked while eating a Hungry Jacks "Yumbo" burger at her Edmonton home on April 29, 2021.

    

    Coronial findings published on Tuesday by Deputy State Coroner Stephanie Gallagher revealed how Ms Gilligan, 42, lost consciousness and later died after shoving the burger into her mouth.

    

    The inquest heard Ms Gilligan's support worker Margaret Lowry had purchased a burger for each of them and had intended to cut Ms Gilligan's into small pieces - but Ms Gilligan quickly snatched it and ran outside first.

    

    "The support worker called an ambulance and Ms Gilligan was taken to hospital but later died," Ms Gallagher said.

    

    The inquest heard Ms Gilligan had suffered a catastrophic brain injury and was pronounced brain dead on May 3, 2021.

    

    Her family have since expressed concerns about whether it had been appropriate to give her the burger given her disability and dietary needs.

    

    Ms Gilligan had been living in accommodation provided by Endeavour Foundation at the time of her death, as she required support for her intellectual disability associated with Cornelia De Lange Syndrome.

    

    She had been largely non-verbal and "required assistance with all her activities of daily living", Ms Gallagher noted.

    

    Ms Gilligan's mother told police her daughter was supposed to be given soft-textured foods and also be "completely supervised and assisted with eating, as if she was left alone with food then it is likely a choking incident would occur".

    

    Ms Gilligan's death was investigated by the Office of the Work Health and Safety Prosecutor, who concluded they were "not satisfied there was sufficient evidence to commence a prosecution against either the Endeavour Foundation or its worker, Ms Lowry".

    

    Ms Gallagher said in her findings on Tuesday that she accepted Ms Gilligan's death was preventable; however, she found Ms Lowry's supervision and Endeavour's systems regarding diet were both appropriate.

    

    "The concerns expressed by Ms Gilligan's family must be balanced against the fact that Ms Gilligan was a challenging client who did not cope well with new support workers," Ms Gallagher said.

    

    Support workers had given varying views to the inquest about whether giving fast food to a client was appropriate - with one suggesting it may actually support their independence.

    

    However, the worker noted that the decision should still be communicated with and resolved with the appropriate decision-maker - which would have been Ms Gilligan's mother in this case.

    

    A dietitian had given evidence saying she would not consider a burger or bun appropriate for Ms Gilligan's specific meal plan.

    

    Ms Gallagher ultimately agreed that the burger, even if cut up into small pieces, would not have complied with the standards set out for Ms Gilligan's diet - but she pointed out that Ms Gilligan's meal plan had contained other foods that also didn't comply with the standards.

    

    She said this had been due to competing considerations surrounding Ms Gilligan's quality of life, and the foods included had been ones Ms Gilligan liked or ones that gave her some level of autonomy or independence.

    

    Ms Gallagher considered it was not unreasonable for Ms Lowry to have thought a burger was suitable under the circumstances, given she had not been given specific training on the requirements of Ms Gilligan's "soft and bite-sized" diet, and the meal plan had included sandwiches.

    

    "Ms Lowry's evidence, which was not challenged, was that she had given Ms Gilligan cheeseburgers before, and Ms Gilligan had been able to eat them when they had been cut up into small pieces," Ms Gallagher noted.

    

    The inquest heard Endeavour had multiple policies and procedures in place to ensure workers had adequate information and training to care for a person with eating and swallowing difficulties.

    

    Endeavour staff had emphasised during the inquest that the support worker's goal is to support their clients to live as independently as possible.

    

    They acknowledged that the introduction of the NDIS had created a separation between Supported Independent Living providers like Endeavour and their client's other health services, however.

    

    In Ms Gilligan's case, the court heard this resulted in delayed follow ups with her other health services, a lack of collaboration on meal plans, and no guidance or training being provided to Ms Gilligan's support workers about her specific diet requirements.

    

    Ms Gallagher found Endeavour had not received sufficient information from Ms Gilligan's health care providers to identify the "real risk" bread products posed to her, and acknowledged Ms Gilligan's risk of choking was not appropriately managed by her overall system of care.

    

    "Endeavour has made sensible and appropriate changes to its systems and processes since Ms Gilligan's death to ensure that a similar tragic outcome does not occur in the future," Ms Gallagher said.

    

    "... I do not accept, on the evidence before me, that the responsibility for the circumstances which led to Ms Gilligan's death lies with Endeavour Foundation or their employees and agents."

    

    She found four recommendations suggested by Endeavour to be practical - including that the NDIS or NDIA review the processes for Supported Independent Living providers to access information about allied health supports, and consider appropriate funding in NDIA plans to ensure training of Supported Independent Living staff.

Headline: Queensland disability client Bridie Gilligan choked to death on Hungry Jacks burger, coroner finds
Full Text:
Coronial findings revealed the woman lost consciousness and later died after shoving the fast food chain's "Yumbo" burger into her mouth at her Cairns home.

    A Queensland disability client choked to death on a Hungry Jacks burger after snatching it from her support worker, a coroner has found.

    

    had a history of choking and was on a restricted diet when she choked while eating a Hungry Jacks "Yumbo" burger at her Edmonton home on April 29, 2021.

    

    Coronial findings published on Tuesday by Deputy State Coroner Stephanie Gallagher revealed how Ms Gilligan, 42, lost consciousness and later died after shoving the burger into her mouth.

    

    The inquest heard Ms Gilligan's support worker Margaret Lowry had purchased a burger for each of them and had intended to cut Ms Gilligan's into small pieces - but Ms Gilligan quickly snatched it and ran outside first.

    

    "The support worker called an ambulance and Ms Gilligan was taken to hospital but later died," Ms Gallagher said.

    

    The inquest heard Ms Gilligan had suffered a catastrophic brain injury and was pronounced brain dead on May 3, 2021.

    

    Her family have since expressed concerns about whether it had been appropriate to give her the burger given her disability and dietary needs.

    

    Ms Gilligan had been living in accommodation provided by Endeavour Foundation at the time of her death, as she required support for her intellectual disability associated with Cornelia De Lange Syndrome.

    

    She had been largely non-verbal and "required assistance with all her activities of daily living", Ms Gallagher noted.

    

    Ms Gilligan's mother told police her daughter was supposed to be given soft-textured foods and also be "completely supervised and assisted with eating, as if she was left alone with food then it is likely a choking incident would occur".

    

    Ms Gilligan's death was investigated by the Office of the Work Health and Safety Prosecutor, who concluded they were "not satisfied there was sufficient evidence to commence a prosecution against either the Endeavour Foundation or its worker, Ms Lowry".

    

    Ms Gallagher said in her findings on Tuesday that she accepted Ms Gilligan's death was preventable; however, she found Ms Lowry's supervision and Endeavour's systems regarding diet were both appropriate.

    

    "The concerns expressed by Ms Gilligan's family must be balanced against the fact that Ms Gilligan was a challenging client who did not cope well with new support workers," Ms Gallagher said.

    

    Support workers had given varying views to the inquest about whether giving fast food to a client was appropriate - with one suggesting it may actually support their independence.

    

    However, the worker noted that the decision should still be communicated with and resolved with the appropriate decision-maker - which would have been Ms Gilligan's mother in this case.

    

    A dietitian had given evidence saying she would not consider a burger or bun appropriate for Ms Gilligan's specific meal plan.

    

    Ms Gallagher ultimately agreed that the burger, even if cut up into small pieces, would not have complied with the standards set out for Ms Gilligan's diet - but she pointed out that Ms Gilligan's meal plan had contained other foods that also didn't comply with the standards.

    

    She said this had been due to competing considerations surrounding Ms Gilligan's quality of life, and the foods included had been ones Ms Gilligan liked or ones that gave her some level of autonomy or independence.

    

    Ms Gallagher considered it was not unreasonable for Ms Lowry to have thought a burger was suitable under the circumstances, given she had not been given specific training on the requirements of Ms Gilligan's "soft and bite-sized" diet, and the meal plan had included sandwiches.

    

    "Ms Lowry's evidence, which was not challenged, was that she had given Ms Gilligan cheeseburgers before, and Ms Gilligan had been able to eat them when they had been cut up into small pieces," Ms Gallagher noted.

    

    The inquest heard Endeavour had multiple policies and procedures in place to ensure workers had adequate information and training to care for a person with eating and swallowing difficulties.

    

    Endeavour staff had emphasised during the inquest that the support worker's goal is to support their clients to live as independently as possible.

    

    They acknowledged that the introduction of the NDIS had created a separation between Supported Independent Living providers like Endeavour and their client's other health services, however.

    

    In Ms Gilligan's case, the court heard this resulted in delayed follow ups with her other health services, a lack of collaboration on meal plans, and no guidance or training being provided to Ms Gilligan's support workers about her specific diet requirements.

    

    Ms Gallagher found Endeavour had not received sufficient information from Ms Gilligan's health care providers to identify the "real risk" bread products posed to her, and acknowledged Ms Gilligan's risk of choking was not appropriately managed by her overall system of care.

    

    "Endeavour has made sensible and appropriate changes to its systems and processes since Ms Gilligan's death to ensure that a similar tragic outcome does not occur in the future," Ms Gallagher said.

    

    "... I do not accept, on the evidence before me, that the responsibility for the circumstances which led to Ms Gilligan's death lies with Endeavour Foundation or their employees and agents."

    

    She found four recommendations suggested by Endeavour to be practical - including that the NDIS or NDIA review the processes for Supported Independent Living providers to access information about allied health supports, and consider appropriate funding in NDIA plans to ensure training of Supported Independent Living staff.

Headline: Thriving Kids explained: What Labor's NDIS alternative means for you
Full Text:
Finer details of the $4bn Thriving Kids program designed to shift many kids off the NDIS have finally been released. So what does it mean for families, and what questions remain? 

    Albanese to move children with mild autism off NDIS funding

    

    State and Territory leaders are considering a secret NDIS report as Federal Labor looks to lock in plans for...

    

    more less

    

    The Labor government has finally revealed the proposed model for its $4bn Thriving Kids plan, but parents, stakeholders and educators are still calling for more details.

    

    Thriving Kids will support children aged 8 and under with developmental delay and/or autism with low to moderate support needs.

    

    Health and Disability Minister Mark Butler said the new program would be "easy to access" and won't require a costly and lengthy formal diagnosis.

    

    Its four-pillar design places parents as children's "most important teacher and support".

    

    But questions still remain on the implications for the potentially hundreds of thousands of children set to be on the program, as the ball sits in the states' and territories' court.

    

    The states and territories will deliver Thriving Kids and Mr Butler said the government plans to

    

   What is Thriving Kids?

     Thriving Kids of the National Disability Insurance Scheme (NDIS), which is projected to reach $63.4bn by 2028-29.

    

    It will shift young children aged 8 and under with developmental delay and/or autism with low to moderate support needs from the NDIS into state-led support streams.

    

    The Albanese government says it is confident the program will deliver children the support they need to thrive.

    

    But parents of children on the scheme fear Thriving Kids will and place them in a one-size-fits-all model.

    

    Thriving Kids will roll out from October 1 this year, with full implementation by January 2028.

    

    The $4bn price tag will be split 50/50 between the federal government and states and territories.

    

   Who will be on Thriving Kids?

     While Thriving Kids is designed to ease the pressure on the NDIS by for young children with low to moderate support needs, Mr Butler could not say how many children will be on the scheme.

    

    "In terms of numbers, the obvious place to start is broadly the number of children on the NDIS under the age of nine now with low to moderate support needs, and we can quantify that broadly," he said.

    

    "I want to stress that until 1 January 2028, when Thriving Kids is fully operational, NDIS is still open for those children. There won't be access changes made until the beginning of 2028."

    

    According to the latest NDIS quarterly report, there were 170,786 children younger than nine with an NDIS plan.

    

    But a proportion of these children have a disability beyond the coverage of Thriving Kids.

    

    Children with permanent and significant disability and children aged 8 and under with high support needs will remain eligible for the NDIS.

    

   How will it work?

     Mr Butler said Thriving Kids will have "essentially four pillars".

    

    "The first pillar is to raise awareness and early identification of children with developmental issues," Mr Butler said.

    

    "The second is to provide ready access to information, advice, and navigation for parents in particular.

    

    "The third is to build the skills of parents.

    

    "And the fourth pillar is to provide access to targeted support for children and parents who need it.

    

    "Those targeted supports will be provided by trained workers, usually allied health professionals like speech pathologists, occupational therapists, physiotherapists, and the like."

    

    States and territories will be responsible for delivering these supports and services. ?

    

    But the pillars place more responsibility on parents to access and provide support for their children.

    

   What do stakeholders say?

     Children and Young People with Disability Australia (CYDA) chief executive Skye Kakoschke-Moore said her organisation was glad to see the final report, but still needed more details.

    

    "The decision-makers are the Commonwealth, states and territories, and they will ultimately decide what will and won't be funded," she said.

    

    "We're waiting for the details of the bilateral agreements to be released.

    

    "What would be really helpful is more information from the states and territories about the sorts of support they want to build on.

    

    "And more information about gaps they should have identified in service type and location."

    

    Autism Association of Australia chief executive Nicole Rogerson said families were still seeking information of what Thriving Kids will look like "on the ground".

    

    "The community have wanted to know more about what Thriving Kids actually means in practice," Ms Rogerson said.

    

    "Today we do have more information, which is welcome, but it also raises new and important questions about how this will work on the ground."

    

    The Royal Australian College of GPs welcomed the release of the plan.

    

    "This is a welcome shift back to putting children and families at the heart of early support," said its president, Dr Michael Wright.

    

    "But this system will only succeed if general practice is properly supported to play its central role in early identification and navigation.

    

    "The model is built on key principles: child and family-centred care, early identification, evidence-based supports, cultural safety, flexible access, and services delivered in children's everyday environments."

    

    Teachers and early educators will be major players in the new model.

    

    Australian Education Union president Correna Haythorpe said she fears the model may increase teachers' workload.

    

    "The AEU welcomes the intent of the Thriving Kids program, but its success will depend entirely on how it is implemented," Ms Haythorpe said.

    

    "Thriving Kids must support teachers, not shift the burden of under-resourced services onto schools."

    

    What's your NDIS experience and will your family be affected by Thriving Kids? Share your story with us at

Headline: Nine years after concerns were raised, Mater staff feel action will occur
Full Text:
Calvary Mater Newcastle staff feel for the first time that action will be taken to fix the mould fiasco at the hospital, after almost a decade of frustration over maintenance trouble.

    In NSW Parliament on Tuesday, the Opposition and Greens put pressure on the Minns government to ensure it follows through on promised action.

    

    Greens MP Amanda Cohn said "there were critical safety and maintenance issues at the Calvary Mater hospital that were reported by staff through the proper channels as early as 2017".

    

    "It has taken years for there to be the attention on this issue that it deserves," Dr Cohn said.

    

    Staff estimate the systemic mould issue has been occurring for at least a year, but water leaks and other maintenance trouble had been happening for years.

    

    Shadow Health Minister Sarah Mitchell said "the problem we have is there are repeated incidents every week".

    

    "The staff up there literally told the Newcastle Herald it feels like an episode of Stranger Things . This is not what a health service should look like in 2026."

    

    After meeting NSW Health Minister Ryan Park at the hospital on Monday, staff "felt heard for the first time and that action was going to be taken", one staffer said.

    

    "His remarks and obvious frustration are exactly our experience when dealing with the PPP [public-private partnership]."

    

    As for fixing the mould problem, Mr Park said "it has to happen".

    

    "I'm determined to do it as fast as I possibly can," he said.

    

    The Newcastle Herald reported on Monday that most of the intensive care unit and rooms in three wards were shut at the weekend for "deep cleaning".

    

    These areas had "slightly elevated levels" of the mould Aspergillus fumigatus

    

    The hospital also closed two wards in January due to mould in the air-conditioning ducts and maggots falling from the ceiling onto patients.

    

    This included the closure of a haematology day ward - also known as ward 5D - with cancer patients moved to another ward.

    

    Mr Park said, "I am deeply angry and sorry that staff are in this position".

    

    "They shouldn't have to be worrying about trying to manage this situation with private contractors who should be doing their job."

    

    The Mater is a public hospital run by a private company called Novacare, which is responsible for the building maintenance and cleaning.

    

    Honeywell is also responsible for the hospital's maintenance, as one of four companies that make up Novacare, along with Westpac Bank, Medirest and Abigroup.

    

    Mr Park said the government was looking at legal options and would "hold the contractors to account".

    

    Late last year, the minister "brought in the contractors on multiple occasions at my office in Sydney and at Parliament House".

    

    "I'd been given the clear impression and undertaking that there was a pathway forward to deal particularly with the cladding, which will go a long way to deal with the water and mould-related issues.

    

    "Over the summer break, it was clear - although there'd been scaffolding put up - that work was not happening."

    

    Mr Park said he and NSW Health secretary Susan Pearce made a decision to "step into" the PPP contract and "begin the cladding rectification works".

    

    Staff say the cladding will help, but the mould was "being caused by the air-conditioning system and humidity".

    

    Wallsend MP Sonia Hornery said "works on the mould infested air-conditioning have begun".

story_title: Calvary Mater Hospital Faces Inquiry Over Maintenance Failures

Headline: Thriving Kids program edges closer, but February deadline remains
Full Text:
A multi-billion dollar plan to get kids off the NDIS is edging closer, but a key deadline remains.

    The Albanese government has passed an important "milestone" in its bid to get children with mild autism or developmental needs off the NDIS, but a key deadline still looms.

    

    The Thriving Kids Advisory Group on Tuesday recommended children under the age of nine with low to moderate needs would be better serviced outside the nationwide disability scheme.

    

    The government hopes the program will alleviate pressure on the beleaguered NDIS, but has faced pushback from states and territories who say they were not consulted prior to it being announced.

    

    The Albanese government agreed to invest $2bn, or half of the const, to deliver Thriving Kids, but has been negotiating with state and territory governments over their 50 per cent.

    

    Last week, Commonwealth, state, and territory governments agreed on a funding split that would see the federal government cough up at least $1.4bn of the $2bn commitment directly to states and territories after an impasse over hospital funding was ended.

    

    Health Minister Mark Butler said all states and territories had since also signed an agreement on foundational supports on Monday, including schedules for the Thriving Kids program.

    

    That agreement sets the stage for one final deadline for bilateral agreements to be concluded between the Commonwealth and the states before South Australia enters caretaker mode in late February.

    

    Mr Butler said the agreement sets out the "overarching principles" which will now be subject to negotiations with the states and territories to ensure funds are flowing by February 20.

    

    "We were aiming to wrap up all of those bilateral schedules or bilateral agreements with every state and territory government before South Australia goes into caretaker later in February," he said.

    

    "So we've got a lot of work to do over coming weeks, but we have done a huge amount of work in just five months to kick start a process that had, frankly, started to drift and to provide parents and all of those other groups – educators and community groups – who wrap their arms around parents, who have children with developmental issues, to give them confidence there will be a program in place to give their children every opportunity to thrive."

    

    South Australians will head to the polls in March, prior to which the state government will enter caretaker mode, putting a handbrake on most government business.

    

    The poll had put pressure on the Commonwealth, states, and territories to agree to a public hospital funding deal at national cabinet last week following years of negotiations.

    

    Addressing the Thriving Kids program, Mr Butler said the Albanese government had come into power with an NDIS scheme that was "still growing 22 per cent per annum, placing a very significant burden on the budget".

    

    "I also spoke of the need to address what I viewed as an alarming number of young children who had been enrolled in the NDIS … a scheme that was obviously designed for significant and permanent, usually lifelong disability," he said.

    

    Mr Butler said Thriving Kids, which the advisory group recommended focus on identification and support in "everyday settings", would drive down the growth rate of the NDIS and ensure it was sustainable long term.

    

    Professor Frank Oberklaid AM, advisory group co-chair, said treatment of relevant children under the NDIS was a "broken system".

    

    "It did not work for kids, and pediatricians were becoming increasingly frustrated about how it distorted clinical practice," he said.

    

    "We wanted an approach that got away from looking at what's wrong with a child and what do we need to treat, towards acknowledging individual differences, acknowledging that many, many children just need a bit of support over six or 12 months in order to thrive, and their parents need support."

    

    Originally published as Thriving Kids program edges closer, but February deadline remains

story_title: Government Unveils 'Thriving Kids' NDIS Alternative Program
story_summary: The Albanese government has released the model for its $4 billion 'Thriving Kids' program, a major reform designed to shift children aged eight and under with developmental delays or mild autism from the National Disability Insurance Scheme (NDIS) to state-led support systems. While the move has been welcomed by some stakeholders, disability groups, parent advocates, and the Australian Education Union have called for more operational details and raised concerns about implementation, parental control, and potential increased workloads for teachers. The program is set for a phased rollout starting in late 2024, with full implementation by 2028.

Headline: NT Health receive 222 new doctors
Full Text:
The NT Government is celebrating the arrival of more than 200 much-needed doctors, with Health Minister Steve Edgington saying he can sense a turn in health worker retention. Details.

    The NT Government is celebrating the arrival of more than 200 much-needed doctors, while Health Minister Steve Edgington says he can sense a turn in health worker retention.

    

    A total of 222 doctors have started work with NT Health, with the large intake bringing their expertise to major towns across the Territory.

    

    Fifty intern doctors have commenced their duties at Royal Darwin and Palmerston hospitals with 36 Resident Medical Officers and 100 registrars starting alongside them.

    

    Rotations from this cohort include:

    

    Katherine Hospital: 5 interns and 35 RMOs;

    

    Gove District Hospital: 10 interns and 24 RMOs;

    

    Alice Springs Hospital: 3 interns will rotate from Royal Darwin Hospital for the first time.

    

    Meanwhile at Alice Springs Hospital, 10 doctors have commenced alongisde 26 RMOs.

    

    Tennant Creek Hospital: 5 RMOs from this cohort will complete a rotation.

    

    Mr Edgington said he was particularly pleased with the positive retention numbers, with fresh data showing 78 per cent of RDH trainee doctors had stayed in the NT to continue medical training.

    

    "Our focus is simple: grow the health workforce so Territorians get better care and stronger frontline services, whether you live in Darwin, Katherine, Gove, Alice Springs or Tennant Creek," he said.

    

    "I'm pleased that a large proportion of NT Health's Royal Darwin and Palmerston Hospital doctor-in-training workforce is continuing from 2025 into 2026, with a retention rate of 78 per cent, a significant increase on last year.

    

    "In Central Australia, more than 40 trainee doctors have also chosen to stay on at Alice Springs Hospital in 2026."

    

    Director Medical Services of RDH, Dr Danika Thiemt, welcomed the new arrivals.

    

    "Doctors in Training are at the heart of our hospitals, providing essential frontline care and playing a critical role in delivering high quality medicine across the NT," she said.

    

    "We are pleased to welcome all new staff joining Royal Darwin and Palmerston Hospitals in 2026, and we are delighted that so many of our doctors have chosen to continue their careers in the Top End and contribute to the Northern Territory community.

    

    "We hope all our new doctors find living and working in the Territory as rewarding as we do."

    

    Down south, assistant general manager of Alice Springs Hospital, Melissa Henwood, was also pleased.

    

    "Interns make a valuable contribution to the delivery of high-quality patient care, and we are committed to supporting them throughout their first year of clinical practice and important stage of their professional journey," she said.

    

    "Their arrival strengthens the hospital's workforce and enhances our capacity to meet service demand and the healthcare needs of our community."

    

    The new reinforcements mark another significant health-related announcement in recent days.

    

    Prime Minister Anthony Albanese promised an extra $1 billion to the NT in hospital funding, which Chief Minister Lia Finocchiaro called a "pivotal moment" for the jurisdiction.

    

    "The Territory will come home with a historic deal, and while we would have liked more, it represents a significant increase that will go a long way to delivering better healthcare for Territorians," she said.

    

    "We are a small population with a vast landmass, significant vulnerability, and real need."

Headline: Funding cut put Victoria's vaccine database at risk
Full Text:
February 3, 2026 — 11:30am

    

    Victorian health officials feared the state's childhood immunisation database was at risk of collapse after the Allan government pulled funding and local councils threatened to abandon the system over new fees.

    

    Internal correspondence released under freedom of information, 15 months after The Age lodged the request, detail the hamfisted attempts to plug the funding gap that councils warned could compromise immunisation rates in Victoria.

    

    The Department of Health had warned that Central Immunisation Records Victoria — a platform used to record vaccines, bookings, consent and stock — was at risk if councils refused to pay a new co-payment fee to plug a $2 million funding hole.

    

    The department had previously assured local councils its state-run record system would remain free.

    

    But in its 2024-25 budget, the Victorian government unexpectedly withheld funding to operate the platform.

    

    "Provision of $2.0 million p.a. funding for the department to support the ongoing operations of CIRV from 24/25 onwards was not provided in the recent state budget," a June 2024 department brief said.

    

    The department feared that pursuing councils for the full cost to cover the funding shortfall would be too risky and potentially "compromise their current immunisation activities". And if too many councils walked away from the platform, health officials also feared there wouldn't be enough money to operate it.

    

    Instead, councils were blindsided with a $6000 annual fee to use the system, plus a $2 charge for every jab administered.

    

    "This has landed in a good place I think," Health Department Secretary Euan Wallace wrote in a June 2024 email about the initial fee proposed.

    

    If every council agreed to pay, the proposed funding arrangement would have recouped $1.2 million of $2 million needed each year to operate the database.

    

    Under a national program, councils do about 430,000 immunisations for Victorian children each year. This represents about 90 per cent of all vaccinations for school-aged kids and 45 per cent of those for younger children and babies.

    

    Councils are required to report this data to the Department of Health, either through Central Immunisation Records Victoria or privately operated databases.

    

    Following a barrage of complaints, the department twice backed down, delaying the rollout of a $2.32 fee per-jab fee, capped at $25,000 a year, after receiving a barrage of complaints.

    

    Several local councils asked how they could "hand back" responsibility to the state, complaining that they were already disproportionately bearing the cost of immunisations.

    

    The department maintained councils were welcome to move to private competitors to record their vaccinations. But internal correspondence shows the department dreaded this possibility and reduced the co-payment fee to stop councils leaving for commercial alternatives.

    

    "Have reduced pricing to councils to ensure CIRV is cheaper than its main competition," briefing documents state.

    

    Councils also warned the department they could be forced to scale down vaccination programs, cease other critical services, or charge vulnerable families.

    

    "I have no choice but to re-inform you that we will not be paying," Wellington Shire Council general manager of development Andrew Pomeroy told the department in August 2024.

    

    "Another one …," then-deputy secretary of eHealth Victoria Dr Lance Emerson wrote the same month, forwarding a complaint from Maroondah City Council.

    

    Amid the funding fight, the main competitor to the government system, VaxApp, proactively approached municipalities to pitch their product.

    

    Moira Shire asked the City of Melbourne why they already used VaxApp and if they would ever turn to the government's record system.

    

    "We would never consider moving back to CIRV (even if it was still free)," the City of Melbourne replied.

    

    Seventy councils and health services used the record system in 2024. This has since fallen to 40, with at least one more council, the City of Yarra, preparing to transition away from it.

    

    Councils explained they had invested significant resources in migrating to and developing staff proficiency in Central Immunisation Records Victoria in good faith, on the basis it would always be free.

    

    Despite the correspondence revealing that Warrnambool City Council had asked how to "hand back" immunisation services to the department, chief executive Andrew Mason said the council had "very reluctantly" agreed to pay the fee but remained "deeply disappointed" in the cost-shifting.

    

    "We came to the conclusion that charging users to cover the software cost was a poor public health outcome and could result in reduced immunisation rates," he said in a statement to The Age

    

    Stonnington Council also stayed with the platform.

    

    "Council is concerned that exiting CIRV would fragment public health data across private platforms and undermine data security," a spokesman said.

    

    "Council already subsidises the majority of its immunisation program, and the introduction of a fee to register vaccinations, which councils are required by law to do, blindsided the industry and represents further cost shifting to local government."

    

    A Department of Health spokeswoman said vaccination was one of the most effective ways to protect the health of Victorians.

    

    "The Victorian government funded the development and initial operation of CIRV to cover the council's costs. If councils do not wish to use this platform, it is not mandatory."

Headline: Surgeons in shock as controversial heart surgeon appointed Queensland patient safety lead
Full Text:
Surgeons in Queensland are in shock as a heart surgeon who previously had conditions placed on his registration is appointed to a top patient safety role.

    Queensland surgeons are in shock after a cardiothoracic surgeon who previously had conditions placed on his registration by the medical regulator was appointed as the state's top doctor in surgical quality and safety.

    

    Michael Byrom has been appointed by Clinical Excellence Queensland as the state's inaugural medical and surgical lead who will head the establishment of a new surgical quality and assurance committee that will identify and address systemic issues contributing to preventable surgical morbidity and mortality. He will also lead a program of quality improvement in surgery to improve outcomes.

    

    Dr Byrom was at the centre of controversy in NSW six years ago when he was subject to investigation by medical regulators arising from mandatory notifications made by surgical colleagues on the grounds of patient safety.

    

    The Medical Council of NSW took the rare step in 2020 of imposing conditions on Dr Byrom's practice in order to protect the public agreed to by him and reported on the register of the Australian Healthcare Practitioner Regulation Agency, that restricted the cardiothoracic surgeon from performing complex surgery and subjected him to a program of supervision and retraining.

    

    The heart surgeon was also barred for a period from supervising trainee surgeons.

    

    The conditions were later lifted and there are currently no restrictions listed by AHPRA on Dr Byrom's medical registration.

    

    Dr Byrom and his former employer, Royal Prince Alfred Hospital, said at the time the conditions were imposed because the surgeon had not practised cardiothoracic surgery for an extended period of time following the allegations that were made against him by colleagues, and he required a program of supervised reskilling.

    

    Clinical Excellence Queensland deputy director-general Helen Brown issued a circular to all staff at Queensland Health on Tuesday announcing Dr Byrom's appointment as surgical medical lead, which she said marked "an important step in strengthening statewide quality and safety".

    

    Dr Byrom will be the clinical excellence agency's key point of contact in its work partnering with the Queensland Surgical Services Clinical Network, the Queensland National Surgical Quality Improvement Program, the Royal Australasian College of Surgeons, clinicians, patients and other key stakeholders "to embed a culture of safety, learning and continuous improvement".

    

    Dr Brown described Dr Byrom as a cardiothoracic surgeon with extensive experience in surgical quality improvement and clinical governance.

    

    Dr Byrom was the subject of reporting in The Australian in 2019 after three concerned NSW surgeons lodged a complaint regarding his practice with medical regulators, alleging preventable deaths. Dr Byrom denied the allegations and contended they were lodged by former business partners who were motivated by malice. Regulators and administrators at RPA were criticised at the time for allowing Dr Byrom to repeatedly return to unsupervised practice despite commissioned reviews finding his surgical practice did not meet expected standards.

    

    Dr Byrom said in the wake of the Medical Council conditions that the NSW regulator had found his surgical results to be consistent with those of his peers and had found no evidence of any risks to the safety of his patients.

    

    Some within leadership positions at Australia's surgical medical college, RACS, as well as Queensland branch of the college, were flabbergasted on Tuesday to be informed of Dr Byrom's appointment. RACS had privately endorsed Sunshine Coast-based pediatric surgeon Bhavesh Patel for the top clinical excellence role.

    

    "I have had all sorts of calls from all sorts of people saying 'are you serious?'" said one Queensland surgeon of the response from colleagues. "This person is supposed to set best practice for medicine and surgery. Why was the background of Dr Byrom not addressed in the release?"

    

    Dr Byrom, who has recently been working in a public hospital in Brisbane as a cardiothoracic surgeon after a stint in Geelong in Victoria, said in the QH Update that he regarded his elevation to the top patient safety role as a privilege.

    

    "Clinicians carry the responsibility of making a profound impact in the lives of patients," Dr Byrom said in the circular.

    

    "Leveraging the expertise of Clinical Excellence Queensland, these initiatives aim to provide clinicians with the support and tools to deliver better outcomes for their patients and to the population of Queensland."

    

    Queensland Health defended the appointment on Tuesday night, with a spokesperson saying Dr Byrom was "appointed following a rigorous recruitment process that included extensive background checks and full transparency. He has no restrictions placed on him by the Australian Health Practitioner Regulation Agency."

Headline: Thriving Kids aims to 'mainstream' disability support
Full Text:
Families, schools and early learning providers will bear greater responsibility for caring for children with developmental delays and mild autism in a major shake-up of Australia's disability support system.

    The $4 billion Thriving Kids program aims to help reduce the soaring price tag of the National Disability Insurance Scheme, which is projected to cost $46 billion this financial year.

    

    The government signed a deal with the states on Monday to provide extra support outside the NDIS for children with more moderate disabilities.

    

    Health Minister Mark Butler said the program would allow children to get support without having to spend years chasing a formal diagnosis.

    

    "(Support services) will be available where children and their parents live and learn and play in the community in a broad-based, mainstream way," he told reporters in Canberra on Tuesday.

    

    Children under nine will be covered by the Thriving Kids program, which Mr Butler said would begin rolling out from the start of October, and be fully operational by 2028.

    

    Frank Oberklaid, a pediatrician who worked with Mr Butler to develop the program's principles, said it would be a major shift in the way children with disabilities were supported.

    

    "(The old system) did not work for kids," he said, speaking alongside the health minister.

    

    "The focus on the child's weaknesses and making a diagnosis doesn't work for kids.

    

    "Pediatric practice is about what does this child and this family need over the next six, 12 months? What sort of supports do they need to make a difference, not what is the diagnosis?" Professor Oberklaid said.

    

    Under the principles for the disability program, parents will be provided with resources like online courses, short videos and peer support groups to help them support their children.

    

    Children and families who need extra help will also get access to specialists including occupational therapists, speech pathologists, physiotherapists and psychologists.

    

    "It won't be a traditional Medicare-funded allied health professional visit that might be bulk billed or might attract a gap fee. This will be quite a different model than state governments particularly asked us for," Mr Butler said.

    

    The health minister said schools and early learning centres would play a crucial role in identifying and supporting young people with developmental delays.

story_summary: The Albanese government has released the model for its $4 billion 'Thriving Kids' program, a major reform designed to shift children aged eight and under with developmental delays or mild autism from the National Disability Insurance Scheme (NDIS) to state-led support systems. Championed by Health Minister Mark Butler, the program aims to provide community-based support and easier access to early intervention without requiring a formal medical diagnosis, in an effort to make the NDIS more sustainable. While the move has been welcomed by some stakeholders, disability groups, parent advocates, and the Australian Education Union have called for more operational details and raised concerns about implementation, parental control, and potential increased workloads for teachers. The program is set for a phased rollout starting in late 2024, with full implementation by 2028.
