Headline: Children won't need an autism diagnosis to use Thriving Kids scheme
Full Text:
Parents will no longer have to spend thousands of dollars and dozens of hours chasing autism diagnoses for their struggling children to qualify for

   government disability support under the new Thriving Kids program, which will help children with lower support needs outside the NDIS.

    

    After federal and state governments last week signed a long-awaited health and disability funding deal , Health and Disability Minister Mark Butler on Tuesday unveiled the model for Thriving Kids and revealed it would mainly be run through states and territories.

    

    The Albanese government rebuffed a suggestion from its advisory group that children access therapies through new Medicare plans, and will instead give states money to run the scheme their own way. "We won't be funding services directly. We'll be providing that money to states," Butler said.

    

    "[They] will largely leverage off a lot of programs they already run... It won't be a traditional Medicare-funded allied health professional visit, that might be bulk billed or might attract a gap fee. This will be quite a different model that state governments, particularly, asked us for."

    

    Each jurisdiction will devise its own system for supporting tens of thousands of children, aged eight and under, who have autism or developmental delays – many of whom currently have individual packages through the $48 billion NDIS.

    

    State systems will be expected to adhere to the same model, which has four key functions: identifying when children have developmental delays or differences; providing easy access to information about where to get help; supplying programs and tools for parents to build their own skills; and providing targeted support, including therapies and equipment, for children who need it.

    

    The federal government will support Thriving Kids with national components – such as information campaigns, online resources and a call line – but it will otherwise be delivered by states and territories, which will scale up their existing services as well as establish new ones. The Albanese government will finalise individual agreements with each jurisdiction by the end of this month.

    

    Tuesday's release of the Thriving Kids model comes after Butler's taskforce took over the design last August because the states and territories were too slow, after first agreeing to help run new services for kids in late 2023 . The taskforce, led by Professor Frank Oberklaid, consulted over five months with health, disability and education stakeholders.

    

    Oberklaid said the current system was broken. "Paediatricians were getting requests from inexperienced teachers with the words: please confirm a diagnosis of autism so this child can get services. It totally distorted clinical practice," he said.

    

    Autism diagnoses have soared in Australia in the last decade – a trend some experts attribute to the NDIS requirements Sixteen per cent of all Australian six-year-old boys now rely on the scheme.

    

    Under Thriving Kids, families will no longer receive individualised funding or packages, as currently exists under the NDIS. But children will not need any formal diagnosis to access services, which should eliminate lengthy wait times for NDIS support.

    

    "The thing people have complained most about ... is the time it takes to get a diagnosis, the need for a formal diagnosis, which can take years to access and cost thousands of dollars, before parents are given any support for their children," Butler said on Tuesday.

    

    "This will be easy to access. It won't require a formal diagnosis. And those supports, importantly, will be child and family centred."

    

    How the Thriving Kids model will work

    

    The first layer of the Thriving Kids scheme will involve improving the ways that children's developmental support needs are identified – whether through community workers, GPs, or early childhood educators and teachers – so that this happens early on.

    

    Next, there should be several access points that enable parents to seek more help. This should involve a national website and phone line, where parents can ask questions and be directed towards local services and support. There could also be physical entry points, such as at family and children's hubs, or GPs could guide families to further supports.

    

    The third pillar of Thriving Kids will be a program of "Universal Parenting Supports". This should include online courses, webinars and workshops that help parents understand their children's needs and how they can help. Peer-support groups for parents should offer another environment where they can build community.

    

    Supported playgroups also fall into this category, where parents can connect while their children develop social skills, and allied health professionals offer guidance. Family programs that help kids with positive play, communication and emotional regulation would be delivered by trained facilitators.

    

    The fourth element of Thriving Kids will be "Targeted Support", which most closely mirrors the services currently available through the NDIS. This will be available to children who need more occupational therapy, speech pathology, physiotherapy, psychology or other therapies.

    

    Unlike the NDIS model, which gives each family annual funding packages to spend on therapists, these supports will be time-limited and focused on specific goals. A diagnosis won't be required, but a health professional or Thriving Kids provider will make a light-touch needs assessment.

    

    Some children might just require a single type of support, while those with multidisciplinary needs will be connected to a mix of allied health professionals and support services. For the latter group, one "key worker" will help to co-ordinate their care.

    

    Thriving Kids should also make sure that basic equipment – currently accessed through NDIS funding – is made available, for example through loan pools or equipment libraries. It should offer access to tools like laminated cards and communications boards; sensory cushions, calm-down aids and emotion-regulation charts; velcro shoes, adapted cutlery and non-slip mats; and modified high chairs or simple standing frames.

    

    A group of disability organisations on Tuesday said there were still significant uncertainties about how supports, particularly targeted therapies, would be funded and delivered. They said all governments must promise a nationally consistent set of standards, and publish clear implementation plans.

    

    "Without these commitments, people with disability and families risk facing fragmented systems, inconsistent access and widening service gaps," they said.

    

    Next steps will test capacity

    

    But delivery details are still being worked out, and last week's agreement pushed back the start date: Thriving Kids will start rolling out from October this year and launch fully by January 2028.

    

    The review said the "targeted supports" should be delivered where children live, learn and play – such as schools, childcare, at home, or in community hubs. However, delivering Thriving Kids through schools or childcare would need to be carefully designed, so that their main purpose remained education.

    

    The taskforce anticipates teething pains as the workforce adjusts and services are rolled out. "There may be wait times to access some supports," their report said.

    

    To help families waiting, it said Thriving Kids should provide paper-based and online resources that help parents support their kids. These would include games to build motor skills, language exercises and social strategies.

    

    Butler said he expected Thriving Kids to cater to the existing number of children who currently use the NDIS for low or moderate supports. There were 120,444 children in that category on the NDIS last financial year, this masthead has reported , who receive about $1.8 billion in annual payments. The minister said he also expected additional children who had fallen through the cracks of the NDIS to start using Thriving Kids.

    

    The state and federal governments have so far committed to spending $2 billion each over five years to set up and run Thriving Kids. Butler said the Commonwealth would give $1.6 billion of its portion directly to states and territories, and that he anticipated the $4 billion total funding would be sufficient.

Headline: Model revealed for Thriving Kids, the NDIS alternative for some children with developmental delay or autism
Full Text:
After months of uncertainty and speculation, the federal government has revealed how its National Disability Insurance Scheme (NDIS) alternative for some children under eight will work.

    NDIS Minister Mark Butler released the model for Thriving Kids today, marking another step towards a generational reset for the way disability support is accessed and received in Australia.

    

    He said the model aimed to empower parents to support their children, and was in part returning to a system of support that existed prior to the implementation of the NDIS.

    

    Announced without warning last August , Thriving Kids aims to divert children under eight years old with "mild to moderate" developmental delays and autism away from the NDIS.

    

    The model for Thriving Kids was designed with the help of an advisory group, chaired by paediatrician Frank Oberklaid, and has been endorsed by national cabinet.

    

    The first stage of the model would focus on identification. Delays or support needs could be identified by a family member, a school or a health professional.

    

    Stage two would focus on connecting families to the appropriate support.

    

    These "entry points" to Thriving Kids would include online directories to help families find supports, Thriving Kids-aligned health professionals, and resources to upskill educators and medical practitioners working with children.

    

    Stage three would be the actual supports, which were broken up into four categories.

    

    "Low need" families would be pointed towards supports aimed at "empowering parent-led approaches" such as online parenting courses focused on development and neurodiversity, locally organised group activities, peer support and a potential national phone line for parenting advice.

    

    "Moderate need" families and children would have access to more targeted supports from allied health professionals and early childhood workers. Those could be delivered in community hubs, the home or educational settings, and include speech, occupational and physio therapies.

    

    "Low to moderate need" families would straddle both categories of supports.

    

    Children with "significant permanent disability" — including developmental delay or autism — will remain eligible for the NDIS. Those on the scheme before 2028 will stay on it, subject to usual reassessment criteria.

    

    Mr Butler said helping parents better to support their children was critical.

    

    "It's not rocket science. It's been quite well understood that the best thing you can do for a child you is not hitting developmental milestones is to provide parents with support to support their own children," he told reporters.

    

    Mr Butler said many parents would still need additional support from trained workers.

    

    "That is very much contemplated by the program and will be able to be delivered through it and [we] want to make sure that that's as easy to access as possible as well," he said.

    

    Model released for NDIS alternative for some autistic children

    

    The first phase of the program is set to start in October this year, before the full rollout finishes in January 2028.

    

    Thriving Kids is a central pillar of the government's plan to rein in the growth of the NDIS, which now supports more than 750,000 participants — around a quarter of whom are under eight years old — and costs $50 billion a year.

    

    When Labor was elected in 2022, the NDIS was growing at 22 per cent per annum.

    

    Yearly growth is now at 10.1 per cent and the government wants to see it come down further to 5 or 6 per cent — closer to those for aged care and Medicare.

Full Text:
News
Artists across the Hunter are being encouraged to pitch concepts for the Musselbrook Hospital Stage 3 upgrade. New South Wales Health says the artworks will help make clinical spaces more welcoming for patients and staff. The new operating theatre suite has already been completed with the wider project due for completion in 2027. Details are available online at Have Your Say New South Wales The Calvary Mater Hospital in Newcastle will soon see major repair works with the state stepping in to replace non-compliant cladding. New South Wales Health says it was forced to act after ongoing delays by facilities partner, NovaCare. Improvement works on the building's air conditioning system are also underway, and mould remediation planning continues. The government says its priority is maintaining the hospital's safety and functionality.

story_title: Calvary Mater Hospital Faces Inquiry Over Maintenance Failures
story_summary: A New South Wales parliamentary inquiry has been established to investigate serious management and maintenance failures at the Calvary Mater Hospital in Newcastle. The inquiry follows numerous critical incidents, including deadly mould in air-conditioning vents, maggot infestations, and water damage, which led to ward closures and cancelled cancer treatments. Driven by the Coalition and Greens over Labor's opposition, the investigation will examine the effectiveness of the hospital's public-private partnership (PPP) with maintenance provider Honeywell (as part of Novacare). After visiting the hospital, NSW Health Minister Ryan Park apologized to staff and announced the government was stepping in to begin urgent rectification works and exploring legal options to hold the private contractors accountable.

Headline: Parents, schools to take on autism care under Labor’s NDIS alternative
Full Text:
Children with mild autism will not need a medical diagnosis to access services under the 

   federal government's Thriving Kids program

   , but there will be more emphasis on parents and teachers providing care at home and in schools as Labor's alternative to the National Disability Insurance Scheme rolls out from next month.

   Health Minister Mark Butler on Tuesday announced details of the $4 billion scheme designed to stem the flood of children aged under nine with mild autism receiving funding from the NDIS.

   The new program aims to remove 

   tens of thousands of children with mild learning disorders

    who were never intended to be covered by the NDIS and return them to support services delivered through community centres, schools, early childhood centres and other local support networks. It is scheduled to be fully rolled out by the states on January 1, 2028.

   However, funding to the states, which will be largely responsible for overseeing services such as therapy for children, is expected to be finalised by February 20 before the South Australian government goes into caretaker mode ahead of a state election.

   Revealing details for the first time of how the controversial Thriving Kids scheme will work, Butler said parents would not need to go to a paediatrician to get a formal diagnosis to access services. This requirement for NDIS funding had been criticised because it was expensive for poorer families and resulted in long delays.

   However, the sheer volume of children receiving NDIS funding amid claims of overdiagnosis was at the heart of criticism of the 

   $50 billion scheme's

    budget blowout. One in six boys in Australia aged between five and seven have been enrolled in the scheme.

   Butler said Thriving Kids would be a return to community-based support networks which existed before the NDIS, giving parents the capability to support their children. However, there would also be a point where many families needed extra support from trained workers.

   "This will be easy to access. It won't require a formal diagnosis. And those supports, importantly, will be child and family centred. They will be available where children and their parents live and learn and play in the community in a broad-based, mainstream way," he said.

   "It's getting back to a system that long existed before the NDIS but has largely been dismantled because of the NDIS really covering the field. We want to make sure that parents have the confidence, have the capabilities, to support their children."

   He said children with moderate needs might be accessing as many as 70 therapy sessions a year, which would be delivered in schools or childcare settings. State governments would be largely responsible for delivering those services which would be provided in existing child and family centres.

   Thriving Kids is scheduled to be fully operational on January 1, 2028.

   Labor released the 

   Thriving Kids Advisory Report

    on Tuesday and said the scheme would have four pillars: identifying conditions early, providing access to information, building parents' skills and providing access to targeted support.The federal government is providing $2 billion, with $2 billion matched by states and territories, while $1.4 billion of the Commonwealth's contribution will go directly to support states and territories.

   Under the report's 16 recommendations, there would be a single portal of entry with multiple referral pathways for all children with developmental concerns whether they are in the NDIS or not. The scheme would be reviewed after 24 months with input from health bodies, paediatricians, psychiatrists, and nurses.

   The Albanese government secured a funding deal with the states on Friday in exchange for giving them an extra six months for its full implementation and upping its offer to fund public hospitals by $2 billion over the next five years for a total of $25 billion extra.

story_title: Government Unveils 'Thriving Kids' NDIS Alternative Program
story_summary: The Albanese government has released the model for its $4 billion 'Thriving Kids' program, a major reform designed to shift children aged eight and under with developmental delays or mild autism from the National Disability Insurance Scheme (NDIS) to state-led support systems. Championed by Health Minister Mark Butler, the program aims to provide community-based support and easier access to early intervention without requiring a formal medical diagnosis, in an effort to make the NDIS more sustainable. While the move has been welcomed by some stakeholders, disability groups, parent advocates, and the Australian Education Union have called for more operational details and raised concerns about implementation, parental control, and potential increased workloads for teachers. The program is set for a phased rollout starting in late 2024, with full implementation by 2028.

Headline: Thriving Kids program edges closer, but February deadline remains
Full Text:
A multi-billion dollar plan to get kids off the NDIS is edging closer, but a key deadline remains.

    The Albanese government has passed an important "milestone" in its bid to get children with mild autism or developmental needs off the NDIS, but a key deadline still looms.

    

    The Thriving Kids Advisory Group on Tuesday recommended children under the age of nine with low to moderate needs would be better serviced outside the nationwide disability scheme.

    

    The government hopes the program will alleviate pressure on the beleaguered NDIS, but has faced pushback from states and territories who say they were not consulted prior to it being announced.

    

    The Albanese government agreed to invest $2bn, or half of the const, to deliver Thriving Kids, but has been negotiating with state and territory governments over their 50 per cent.

    

    Last week, Commonwealth, state, and territory governments agreed on a funding split that would see the federal government cough up at least $1.4bn of the $2bn commitment directly to states and territories after an impasse over hospital funding was ended.

    

    Health Minister Mark Butler said all states and territories had since also signed an agreement on foundational supports on Monday, including schedules for the Thriving Kids program.

    

    That agreement sets the stage for one final deadline for bilateral agreements to be concluded between the Commonwealth and the states before South Australia enters caretaker mode in late February.

    

    Mr Butler said the agreement sets out the "overarching principles" which will now be subject to negotiations with the states and territories to ensure funds are flowing by February 20.

    

    "We were aiming to wrap up all of those bilateral schedules or bilateral agreements with every state and territory government before South Australia goes into caretaker later in February," he said.

    

    "So we've got a lot of work to do over coming weeks, but we have done a huge amount of work in just five months to kick start a process that had, frankly, started to drift and to provide parents and all of those other groups – educators and community groups – who wrap their arms around parents, who have children with developmental issues, to give them confidence there will be a program in place to give their children every opportunity to thrive."

    

    South Australians will head to the polls in March, prior to which the state government will enter caretaker mode, putting a handbrake on most government business.

    

    The poll had put pressure on the Commonwealth, states, and territories to agree to a public hospital funding deal at national cabinet last week following years of negotiations.

    

    Addressing the Thriving Kids program, Mr Butler said the Albanese government had come into power with an NDIS scheme that was "still growing 22 per cent per annum, placing a very significant burden on the budget".

    

    "I also spoke of the need to address what I viewed as an alarming number of young children who had been enrolled in the NDIS … a scheme that was obviously designed for significant and permanent, usually lifelong disability," he said.

    

    Mr Butler said Thriving Kids, which the advisory group recommended focus on identification and support in "everyday settings", would drive down the growth rate of the NDIS and ensure it was sustainable long term.

    

    Professor Frank Oberklaid AM, advisory group co-chair, said treatment of relevant children under the NDIS was a "broken system".

    

    "It did not work for kids, and pediatricians were becoming increasingly frustrated about how it distorted clinical practice," he said.

    

    "We wanted an approach that got away from looking at what's wrong with a child and what do we need to treat, towards acknowledging individual differences, acknowledging that many, many children just need a bit of support over six or 12 months in order to thrive, and their parents need support."

Headline: Funding cut put Victoria's vaccine database at risk
Full Text:
Victorian health officials feared the state's childhood immunisation database was at risk of collapse after the Allan government pulled funding and local councils threatened to abandon the system over new fees.

    Internal correspondence released under freedom of information, 15 months after The Age lodged the request, detail the hamfisted attempts to plug the funding gap that councils warned could compromise immunisation rates in Victoria.

    

    The Department of Health had warned that Central Immunisation Records Victoria — a platform used to record vaccines, bookings, consent and stock — was at risk if councils refused to pay a new co-payment fee to plug a $2 million funding hole.

    

    The department had previously assured local councils its state-run record system would remain free.

    

    But in its 2024-25 budget, the Victorian government unexpectedly withheld funding to operate the platform.

    

    "Provision of $2.0 million p.a. funding for the department to support the ongoing operations of CIRV from 24/25 onwards was not provided in the recent state budget," a June 2024 department brief said.

    

    The department feared that pursuing councils for the full cost to cover the funding shortfall would be too risky and potentially "compromise their current immunisation activities". And if too many councils walked away from the platform, health officials also feared there wouldn't be enough money to operate it.

    

    Instead, councils were blindsided with a $6000 annual fee to use the system, plus a $2 charge for every jab administered.

    

    "This has landed in a good place I think," Health Department Secretary Euan Wallace wrote in a June 2024 email about the initial fee proposed.

    

    If every council agreed to pay, the proposed funding arrangement would have recouped $1.2 million of $2 million needed each year to operate the database.

    

    Under a national program, councils do about 430,000 immunisations for Victorian children each year. This represents about 90 per cent of all vaccinations for school-aged kids and 45 per cent of those for younger children and babies.

    

    Councils are required to report this data to the Department of Health, either through Central Immunisation Records Victoria or privately operated databases.

    

    Following a barrage of complaints, the department twice backed down, delaying the rollout of a $2.32 fee per-jab fee, capped at $25,000 a year, after receiving a barrage of complaints.

    

    Several local councils asked how they could "hand back" responsibility to the state, complaining that they were already disproportionately bearing the cost of immunisations.

    

    The department maintained councils were welcome to move to private competitors to record their vaccinations. But internal correspondence shows the department dreaded this possibility and reduced the co-payment fee to stop councils leaving for commercial alternatives.

    

    "Have reduced pricing to councils to ensure CIRV is cheaper than its main competition," briefing documents state.

    

    Councils also warned the department they could be forced to scale down vaccination programs, cease other critical services, or charge vulnerable families.

    

    "I have no choice but to re-inform you that we will not be paying," Wellington Shire Council general manager of development Andrew Pomeroy told the department in August 2024.

    

    "Another one …," then-deputy secretary of eHealth Victoria Dr Lance Emerson wrote the same month, forwarding a complaint from Maroondah City Council.

    

    Amid the funding fight, the main competitor to the government system, VaxApp, proactively approached municipalities to pitch their product.

    

    Moira Shire asked the City of Melbourne why they already used VaxApp and if they would ever turn to the government's record system.

    

    "We would never consider moving back to CIRV (even if it was still free)," the City of Melbourne replied.

    

    Seventy councils and health services used the record system in 2024. This has since fallen to 40, with at least one more council, the City of Yarra, preparing to transition away from it.

    

    Councils explained they had invested significant resources in migrating to and developing staff proficiency in Central Immunisation Records Victoria in good faith, on the basis it would always be free.

    

    Despite the correspondence revealing that Warrnambool City Council had asked how to "hand back" immunisation services to the department, chief executive Andrew Mason said the council had "very reluctantly" agreed to pay the fee but remained "deeply disappointed" in the cost-shifting.

    

    "We came to the conclusion that charging users to cover the software cost was a poor public health outcome and could result in reduced immunisation rates," he said in a statement to The Age

    

    Stonnington Council also stayed with the platform.

    

    "Council is concerned that exiting CIRV would fragment public health data across private platforms and undermine data security," a spokesman said.

    

    "Council already subsidises the majority of its immunisation program, and the introduction of a fee to register vaccinations, which councils are required by law to do, blindsided the industry and represents further cost shifting to local government."

    

    A Department of Health spokeswoman said vaccination was one of the most effective ways to protect the health of Victorians.

    

    "The Victorian government funded the development and initial operation of CIRV to cover the council's costs. If councils do not wish to use this platform, it is not mandatory."

    

    From our partners

story_title: Briefs: Tasmanian School Violence, WA Sea Rescue, PALM Scheme
story_summary: A collection of miscellaneous news stories. In Tasmania, the Principals Association welcomed a government action plan to address rising school violence, while an extreme fire danger warning was issued for the east coast. In Western Australia, a 13-year-old boy swam four kilometres to save his family after they were swept out to sea. A new report has criticised the Australian Government's Pacific Australia Labour Mobility (PALM) scheme, highlighting issues of worker exploitation. Headline: Remember life before the NDIS, Butler says as Thriving Kids report released
Full Text:
The commonwealth will begin rolling out $1.4bn of funding for Thriving Kids to states within weeks, with hopes the program to redirect autistic

   children off the NDIS will see parents take a more active role in care and early intervention.

    

    The commonwealth will begin rolling out $1.4bn of funding for Thriving Kids to states within weeks, with hopes the program to redirect autistic children off the NDIS will see parents take a more active role in care and early intervention.

    

    As the federal government faces down its July 2026 deadline of reducing the growth of the $50bn-a-year NDIS to an annual 8 per cent, Health Minister Mark Butler on Tuesday released the hotly anticipated Thriving Kids Advisory Group report.

    

    Mr Butler confirmed jurisdictions would have the funding to pump into their education systems in whatever way they saw fit, and that Thriving Kids would be designed on a state-by-state level.

    

    However, Mr Butler left the door open to federal initiatives as part of the Thriving Kids program, such as the reintroduction of the three-year-old healthy kids check scrapped by Tony Abbott.

    

    Mr Butler said the program would seek to have autistic children catered for more so by their own families at home, rebuffing apprehension that the government was trying to reduce reliance on the NDIS by having parents take on extra responsibility in the care of children with developmental delay,

    

    "I just want to be clear that there was a life before the NDIS," he said in Canberra.

    

    "The NDIS has only been in place for a little over a decade, and before that time, all of the services that we're really talking about through the Thriving Kids program did exist at a state level … It's not rocket science."

    

    He confirmed the decision to hand states the bulk of the commonwealth's $2bn Thriving Kids' investment, rather than the federal government directly funding programs, was made after negotiation with state counterparts.

    

    "We won't be funding services directly. We'll be providing that money to states," he declared.

    

    "Overarching principles (in the Thriving Kids report) will now be the subject of negotiation with each state and territory government so that we can conclude bilateral agreements with them to see funds flow to them by the 20th of February. We're aiming to wrap up all of those bilateral schedules or bilateral agreements with every state and territory government before South Australia goes into caretaker later in February."

    

    The Thriving Kids advisory report, handed to government in December, focuses on six key principles behind the program including to identify children with developmental delay as early as possible and connect them with supports, matched to their level of need.

    

    In announcing the release of the report at a press conference on Tuesday morning, Mr Butler said Thriving Kids would help get the "NDIS back on track".

    

    Other principles include offering supports that are child and family centred, informed by evidence and focused on outcomes, and ensuring that supports are delivered in everyday settings.

    

    Mr Butler said the number of young children with these needs that had been on the NDIS was "alarming" and required action.

    

    "I also spoke of the need to address what I viewed as an alarming number of young children who had been enrolled in the NDIS, a number that had risen to as many as one in six young boys, junior primary age boys," Mr Butler said.

    

    "In some parts of the country, as far as one in four junior primary aged boys had been enrolled on to the NDIS, a scheme that was obviously designed for significant and permanent, usually lifelong, disability.

    

    "Most of those young children were enrolled for developmental delay or autism."

    

    The advisory group report stresses that access to Thriving Kids by this cohort of children "does not require a diagnosis".

    

    Instead the Advisory Group recommended "a functional assessment of support needs to match the child with the most appropriate supports. In addition to this support needs assessment, some identification or diagnostic tools may be used by relevant workers to help better understand needs of the child".

    

    This followed the advisory group finding the market for supporting children was limited, and that there was not an existing workforce for new roles or a pool of specialised support worker positions to be pursued as part of delivering Thriving Kids.

    

    The full Thriving Kids program will be implemented by January 2028, at which point NDIS eligibility will change to ensure children with mild to moderate autism stop accessing the scheme going forwards.

story_title: Government Unveils 'Thriving Kids' NDIS Alternative Program
story_summary: The Albanese government has released the model for its $4 billion 'Thriving Kids' program, a major reform designed to shift children aged eight and under with developmental delays or mild autism from the National Disability Insurance Scheme (NDIS) to state-led support systems. Championed by Health Minister Mark Butler, the program aims to provide community-based support and easier access to early intervention without requiring a formal medical diagnosis, in an effort to make the NDIS more sustainable. While the move has been welcomed by some stakeholders, disability groups, parent advocates, and the Australian Education Union have called for more operational details and raised concerns about implementation, parental control, and potential increased workloads for teachers. The program is set for a phased rollout starting in late 2024, with full implementation by 2028.

Headline: Bendigo, Burnie based Pier Support Services Pty Ltd into liquidation for $1.5m
Full Text:
A Victorian NDIS provider "committed to providing dignity" to the disabled racked up a staggering $1.5m in debt owed to dozens of staff and the ATO.

    A Victorian NDIS provider "committed to providing dignity" to the disabled racked up a staggering $1.5m in debt in just four years, leaving dozens of workers out of pocket.

    

    Pier Support Services Pty Ltd, which was based in Bendigo and Burnie, Tasmania, had just $1400 left in the bank by the time it entered liquidation on October 3, 2025.

    

    Insolvency expert Adam Johnston, of Apex Advisory Australia, was appointed liquidator to wind up the business.

    

    He alleged that Pier Support Services owes its 34 employees – 27 of whom were on casual contracts – more than $370,000 in wages, superannuation, and redundancy payments in a report lodged with the Australian Securities and Investments Commission (ASIC).

    

    The company's financial health had deteriorated so badly that it could only pay its workers "incrementally and as funds became available, rather than in full for each pay period" in its last three months before going under, Mr Johnston said.

    

    The ATO is owed nearly $610,000, which, combined with a number of "many small debts", takes the money owed to unsecured creditors up to $1m.

    

    The company's sole director is 31-year-old Hayden Cooper.

    

    Mr Cooper's LinkedIn page indicated he was based in Bendigo between 2020–2021 as a personal care assistant with HCA Healthcare Australia, a support worker for Ermha Ltd, and mental health practitioner for Mind Australia Limited.

    

    The liquidator accused Mr Cooper of insolvent trading, which occurs when a business takes on new debts while already having overdue payments.

    

    "It is evident from investigations that the director allowed the company to trade while insolvent for a considerable period of time, at least as early as May 2024," Mr Johnston said.

    

    "I intend to make a demand on the director for insolvent trading."

    

    Pier Support Services posted losses of nearly $600,000 in the 18 months from July 2024 to December 2025.

    

    Mr Cooper attributed the company's failures to "industry pressures within the NDIS industry, delayed cash receipts, operational resourcing challenges, and personal circumstances impacting management capacity", according to Mr Johnston's report.

    

    However, Mr Johnston said the business bit off more than it could chew by borrowing money to open an office at 223 Barnard St Bendigo and purchase property in Tasmania to "accommodate participants".

    

    "This timing and funding of the expansion, in the absence of commensurate governance and financial oversight, added further pressure to the company's cashflow and its ability to meet its liabilities," he said.

    

    The NDIS has been the subject of media reporting covering the systemic rorting of the program.

    

    In November last year, former NDIS planner James Davies pleaded guilty to engaging in conduct with the intention of dishonestly causing a loss after he was busted siphoning $700,000 in fake disability claims between April and May 2020.

    

    The Herald Sun is not inferring that Mr Cooper defrauded the NDIS in any way.

    

    Mr Cooper was contacted for comment.

    

    If you have a tip related to this story, please reach out to edward.russell@news.com.au.
